background_cases: A hammer is used to drive nails; it is a tool.
A saw is used to cut wood; it is a tool.
A screwdriver is used to turn screws; it is a tool.
definition: An object is a tool if and only if (i) it is a physical artefact, (ii) it is designed for a specific practical purpose, and (iii) it extends the capabilities of the human hand.
conditions: physical artefact
designed for specific purpose
extends hand capabilities
counterexample: A stone picked up from the ground and used to crack a nut is not a designed artefact, yet it functions as a tool. Conversely, a computer program is designed for a specific practical purpose and extends human capabilities, but it is not a physical artefact.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all manufactured implements. The definition required both artefactuality and physicality, but tool-use predates manufacture (unmodified stones are tools when used as such) and extends beyond the physical (software is a tool in the relevant functional sense).
passage:
Consider three familiar tools. A hammer is used to drive nails; a saw is used to cut wood; a screwdriver is used to turn screws. Each is a physical artefact, each is designed for a specific practical purpose, and each extends the capabilities of the human hand. From these cases one might define a tool: an object is a tool if and only if it is a physical artefact, it is designed for a specific practical purpose, and it extends the capabilities of the human hand.

But this definition proves both too narrow and, in a different respect, too restrictive. Consider a stone picked up from a riverbed and used, without any modification, to crack a walnut. The stone is not an artefact — it was not designed or manufactured — yet it functions as a tool. The first condition fails, yet we should say the stone is being used as a tool. Now consider a computer program designed to perform complex calculations. It is designed for a specific practical purpose and extends human capabilities far beyond what the hand can achieve. But it is not a physical object. The background cases were all manufactured physical implements; the definition captured what those particular tools had in common but failed to accommodate both the primitive tool — the unmodified natural object pressed into service — and the modern tool that exists only as information.

background_cases: English is a language: it has a vocabulary, a grammar, and is spoken by a community of speakers.
French is a language: it has its own vocabulary, grammar, and community of speakers.
Mandarin is a language: it has a distinct writing system, grammar, and the largest community of native speakers.
definition: A system of communication is a language if and only if (i) it has a vocabulary of distinct symbols or words, (ii) it has a grammar governing how symbols are combined, and (iii) it is used by a community of speakers for communication.
conditions: vocabulary of symbols
grammar governing combination
used by community for communication
counterexample: The formal language of propositional logic has a vocabulary of symbols and a grammar governing their combination, but it is not used by any community as a medium of ordinary communication. The first two conditions are met, the third is debatable, yet we call it a language.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all natural languages used for everyday communication. The definition captured the social function of these languages but excluded formal and artificial languages that satisfy the structural conditions without serving the communicative function of natural language.
passage:
Consider three cases of language. English has a vocabulary, a grammar, and is spoken by a community of speakers. French has its own vocabulary, grammar, and community. Mandarin has a distinct writing system, grammar, and the largest community of native speakers in the world. From these cases one might define language: a system of communication is a language if and only if it has a vocabulary of distinct symbols or words, a grammar governing how symbols are combined, and it is used by a community of speakers for communication.

But consider the language of propositional logic. It has a vocabulary: the symbols p, q, r, together with connectives and brackets. It has a grammar: precise rules specify which strings of symbols are well-formed formulae. Yet it is not used by any community as a medium of ordinary communication; it is a formal system employed within the discipline of logic for the analysis of arguments. The first two conditions are met, but the third is at best doubtful, for logicians do not converse in propositional logic. Yet we do not hesitate to call it a language — a formal language. The background cases were all natural languages serving everyday communicative purposes; the definition was drawn from these and failed to accommodate formal and artificial languages whose purpose is structural rather than communicative.

background_cases: A gold sovereign is accepted in exchange for goods, retains its value over time, and is minted by a government.
A banknote issued by the Bank of England is accepted in shops, maintains a reasonably stable value, and is printed under governmental authority.
A silver dollar circulates widely in commerce, holds its value against inflation, and is coined by the United States Mint.
definition: An object is money if and only if (i) it is made of or backed by a precious material, (ii) it is produced by a governmental authority, and (iii) it is widely accepted in exchange for goods and services.
conditions: made of or backed by precious material
produced by governmental authority
widely accepted in exchange
counterexample: The large stone discs (rai) used as currency on the island of Yap are made of limestone, not a precious material, and are not produced by any governmental authority — they are quarried and transported by individual families. Yet they are widely accepted in exchange for goods and services and function as money within the community. The first two conditions fail.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved metallic currencies issued by modern nation-states. The definition treated precious material and governmental issuance as essential, but these were incidental features of the particular monetary systems observed, not universal requirements of money as such.
passage:
Consider three cases of money. A gold sovereign is accepted in exchange for goods, retains its value over time, and is minted by a government. A banknote issued by the Bank of England circulates in commerce, maintains a reasonably stable value, and is printed under governmental authority. A silver dollar circulates widely, holds its value, and is coined by the United States Mint. From these cases one might extract a definition: an object is money if and only if it is made of or backed by a precious material, it is produced by a governmental authority, and it is widely accepted in exchange for goods and services.

But this definition, drawn from the monetary systems of modern industrial states, proves too restrictive. Consider the large stone discs, known as rai, used as currency on the island of Yap in the western Pacific. These discs, some of considerable size, are made of ordinary limestone quarried from a distant island and transported by canoe. Limestone is not a precious material by any standard. The discs are not produced by any governmental authority; they are quarried and fashioned by individual families. Yet they are widely accepted in exchange for goods and services within the Yapese community, and they function as money in every practical respect: debts are denominated in rai, transactions are conducted with reference to them, and their value is socially established and maintained. The first two conditions fail. The background cases were all instances of metallic currency issued by modern states; the definition mistook the incidental features of these particular systems — precious metal, governmental authority — for essential features of money as such. Money, it appears, is a social convention, not a material or governmental fact.

background_cases: Bread is consumed for nourishment; it provides carbohydrates, is readily digestible, and is a staple of the human diet.
An apple is eaten for its nutritional content; it provides vitamins, fibre, and sugars that sustain the body.
A piece of roast beef is consumed for its protein and caloric content; it nourishes and satisfies hunger.
definition: A substance is food if and only if (i) it is of organic origin, (ii) it provides caloric or nutritional value when ingested, and (iii) it is consumed primarily for the purpose of nourishment.
conditions: of organic origin
provides caloric or nutritional value
consumed primarily for nourishment
counterexample: Salt is consumed regularly as part of human meals and is essential for bodily function, yet it is of mineral origin, not organic, and provides no calories. The first and, partially, the second conditions fail, yet salt has been regarded as a food — or at the very least as an indispensable component of food — throughout human history.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all calorie-rich organic staples. The definition assumed that all food is organic and caloric, but essential dietary components such as salt and water are inorganic and non-caloric, yet integral to what it means to eat and be nourished.
passage:
Consider three staples of the human diet. Bread is consumed for nourishment; it provides carbohydrates and is a dietary staple across cultures. An apple provides vitamins, fibre, and sugars that sustain the body. Roast beef is consumed for its protein and caloric content. From these cases one might extract a definition: a substance is food if and only if it is of organic origin, it provides caloric or nutritional value when ingested, and it is consumed primarily for the purpose of nourishment.

But this definition, drawn from calorie-rich organic substances, proves too narrow. Consider salt. Human beings have consumed salt throughout recorded history; wars have been fought over it, economies built upon it, and entire trade routes established for its transport. Salt is essential for bodily function: without sufficient sodium, the human body cannot maintain fluid balance, transmit nerve impulses, or contract muscles. Yet salt is not of organic origin; it is a mineral. And salt provides no calories whatever. The first condition fails, and the second is at best only partially met (salt provides nutritional value in the sense of an essential mineral, but not caloric value). Yet salt is indisputably part of the human diet and has been treated as a food, or at minimum as an indispensable component of food, since antiquity. The background cases were all calorie-rich organic staples; the definition abstracted organic origin and caloric value as essential, when in truth the concept of food extends to inorganic, non-caloric substances that are nonetheless vital to nourishment.

background_cases: Two schoolboys who play together daily, confide in one another, and defend each other against bullies are friends.
Two women who have shared a flat for years, celebrated each other's successes, and supported each other through hardships are friends.
Two colleagues who meet regularly outside work, enjoy each other's company, and trust one another with personal matters are friends.
definition: Two persons are friends if and only if (i) they spend time together regularly, (ii) they enjoy one another's company, and (iii) they know one another's personal histories and characters in some detail.
conditions: spend time together regularly
enjoy one another's company
know one another's personal histories and characters
counterexample: Two men who corresponded by letter for decades, having met only once in youth, may enjoy each other's company only through the written word and may not spend time together in any ordinary sense. Yet they know one another's characters intimately from the correspondence and regard one another as the closest of friends. The first condition fails.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved friends in physical proximity who met regularly. The definition treated regular face-to-face contact as essential, but friendship may be sustained entirely through correspondence or other forms of communication at a distance.
passage:
Consider three cases of friendship. Two schoolboys who play together daily, confide in one another, and defend each other against bullies are friends. Two women who have shared a flat for years, celebrated each other's successes, and supported each other through adversity are friends. Two colleagues who meet regularly outside the office, enjoy each other's company, and trust one another with personal matters are friends. From these cases one might define friendship: two persons are friends if and only if they spend time together regularly, they enjoy one another's company, and they know one another's personal histories and characters in some detail.

But this definition, drawn from friendships sustained by physical proximity, proves too narrow. Consider two men who met once as young scholars at a conference and thereafter maintained a correspondence spanning several decades. They exchange long, thoughtful letters about their work, their families, their disappointments, and their hopes. Each knows the other's character with an intimacy that few face-to-face acquaintances achieve. Each regards the other as his closest friend. Yet they do not spend time together in any ordinary sense: they have not been in the same room since that first meeting. The first condition fails. The background cases all involved friends who met regularly in person; the definition treated this regular physical contact as essential to friendship. But friendship, it appears, may be sustained and even deepened through correspondence alone, without any physical co-presence whatever. The definition has confused a common circumstance of friendship with a necessary condition of it.

background_cases: A schoolmaster canes a boy for disrupting the class; the pain is immediate and directly administered.
A court sentences a thief to six months' imprisonment; the deprivation of liberty is imposed by formal decree.
A parent confines a child to his room for breaking a household rule; the restriction is applied at once.
definition: An action constitutes punishment if and only if (i) it inflicts a deprivation or suffering upon the offender, (ii) it is imposed in direct response to a specific transgression, and (iii) it is administered by a single identifiable punisher.
conditions: inflicts deprivation or suffering
imposed in direct response to specific transgression
administered by single identifiable punisher
counterexample: A merchant in a small town cheats his customers, and the entire community gradually ceases to do business with him. The resulting isolation and loss of livelihood inflict genuine suffering. The response is to his specific transgression. But no single identifiable person administers the punishment; it arises from the diffuse, uncoordinated decisions of many individuals. The third condition fails, yet the merchant is punished.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved a single agent (schoolmaster, court, parent) administering punishment directly. The definition treated this centralised agency as essential, but punishment may emerge from the collective and uncoordinated responses of a community without any single punisher.
passage:
Consider three cases of punishment. A schoolmaster canes a boy for disrupting the class; the pain is immediate and directly administered. A court sentences a thief to six months' imprisonment; the deprivation of liberty is imposed by formal decree. A parent confines a child to his room for breaking a household rule. From these cases one might extract a definition: an action constitutes punishment if and only if it inflicts a deprivation or suffering upon the offender, it is imposed in direct response to a specific transgression, and it is administered by a single identifiable punisher.

But consider a merchant in a small market town who has been discovered cheating his customers — giving short weight, substituting inferior goods, and the like. No authority takes formal action against him. No single person decides to punish him. Yet gradually, one by one, his customers cease to patronise his shop. His neighbours grow cold. He is excluded from the social gatherings he once enjoyed. His livelihood dwindles and his standing in the community is destroyed. The resulting isolation and impoverishment inflict genuine suffering, and the community's response is plainly directed at his specific transgression. But there is no single identifiable punisher: the punishment arises from the diffuse, uncoordinated decisions of dozens of individuals, each acting independently. The third condition fails. The background cases all involved a single, identifiable agent administering punishment; the definition mistook this centralised agency for an essential feature. But punishment may emerge from the collective responses of a community, without any one person wielding the punitive authority.

background_cases: A family lives in a detached house that they own; the house is their home.
A young man rents a flat in the city where he has lived for several years; the flat is his home.
An elderly couple have occupied the same cottage since their marriage; the cottage is their home.
definition: A place is a person's home if and only if (i) it is a fixed physical structure, (ii) the person sleeps there regularly, and (iii) the person has chosen to live there.
conditions: fixed physical structure
person sleeps there regularly
person has chosen to live there
counterexample: A nomadic herdsman of the Central Asian steppe moves his felt tent from pasture to pasture with the seasons. He sleeps in the tent every night, and he has chosen this way of life. But the tent is not a fixed physical structure; it is dismantled and reassembled at each new site. The first condition fails, yet the tent is plainly his home — it is where his family lives, where he keeps his possessions, and where he returns at the end of each day.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved sedentary dwellings permanently fixed to one location. The definition treated fixity of structure as essential, but for nomadic peoples the home is a portable dwelling that moves with them, and its status as home depends upon habitual use and personal attachment rather than upon permanence of location.
passage:
Consider three cases of home. A family lives in a detached house that they own; the house is their home. A young man rents a flat in the city where he has lived for several years; the flat is his home. An elderly couple have occupied the same cottage since their marriage; the cottage is their home. From these cases one might define home: a place is a person's home if and only if it is a fixed physical structure, the person sleeps there regularly, and the person has chosen to live there.

But this definition, drawn from the sedentary dwellings of settled societies, proves too restrictive. Consider a nomadic herdsman of the Central Asian steppe. He and his family live in a felt tent — a ger — which they dismantle at the end of each season and transport by pack animal to the next pasture. The herdsman sleeps in the ger every night. He has chosen this way of life freely, following the tradition of his ancestors. The ger is where his family gathers, where his children sleep, where he stores his possessions, and where he returns each evening from the pastures. In every respect that matters, the ger is his home. Yet it is not a fixed physical structure; it is portable, and its location changes several times a year. The first condition fails. The background cases all involved permanent, immovable dwellings; the definition mistook the fixity of these particular structures for an essential feature of home. But for nomadic peoples, the home is the dwelling itself — wherever it happens to stand — and its status as home depends upon habitual use and personal attachment, not upon permanence of location.

background_cases: Two children play draughts after school, following the familiar rules, each trying to capture all the other's pieces.
A cricket match between two village teams proceeds according to the laws of cricket, with each side trying to outscore the other over the course of an afternoon.
Two friends play a game of tennis on a public court, serving, returning, and keeping score according to the standard rules.
definition: An activity A is a game if and only if (i) A involves two opposing sides, (ii) A is governed by rules that determine which moves are permissible, and (iii) A concludes with a score or tally by which one side is declared the winner.
conditions: A involves two opposing sides
A is governed by rules that determine which moves are permissible
A concludes with a score or tally by which one side is declared the winner
counterexample: A child plays a game of patience alone at a kitchen table, dealing out cards according to fixed rules and attempting to arrange the entire deck into ordered sequences. The activity is governed by precise rules — rules as strict as those of draughts — and it concludes with a determinate outcome: either the arrangement is completed or it is not. Yet there are no two opposing sides; the child plays alone. And the game does not conclude with a score or tally by which one side is declared the winner; the child either succeeds or fails against the structure of the game itself. The first and third conditions fail, yet patience is manifestly a game.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all contests between two opposing parties — draughts between two children, cricket between two village teams, tennis between two friends. The definition abstracted the features common to these cases: opposition, rules, and a decisive score. But the cases were drawn exclusively from competitive, two-sided contests, and the definition mistook the features of competitive games for the features of games as such. Patience reveals that a game need not involve opposition or a winner; it may be a solitary activity in which the player contends against the game's own structure. The concept of a game is broader than any definition drawn from competitive cases alone.
passage:
Consider three activities that are unquestionably games. Two children play draughts after school, each attempting to capture all the other's pieces. Two village cricket teams compete over the course of an afternoon, each trying to outscore the other. Two friends play tennis on a public court, serving and returning according to the standard rules. From these cases one might extract a definition: an activity is a game if and only if it involves two opposing sides, it is governed by rules determining which moves are permissible, and it concludes with a score or tally by which one side is declared the winner. The three cases satisfy all three conditions, and the definition appears to identify the essential features of a game.

But one must consider patience. A child sits alone at a kitchen table and deals out a pack of cards according to well-known rules. The child turns cards, moves them between columns, and attempts to arrange the entire deck into four ascending sequences by suit. The rules are precise and binding — as strict as those governing any move in draughts. And the activity concludes with a determinate outcome: the child either succeeds in completing the arrangement or fails. Yet there are no two opposing sides. The child plays alone, against the structure of the game itself — against the chance arrangement of the cards and the constraints of the rules. There is no score and no winner; there is only success or failure. The background cases were all competitive, two-sided contests, and the definition abstracted opposition, rules, and scoring as jointly essential to the concept of a game. Patience reveals that a game may be solitary, non-competitive, and without a tally of any kind, and that what makes it a game is not the presence of an opponent but the presence of a rule-governed structure within which the player acts purposefully towards a goal.

background_cases: A wooden kitchen chair with four straight legs, a flat seat, and a vertical back-rest is used daily by a family at mealtimes.
An upholstered armchair with four legs, a cushioned seat, and a high padded back stands in a sitting room and is favoured for reading.
A folding metal chair with four legs, a flat seat, and a low back-rest is set out in rows for a village meeting.
definition: An object x is a chair if and only if (i) x has four legs, (ii) x has a distinct back-rest rising above the seat, and (iii) x is intended for a single seated occupant.
conditions: x has four legs
x has a distinct back-rest rising above the seat
x is intended for a single seated occupant
counterexample: A hammock — a length of canvas or netting suspended between two trees by cords at either end — is used as a seat by a single occupant. The occupant sits in the hammock, and indeed many persons in tropical climates use hammocks as their principal seating furniture, preferring them to conventional chairs for reasons of comfort and ventilation. Yet a hammock has no legs whatsoever; it is suspended, not supported from below. And it has no distinct back-rest; the fabric curves around the occupant without any differentiated structure. The first and second conditions fail, yet the hammock functions as a chair and is treated as one in the cultures where it is customary.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all rigid, four-legged, back-rested objects — a kitchen chair, an armchair, a folding chair. The definition abstracted legs, a back-rest, and single occupancy as essential features. But the cases were drawn exclusively from the tradition of European furniture, and the definition mistook the structural features of that tradition for necessary conditions of the concept. The hammock reveals that a chair — or at any rate an object that performs the function of a chair — may be flexible, legless, and without a differentiated back-rest, and that what is essential to the concept is the capacity to support a person in a seated or semi-reclined posture, not the possession of any particular structural features.
passage:
Consider three objects that are paradigmatic chairs. A wooden kitchen chair with four straight legs, a flat seat, and a vertical back-rest. An upholstered armchair with four legs, a cushioned seat, and a high padded back. A folding metal chair with four legs, a flat seat, and a low back-rest. From these cases one might extract a definition: an object is a chair if and only if it has four legs, it has a distinct back-rest rising above the seat, and it is intended for a single seated occupant. The three cases satisfy all three conditions, and the definition appears to capture the essential structure of a chair.

But one must consider the hammock. In many tropical and subtropical regions, the hammock serves as the principal item of seating furniture. A length of woven cotton or netting is suspended between two fixed points — trees, posts, hooks set into a wall — and a single person sits or reclines in the resulting curve of fabric. The hammock supports the occupant comfortably; it is used for sitting, for resting, for conversation, for all the purposes that a chair serves in other cultures. Yet it has no legs. It is not supported from below but suspended from above; its entire weight, and the weight of its occupant, is borne by the cords at either end. And it has no distinct back-rest; the fabric curves continuously around the occupant, providing support on all sides without any differentiated vertical structure. The first and second conditions of the definition fail entirely. The background cases were drawn from the European tradition of rigid, four-legged, back-rested furniture, and the definition abstracted the structural features of that tradition as necessary conditions of the concept. The hammock reveals that the concept of a chair — or of that which performs the function of a chair — is not bound to any particular structural form, and that what matters is the capacity to support a single person in a position of seated or semi-reclined repose.

background_cases: A large oil painting by Titian, displayed in a gilded frame in the Uffizi Gallery, is universally acknowledged as a work of art.
A bronze sculpture by Rodin, standing upon a marble pedestal in a public museum, is regarded without question as a work of art.
A fresco by Giotto, covering the wall of a chapel in Padua and preserved for centuries, is celebrated as a masterpiece of the painter's art.
definition: An object x is a work of art if and only if (i) x is a physical object of substantial size, (ii) x is displayed in a gallery, museum, or other institution devoted to the exhibition of art, and (iii) x is intended by its creator to endure indefinitely.
conditions: x is a physical object of substantial size
x is displayed in a gallery, museum, or other institution devoted to the exhibition of art
x is intended by its creator to endure indefinitely
counterexample: A brief lyric poem — fourteen lines, say, composed in an afternoon and written upon a single sheet of paper — is a work of art. It may never be displayed in a gallery or museum; it may be published in a periodical, read aloud in a drawing room, or passed from hand to hand in manuscript. It is not an object of substantial size; it occupies a fraction of a page. And while the poet may hope that the poem will be remembered, the physical sheet upon which it is written is not intended to endure indefinitely. Yet the poem is a work of art — as much so as any painting or sculpture.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all large, durable, physical objects displayed in institutional settings — a painting in a gallery, a sculpture in a museum, a fresco on a chapel wall. The definition abstracted size, institutional display, and intended permanence as essential to art. But these features characterised the particular mode of presentation of visual art in the European tradition, and the definition mistook the conditions of display for conditions of art itself. The lyric poem reveals that a work of art may be small, immaterial in the relevant sense, independent of any institution, and indifferent to physical permanence, and that the concept of art extends well beyond the gallery and the museum.
passage:
Consider three objects universally acknowledged as works of art. A large oil painting by Titian, displayed in a gilded frame in the Uffizi. A bronze sculpture by Rodin upon a marble pedestal in a public museum. A fresco by Giotto covering the wall of a chapel in Padua. From these cases one might extract a definition: an object is a work of art if and only if it is a physical object of substantial size, it is displayed in a gallery, museum, or other institution devoted to the exhibition of art, and it is intended by its creator to endure indefinitely. The three cases satisfy all three conditions: each is large, each is institutionally displayed, and each was created with a view to permanence.

But one must consider the lyric poem. A poet composes a sonnet — fourteen lines of verse, written in an afternoon upon a single sheet of paper. The poem is published in a periodical, read aloud at a gathering, copied into a commonplace book. It is not a physical object of substantial size; it occupies a fraction of a page, and its existence as a work of art does not depend upon any particular physical embodiment — it may be memorised, recited, reprinted, and transmitted entirely without the original manuscript. It is not displayed in a gallery or museum; poems are not hung upon walls or placed upon pedestals. And while the poet may hope that the poem will survive, the sheet of paper upon which it is first written is not intended to endure indefinitely; the poem's persistence depends upon being read, remembered, and reproduced, not upon the durability of any physical substrate. All three conditions fail. The background cases were drawn from the visual arts as displayed in institutional settings, and the definition mistook the features of that setting — physical scale, institutional display, material permanence — for the features of art as such. The poem reveals that a work of art may be small, immaterial, non-institutional, and indifferent to the durability of any physical object.

background_cases: English, spoken by hundreds of millions of persons across many countries, possesses a vast vocabulary, a complex grammar, and an extensive written literature.
Mandarin Chinese, spoken by over a thousand million persons, has a tonal phonology, a logographic writing system, and a literary tradition stretching back millennia.
Classical Arabic, the language of the Quran, has a highly inflected grammar, a rich morphological system, and a body of poetry and prose spanning fourteen centuries.
definition: A system S is a language if and only if (i) S is spoken by a large community numbering at least several millions, (ii) S possesses a written form, and (iii) S has a substantial body of literature — poetry, prose, or sacred texts.
conditions: S is spoken by a large community numbering at least several millions
S possesses a written form
S has a substantial body of literature — poetry, prose, or sacred texts
counterexample: Consider a language spoken by a small community of a few hundred persons in a remote valley — a tongue passed from parents to children for generations, possessing a grammar, a phonology, and a vocabulary adequate to all the communicative needs of the community. This tongue has never been written down; it exists solely in speech. It has no literature in the conventional sense — no poetry committed to paper, no prose narratives preserved in books, no sacred texts. Yet it is a language: its speakers use it to converse, to argue, to tell stories, to sing, to conduct the affairs of daily life. The first, second, and third conditions all fail, yet the system is indisputably a language.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all major world languages — English, Mandarin, Arabic — characterised by vast speaker populations, established writing systems, and extensive literary traditions. The definition abstracted these features as essential to the concept of a language. But the cases were drawn from the most prominent and well-documented languages, and the definition mistook the features of culturally dominant tongues for necessary conditions of language as such. The small, unwritten language of a remote community reveals that a language need not be spoken by millions, need not possess a written form, and need not have a literary tradition. What makes a system a language is its grammatical structure and its capacity to serve the full range of human communicative needs, not its size, its documentation, or its cultural prestige.
passage:
Consider three systems that are unquestionably languages. English, spoken by hundreds of millions, possessing a vast vocabulary, a complex grammar, and an immense written literature. Mandarin Chinese, spoken by over a thousand million persons, with a tonal phonology, a logographic script, and a literary tradition of several millennia. Classical Arabic, with its highly inflected grammar and a body of poetry and prose spanning fourteen centuries. From these cases one might extract a definition: a system is a language if and only if it is spoken by a large community numbering at least several millions, it possesses a written form, and it has a substantial body of literature. The three cases satisfy all three conditions, and the definition appears to identify what is essential to a language.

But one must consider the small, unwritten tongue of a remote community. In a valley cut off from the wider world, a few hundred persons speak a language that has been passed from parents to children for as long as any member of the community can remember. The language has a grammar — rules governing the combination of sounds into words and words into sentences. It has a phonology — a system of meaningful distinctions between sounds. It has a vocabulary adequate to all the communicative needs of the community: terms for plants and animals, for kinship relations, for weather, for the features of the landscape, for the emotional and social life of its speakers. But it has never been written down. No scholar has devised an orthography for it, and no member of the community writes in it. It has no literature in the conventional sense — no poems on paper, no prose narratives in books. And it is spoken by a few hundred persons, not several millions. All three conditions of the definition fail. Yet the system is a language in every meaningful sense. The definition was drawn from the most prominent and culturally dominant tongues, and it mistook the features of cultural prominence — large populations, writing, literature — for necessary features of language itself.

background_cases: A child who has broken a vase tells a parent, 'I did not touch it,' intending the parent to accept this falsehood and thereby avoid punishment.
A merchant, wishing to sell a defective horse, tells a buyer, 'The animal is perfectly sound,' intending the buyer to accept this claim and complete the purchase.
A student who has not completed an assignment tells a teacher, 'I left it at home,' intending the teacher to accept this excuse and grant an extension.
definition: An utterance U is a lie if and only if (i) U is a spoken assertion of a factual proposition, (ii) the speaker holds the proposition to be false, and (iii) the speaker produces U in order to obtain a personal benefit — to avoid punishment, to gain an advantage, or to secure some practical good.
conditions: U is a spoken assertion of a factual proposition
the speaker holds the proposition to be false
the speaker produces U in order to obtain a personal benefit
counterexample: A doctor, attending a patient who is gravely and incurably ill, tells the patient, 'Your condition is improving; I expect a full recovery.' The doctor holds this to be false — the doctor knows that the patient is dying. The utterance is a spoken assertion of a factual proposition, and the doctor holds the proposition to be false. But the doctor does not produce the utterance in order to obtain a personal benefit. The doctor lies to spare the patient anguish, to preserve the patient's hope, to ease the remaining days of a dying person. The motive is compassion, not self-interest. The third condition fails, yet the doctor has told a lie — a compassionate lie, perhaps a defensible one, but a lie nonetheless.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all instances of self-interested deception — a child avoiding punishment, a merchant gaining a sale, a student securing an extension. The definition abstracted personal benefit as essential to lying because every case in the sample was motivated by self-interest. But the cases were drawn from a narrow range of situations in which the liar stood to gain, and the definition mistook this contingent feature for a necessary condition. The doctor's compassionate lie reveals that a person may lie for the benefit of another, with no personal gain in view, and that the essence of lying is the deliberate assertion of what one holds to be false, regardless of the motive that prompts it.
passage:
Consider three cases of lying. A child who has broken a vase tells a parent, 'I did not touch it,' to avoid punishment. A merchant, wishing to sell a defective horse, tells a buyer, 'The animal is perfectly sound,' to secure the sale. A student who has not done an assignment tells a teacher, 'I left it at home,' to gain an extension. From these cases one might extract a definition: an utterance is a lie if and only if it is a spoken assertion of a factual proposition, the speaker holds the proposition to be false, and the speaker produces the utterance in order to obtain a personal benefit. The three cases satisfy all three conditions: in each, the speaker asserts a falsehood for the speaker's own advantage.

But one must consider the doctor. A physician attends a patient who is gravely and incurably ill. The patient asks, 'Am I getting better?' The doctor, knowing that the patient is dying, replies, 'Your condition is improving; I expect a full recovery.' The doctor holds this to be false — the medical evidence admits of no ambiguity. The utterance is a spoken assertion of a factual proposition, and the doctor holds the proposition to be false. But the doctor does not lie for personal benefit. The doctor lies to spare the patient suffering, to preserve what remains of the patient's hope, to ease the burden of a terminal illness. The motive is compassion, not self-interest; the doctor gains nothing and indeed risks professional censure. The third condition fails. The background cases were all self-interested lies, and the definition abstracted self-interest as a necessary condition of lying. The doctor's case reveals that a lie may be told from the most generous of motives, and that what makes an utterance a lie is not the motive behind it but the deliberate assertion of what the speaker holds to be false.

background_cases: A man promises his neighbour to return a borrowed ladder by Saturday, and both parties understand that a commitment has been made.
A woman promises her employer to complete a report by the end of the week, and the employer relies upon this commitment in planning the week's work.
A friend promises another friend to attend a birthday celebration, and the host counts upon this commitment when arranging the number of places at the table.
definition: An utterance U constitutes a promise if and only if (i) U concerns a future action that the speaker is able to perform, (ii) the hearer relies upon U in making plans or decisions, and (iii) the promised action is modest in scope and readily achievable.
conditions: U concerns a future action that the speaker is able to perform
the hearer relies upon U in making plans or decisions
the promised action is modest in scope and readily achievable
counterexample: A young man promises his dying mother that he will devote his life to the care of his younger siblings — that he will provide for them, educate them, and see them safely into adulthood. The promise concerns a future course of action, and the mother relies upon it; it is, indeed, her chief comfort in her final days. But the promised action is not modest in scope or readily achievable. It is an undertaking of the most demanding and far-reaching kind, requiring years of effort, sacrifice, and sustained commitment. The third condition fails, yet the promise is as genuine and as binding as any promise can be.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all modest, everyday promises — returning a ladder, completing a report, attending a party. The definition abstracted modesty of scope and ready achievability as essential features of promising, because the background cases all involved simple, limited undertakings. But the cases were drawn from the routine transactions of daily life, and the definition mistook the modesty of these transactions for a necessary condition of the concept. The deathbed promise reveals that a promise may be vast in scope, demanding in execution, and far from readily achievable, and that the binding force of a promise does not depend upon the ease with which it can be kept.
passage:
Consider three cases of promising. A man promises his neighbour to return a borrowed ladder by Saturday. A woman promises her employer to complete a report by the end of the week. A friend promises to attend a birthday celebration. From these cases one might extract a definition: an utterance constitutes a promise if and only if it concerns a future action the speaker is able to perform, the hearer relies upon it in making plans, and the promised action is modest in scope and readily achievable. The three cases satisfy all three conditions: each involves a simple, limited undertaking that can be accomplished without great difficulty.

But one must consider the deathbed promise. A young man sits at the bedside of his dying mother. She asks him to promise that he will devote himself to the care of his younger siblings — that he will provide for them, see that they are educated, and watch over them until they are grown. The young man promises. The promise concerns a future action, and the mother relies upon it; it is her chief consolation in her last hours. But the promised action is not modest in scope. It is an undertaking of immense magnitude — years of labour, sacrifice, and sustained responsibility, with no certainty of success. It is not readily achievable; it will demand everything the young man has to give, and perhaps more. The third condition of the definition fails entirely. Yet the promise is genuine, binding, and as weighty as any promise can be. The background cases were drawn from the routine, manageable transactions of daily life, and the definition mistook the modest scope of these transactions for an essential feature of promising itself. The deathbed promise reveals that the concept of a promise encompasses undertakings of the greatest difficulty and the most far-reaching consequence, and that the binding force of a promise is independent of the ease with which it may be fulfilled.

background_cases: A horse-drawn carriage conveys passengers along a road from one town to another, following a regular route and schedule.
A steam locomotive pulls a train of carriages along iron rails, transporting passengers and goods between cities.
A sailing ship carries passengers and cargo across the sea from one port to another, following established maritime routes.
definition: A conveyance C is a vehicle if and only if (i) C follows a fixed route or course, (ii) C is designed to carry multiple passengers or a substantial quantity of goods, and (iii) C is operated according to a schedule or timetable.
conditions: C follows a fixed route or course
C is designed to carry multiple passengers or a substantial quantity of goods
C is operated according to a schedule or timetable
counterexample: Consider a child's bicycle. A child rides a bicycle to school, choosing a different route each day according to whim — through the park on Monday, along the canal on Tuesday, down the high street on Wednesday. The bicycle carries only the child and perhaps a satchel. It is not operated according to any schedule; the child rides when the child pleases. Yet the bicycle is a vehicle — a means of personal conveyance, as unambiguously a vehicle as any carriage or locomotive. All three conditions fail.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all public conveyances operating on fixed routes and schedules — a carriage service, a railway, a shipping line. The definition abstracted fixed routes, passenger or cargo capacity, and scheduled operation as essential features of vehicles. But these were features of commercial and public transport, not of vehicles as such. The child's bicycle reveals that a vehicle may follow no fixed route, may carry only a single person, and may operate according to no schedule whatsoever. The definition confused the features of one culturally prominent category of vehicle — the scheduled public conveyance — with the necessary conditions of the concept itself.
passage:
Consider three conveyances that are unquestionably vehicles. A horse-drawn carriage follows a regular route between two towns, carrying passengers according to a fixed schedule. A steam locomotive pulls carriages along iron rails between cities, transporting passengers and goods on a published timetable. A sailing ship carries passengers and cargo across the sea along established maritime routes. From these cases one might extract a definition: a conveyance is a vehicle if and only if it follows a fixed route, it is designed to carry multiple passengers or a substantial quantity of goods, and it is operated according to a schedule. The three cases satisfy all three conditions, and the definition appears to identify the essential features of a vehicle.

But one must consider the child's bicycle. A child rides a bicycle to school each morning, choosing a different route according to whim — through the park on one day, along the canal on another, down the high street on a third. The bicycle carries no one but the child and, on occasion, a school satchel strapped to the handlebars. It follows no fixed route; the child's path varies daily. It carries no multiple passengers and no substantial cargo; it is a conveyance for one small person. And it operates according to no schedule or timetable; the child rides when the child is ready, arriving when chance and inclination dictate. All three conditions of the definition fail. Yet the bicycle is a vehicle. It is listed as such in dictionaries; it is classified as such by highway authorities; and it is, in the most straightforward sense, a means of conveyance — a device by which a person travels from one place to another. The background cases were drawn from the domain of public and commercial transport, and the definition mistook the features of that domain — fixed routes, passenger capacity, scheduled operation — for necessary features of the concept of a vehicle, when in truth a vehicle may be private, personal, route-less, and entirely unscheduled.

background_cases: A carpenter uses a saw to cut timber into planks, holding the saw in both hands and drawing it back and forth across the wood.
A stonemason uses a chisel and mallet to shape blocks of limestone, striking the chisel with the mallet to chip away material.
A blacksmith uses a hammer and anvil to forge iron, heating the metal in a forge and striking it into shape upon the anvil's face.
definition: An object x is a tool if and only if (i) x is used to apply force to a material in order to cut, shape, or join it, (ii) x is wielded by the hands and arms of the user, and (iii) x acts directly upon its material without any intervening mechanism or stored energy.
conditions: x is used to apply force to a material in order to cut, shape, or join it
x is wielded by the hands and arms of the user
x acts directly upon its material without any intervening mechanism or stored energy
counterexample: Consider a pair of spectacles. Spectacles are tools of vision — instruments that enable a person whose sight is deficient to see with clarity. They are placed upon the nose and hooked behind the ears; they are not wielded by the hands and arms. They do not apply force to any material; they refract light through shaped lenses. And they do not act upon any material in the sense of cutting, shaping, or joining; they act upon light itself, bending it to correct the deficiencies of the eye. All three conditions fail, yet spectacles are tools — tools of sight, as indispensable to the scholar and the craftsperson as any hammer or chisel.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all instances of manual material-working — sawing, chiselling, forging — in which force is applied by hand to reshape a physical substance. The definition abstracted force-application, hand-wielding, and direct material contact as essential to the concept of a tool. But the cases were drawn from the workshop and the forge, and the definition mistook the features of material-working tools for the features of tools as such. Spectacles reveal that a tool need not apply force, need not be wielded by the hands, and need not act upon a material at all. What is essential to a tool is that it extends or augments a human capacity — whether the capacity to shape iron or the capacity to see — and the concept of a tool is broad enough to encompass instruments that act upon light, upon sound, upon information, no less than upon wood and stone.
passage:
Consider three objects that are paradigmatic tools. A carpenter's saw, held in both hands and drawn back and forth across timber to cut it into planks. A stonemason's chisel, struck with a mallet to chip away limestone and shape it into blocks. A blacksmith's hammer, brought down upon heated iron to forge it into useful forms. From these cases one might extract a definition: an object is a tool if and only if it is used to apply force to a material in order to cut, shape, or join it, it is wielded by the hands and arms of the user, and it acts directly upon its material without any intervening mechanism or stored energy. The three cases satisfy all three conditions, and the definition appears to identify what is essential to a tool.

But one must consider spectacles. A pair of spectacles — two shaped lenses set in a frame and resting upon the nose — is a tool. It is a tool of vision, an instrument that enables a person whose natural sight is deficient to read, to work, to navigate the world with clarity and precision. Spectacles have been described as tools since their invention, and no competent speaker would deny them the title. Yet spectacles do not apply force to any material; they do not cut, shape, or join anything. They are not wielded by the hands and arms; they sit passively upon the face. And they do not act directly upon a material in the relevant sense; they act upon light, bending it through curved glass to correct the optical deficiencies of the eye. All three conditions of the definition fail. The background cases were drawn from the domain of manual material-working — the carpenter's shop, the mason's yard, the blacksmith's forge — and the definition abstracted the features of that domain as necessary conditions of the concept. Spectacles reveal that the concept of a tool is far broader than any account centred on force, hand-wielding, and material-shaping can accommodate. A tool is whatever extends or augments a human capacity, and the capacity to see is no less a candidate for augmentation than the capacity to cut or to strike.

background_cases: A tenant promises a landlord to pay the monthly rent on the first of each month, and both parties regard this commitment as binding.
A borrower promises a lender to repay a debt of ten pounds by the end of the year, and the lender regards the promise as a firm obligation.
A tradesman promises a customer to deliver and install a new window by Thursday, and the customer relies upon this commitment in arranging to be at home.
definition: An utterance U constitutes a promise if and only if (i) U commits the speaker to performing a specific action within a definite time, (ii) the promised action is one that the hearer desires the speaker to perform, and (iii) a failure to fulfil U would result in measurable material harm to the hearer.
conditions: U commits the speaker to performing a specific action within a definite time
the promised action is one that the hearer desires the speaker to perform
a failure to fulfil U would result in measurable material harm to the hearer
counterexample: A woman promises her elderly father that she will visit him every Sunday afternoon. The father is not in material need; he is well provided for, comfortable, and wants for nothing in a practical sense. A failure to visit would cause no measurable material harm — no financial loss, no disruption to his affairs, no damage to his property. What the father desires is his daughter's company, and what the broken promise would cause is not material harm but loneliness, disappointment, and the particular pain of being forgotten by those one loves. The third condition fails, yet the promise is genuine and its breach would be a serious moral failing.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all commercial or quasi-commercial transactions — paying rent, repaying a debt, delivering goods — in which the failure to keep a promise would result in clear, measurable material harm to the hearer. The definition abstracted material harm as essential to the concept of a promise because it was present in every case examined. But the cases were drawn from the domain of economic exchange, and the definition mistook the commercial context of these promises for a necessary feature of promising itself. The daughter's promise to visit reveals that a promise may concern an action whose value is entirely emotional and relational, and that the harm caused by a broken promise may be grief, loneliness, and disappointment rather than any measurable material loss. The concept of a promise is not confined to transactions that can be valued in pecuniary terms.
passage:
Consider three cases of promising. A tenant promises a landlord to pay rent on the first of each month. A borrower promises a lender to repay a debt of ten pounds by year's end. A tradesman promises a customer to deliver and install a window by Thursday. From these cases one might extract a definition: an utterance constitutes a promise if and only if it commits the speaker to performing a specific action within a definite time, the promised action is one the hearer desires the speaker to perform, and a failure to fulfil the promise would result in measurable material harm to the hearer. The three cases satisfy all three conditions: in each, the failure to keep the promise would cause clear financial or practical harm — unpaid rent, an outstanding debt, an uninstalled window.

But one must consider the daughter's promise to visit. A woman promises her elderly father that she will come to see him every Sunday afternoon. The father is comfortable, well provided for, and in no material need. A failure to visit would cause him no financial loss, no disruption to his practical affairs, no damage to his property or prospects. The harm caused by a broken promise would be entirely of another kind: loneliness, the ache of an empty afternoon, the particular sorrow of feeling that one's daughter has forgotten. These are real harms, and grievous ones, but they are not measurable in material terms. The third condition of the definition fails. The background cases were all drawn from the sphere of economic and practical exchange, and the definition abstracted material harm as a necessary feature of promising. The daughter's promise reveals that the concept of a promise extends far beyond transactions that can be valued in pounds and shillings. A promise may concern an action whose entire value is emotional, relational, and personal, and the harm of its breach may be sorrow rather than loss.

background_cases: Football is a game: two teams of eleven players compete on a pitch, following codified rules enforced by a referee, to score more goals than the opposing side within a fixed period of time.
Tennis is a game: two players or two pairs compete on a court, following codified rules enforced by an umpire, to win more sets than the opponent within a structured format.
Cricket is a game: two teams compete on a field, following detailed laws enforced by umpires, to score more runs than the opposing side within a defined number of innings.
definition: An activity A is a game if and only if (i) A is a physical contest between two opposing sides, (ii) A is governed by a body of codified rules enforced by an impartial official, and (iii) A takes place on a specially prepared playing surface with marked boundaries.
conditions: A is a physical contest between two opposing sides
A is governed by a body of codified rules enforced by an impartial official
A takes place on a specially prepared playing surface with marked boundaries
counterexample: Consider the parlour game of charades, in which participants take turns acting out the title of a book, film, or song through mime, whilst the other participants attempt to guess the title. Charades is a game — it is played at gatherings, it has rules, it is engaged in for amusement, and participants speak of playing it, winning it, and losing it. Yet it is not a physical contest between two opposing sides in any meaningful sense; the emphasis is upon performance and guessing rather than upon physical competition. It is not governed by codified rules enforced by an impartial official; the rules are informal and customary, and no referee presides. And it does not require a specially prepared playing surface with marked boundaries; it is played in drawing rooms, kitchens, and gardens, on any surface or none. All three conditions fail.
abductive_insight: The background cases — football, tennis, cricket — were all organised, competitive, outdoor sports played on prepared surfaces under the supervision of officials. The definition abstracted physical contest, codified rules with officials, and specially prepared surfaces as essential features of games. But the background cases were drawn from a single, prominent subclass of games — formal athletic sports — and the definition mistook the features of this subclass for the features of games as such. Charades reveals that a game may be cooperative or performative rather than competitive, may be governed by informal custom rather than by codified rules enforced by officials, and may require no special playing surface at all. The definition confused the features of organised sport with the concept of a game, overlooking the vast domain of parlour games, word games, guessing games, and imaginative games that are played without pitches, without referees, and without physical contest.
passage:
Consider three activities that are paradigmatic games. Football, in which two sides of eleven players compete on a prepared pitch, following rules enforced by a referee, to place the ball in the opposing goal more often than their opponents do. Tennis, in which two players or two pairs compete on a marked court, following rules enforced by an umpire, to win the requisite number of sets. Cricket, in which two teams compete on a carefully maintained ground, following detailed laws enforced by two umpires, to outscore the opposing side. From these cases one might extract a definition: an activity is a game if and only if it is a physical contest between two opposing sides, it is governed by codified rules enforced by an impartial official, and it takes place on a specially prepared playing surface with marked boundaries.

But one must consider charades. This game has been a staple of social gatherings in English-speaking countries for well over a century. One participant selects the title of a book, a play, a film, or a song, and communicates it to the other participants through mime alone — without speaking, without writing, using only gesture and pantomime. The other participants attempt to guess the title. The game is played with evident enjoyment; there are winners and losers of a sort; and no competent speaker of English would hesitate to call it a game. Yet charades is not a physical contest between two opposing sides. There is no pitch, no ball, no physical confrontation. The emphasis is upon theatrical ingenuity and collaborative guessing rather than upon athletic competition. The rules of charades are not codified in any formal sense; they are customary, varying from household to household, and no impartial official enforces them. If a dispute arises, it is settled by laughter and negotiation, not by appeal to a rulebook. And charades does not take place on a specially prepared playing surface. It is played in drawing rooms, around dinner tables, in gardens — wherever people happen to be gathered. The definition, drawn from the world of organised sport, has mistaken the features of competitive athletics for the essential features of games, and it has thereby excluded one of the most familiar and widespread games of the domestic world.

background_cases: A marble sculpture by Michelangelo, carved from a single block of stone, depicting the human form with extraordinary anatomical precision and emotional force, is a work of art.
An oil painting by Vermeer, depicting a woman reading a letter by a window, rendered with meticulous attention to the play of light upon surfaces, is a work of art.
A bronze casting by Rodin, depicting a figure in torment, modelled with expressive distortion and powerful surface texture, is a work of art.
definition: An object O is a work of art if and only if (i) O is a unique, one-of-a-kind physical object produced by an individual artist, (ii) O is made from traditional fine-art materials such as marble, oil paint, or bronze, and (iii) O requires years of technical apprenticeship to produce.
conditions: O is a unique, one-of-a-kind physical object produced by an individual artist
O is made from traditional fine-art materials such as marble, oil paint, or bronze
O requires years of technical apprenticeship to produce
counterexample: Consider a woodcut print by Albrecht Duerer — his 'Rhinoceros,' for instance, or the 'Apocalypse' series. These prints are works of art of the highest order, admired for their draughtsmanship, their imaginative power, and their technical brilliance. Yet a print is not a unique, one-of-a-kind object; it is one of many impressions pulled from a single block, and each impression is as much the work of art as any other. The prints are not made from traditional fine-art materials in the sense intended — they are made from ink and paper, not from marble or bronze. And whilst the cutting of a woodblock requires great skill, the printing of an impression from the block can be carried out by a competent craftsman without years of technical apprenticeship. The first condition fails, and the second is at best strained, yet Duerer's prints are indisputably works of art.
abductive_insight: The background cases — Michelangelo's sculpture, Vermeer's painting, Rodin's bronze — were all unique objects produced by individual masters working in traditional materials after years of training. The definition abstracted uniqueness, traditional materials, and technical apprenticeship as essential to art. But the background cases were drawn from the most prestigious and materially exclusive forms of Western fine art, and the definition mistook the features of these forms for the features of art as such. Duerer's woodcuts reveal that a work of art may exist in multiple copies, may be produced from humble materials, and may be reproducible by persons other than the original artist. The definition confused the aura of the unique masterpiece with the concept of art itself, overlooking the long and honourable tradition of printmaking, in which artistic genius is expressed through a medium designed for multiplication rather than singularity.
passage:
Consider three objects that are recognised as works of art of the highest rank. A marble sculpture by Michelangelo — the David, let us say — carved from a single block of Carrara marble by the hand of an individual master after years of training in the sculptor's workshop. An oil painting by Vermeer, depicting a quiet domestic scene with a mastery of light and colour that required decades of technical apprenticeship to achieve. A bronze casting by Rodin, modelled in clay by the artist's hands, cast by the lost-wax process, and existing as a unique expression of the sculptor's vision. From these cases one might extract a definition: an object is a work of art if and only if it is a unique, one-of-a-kind physical object produced by an individual artist, it is made from traditional fine-art materials such as marble, oil paint, or bronze, and it requires years of technical apprenticeship to produce.

But one must consider the woodcuts of Albrecht Duerer. His 'Apocalypse' series, published in 1498, depicts the visions of the Book of Revelation with a power and precision that have never been surpassed in the medium. His 'Rhinoceros' of 1515, based upon a written description and a sketch of an Indian rhinoceros that Duerer himself never saw, became the definitive European image of the animal for three centuries. These prints are works of art by any reasonable standard; they hang in the greatest museums of the world, they are studied, admired, and collected, and they are the products of one of the most gifted artistic minds in the history of Western culture. Yet a woodcut print is not a unique, one-of-a-kind object. It is one of many impressions pulled from a carved wooden block, and each impression — so long as it is pulled from the block before significant wear — is as much the work of art as any other. Hundreds of impressions of the 'Rhinoceros' were printed, and each is a genuine Duerer. The prints are made from ink and paper, not from marble, oil paint, or bronze. And whilst the cutting of the block requires extraordinary skill, the printing of impressions from the block is a craft that can be performed by a competent printer. The background cases were all singular masterpieces in prestigious materials, and the definition abstracted their uniqueness, their materiality, and their dependence upon prolonged apprenticeship as essential to art. But the woodcut tradition reveals that art may be multiple, reproducible, and realised in the humblest of materials, and that the genius of the artist may reside not in the preciousness of the object but in the power of the conception.

background_cases: A man promises his friend that he will return a borrowed book by Friday, stating clearly, 'I promise to return it by Friday,' and both parties understand the obligation thereby created.

A merchant promises a customer that the goods ordered will be delivered within a fortnight, declaring, 'You have my word — a fortnight at the most,' and the customer relies upon this assurance in making plans.

A physician promises a patient that the results of a medical examination will be communicated to no one else, saying, 'I give you my word of absolute confidentiality,' and the patient discloses personal information on the strength of this assurance.
definition: An utterance U by a person A constitutes a promise if and only if (i) A explicitly states that they or they are making a promise, using words such as 'I promise' or 'I give my word,' (ii) U concerns a matter over which A has full and sole control, and (iii) the promisee — the person to whom the promise is made — has requested the assurance that U provides.
conditions: A explicitly states that they or they are making a promise, using words such as 'I promise' or 'I give my word'
U concerns a matter over which A has full and sole control
the promisee — the person to whom the promise is made — has requested the assurance that U provides
counterexample: Consider a mother who, tucking her child into bed, says quietly, 'I shall always look after you.' She does not use the words 'I promise' or 'I give my word.' The matter — looking after the child always — is not one over which she has full and sole control; illness, accident, or death may prevent her from fulfilling it. And the child has not requested this assurance; the mother offers it spontaneously, moved by tenderness. All three conditions fail. Yet the mother has made a promise — one of the most solemn and binding promises a human being can make. The child understands it as such, and the mother understands it as such, and the obligation it creates is felt by both as no less real than any formally declared undertaking.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all explicit, formally declared promises concerning matters within the promisor's control, made in response to the promisee's need for assurance — returning a book, delivering goods, maintaining confidentiality. The definition abstracted explicit declaration, full control, and prior request as essential to promising. But the background cases were drawn from transactional, adult contexts in which promises function as instruments of practical coordination. The mother's bedside promise reveals that a promise may be made without any explicit promissory formula, may concern a matter over which the promisor has incomplete control, and may be offered spontaneously rather than in response to a request. The definition mistook the features of formal, transactional promises for the essence of promising itself, overlooking the vast domain of promises that arise from love, from duty, and from the spontaneous expression of commitment — promises that are no less binding for being informal, uncertain in their fulfilment, and unrequested.
passage:
Consider three instances of promising. A man tells his friend, 'I promise to return your book by Friday,' and both parties understand that an obligation has been created. A merchant tells a customer, 'You have my word — the goods will arrive within a fortnight,' and the customer arranges his affairs on the strength of this assurance. A physician tells a patient, 'I give you my word of absolute confidentiality,' and the patient reveals intimate details of his condition. In each case the promisor uses an explicit formula of commitment, the promise concerns a matter within the promisor's control, and the promisee has sought the assurance that the promise provides. From these cases one might extract a definition: an utterance constitutes a promise if and only if the speaker explicitly declares that a promise is being made, the promise concerns a matter over which the speaker has full and sole control, and the promisee has requested the assurance.

But one must consider the promise a mother makes to her child at bedtime. She leans over the small figure in the bed and says, quietly and without ceremony, 'I shall always look after you.' She does not say 'I promise' or 'I give my word'; the promissory formula is absent. The matter she addresses — looking after her child always — is not one over which she has full and sole control. She may fall ill; she may die; circumstances beyond her power may separate them. The promise, taken at its word, is one she cannot guarantee to keep. And the child has not requested this assurance. The child has not said, 'Will you always look after me?' The mother speaks spontaneously, moved by the tenderness of the moment, by the vulnerability of the small person before her. All three conditions of the definition fail. Yet what the mother has done is to make a promise — one of the deepest and most consequential promises that human life affords. She understands it as a promise; the child, even at a young age, understands it as a promise; and the obligation it creates will shape the conduct of both for years to come. The background cases were drawn from the world of adult transactions — the returned book, the delivered goods, the confidential consultation — in which promises are explicit, controlled, and requested. The definition mistook the features of this transactional world for the essence of promising, and thereby excluded the promises that matter most: the promises made from love, offered freely, concerning matters that exceed our control, and binding precisely because they express not a calculated undertaking but an unconditional commitment of the self.

background_cases: English is a language; it has a vocabulary of words, a grammar governing their combination, and it is spoken and written by a community of speakers who use it to communicate thoughts and information.
French is a language; it has a distinct vocabulary, a set of grammatical rules, and a community of speakers who employ it in speech and writing to express and transmit ideas.
Classical Latin is a language; it possesses a vocabulary, a grammar, and a body of written literature, and it was used by a community of speakers for communication over many centuries.
definition: A system of signs S is a language if and only if (i) S has a vocabulary — a finite stock of meaningful units (words) from which expressions are constructed, (ii) S has a grammar — a set of rules governing the combination of words into well-formed expressions, and (iii) S is used by a community of speakers for the purpose of communicating propositional content — that is, for asserting truths, asking questions, and giving commands.
conditions: S has a vocabulary — a finite stock of meaningful units
S has a grammar — rules for combining units into well-formed expressions
S is used by a community of speakers for communicating propositional content
counterexample: Consider the system of musical notation employed in Western music. This system has a vocabulary — a finite stock of meaningful symbols: the note-heads, rests, clefs, time signatures, dynamic markings, and expression indications. It has a grammar — rules governing the combination of these symbols into well-formed scores: the conventions of staff notation, the rules for placing notes upon the staff, the requirements of time signatures, and the principles of harmonic notation. And it is used by a community — composers, performers, and musicologists — who employ it to communicate musical ideas with great precision. Yet it is not a language in the ordinary sense. It does not communicate propositional content; a musical score does not assert truths, ask questions, or give commands. It conveys instructions for the production of sounds, and through those sounds it may express emotions or embody formal structures, but it does not say anything that could be true or false. The third condition fails, yet the system of musical notation satisfies the first two conditions and is used communicatively by a community.
abductive_insight: The background cases — English, French, Classical Latin — were all natural languages used for the full range of human communication, including the assertion of truths and the exchange of information. The definition abstracted vocabulary, grammar, and the communication of propositional content as the jointly essential features of language. But the background cases were drawn from the domain of spoken and written natural languages, and the definition mistook the communicative function of that particular class of sign systems for a necessary feature of all systems that might be called languages. Musical notation reveals that a system may possess vocabulary and grammar, and may be used communicatively by a community, without communicating anything that could be true or false. The definition confused propositional communication — a feature of natural languages — with communication as such, which may convey instructions, express affects, or embody structures without asserting anything.
passage:
Consider three systems that are paradigmatically languages. English has a vocabulary of many thousands of words, a grammar governing their combination into sentences, and a vast community of speakers who use it to communicate thoughts, to assert truths, to ask questions, and to give commands. French possesses the same features — a distinct vocabulary, a set of grammatical rules, and a community that employs the language for the full range of communicative purposes. Classical Latin, though no longer spoken as a native tongue, had a vocabulary, a grammar, and a community of speakers who used it to communicate propositional content of every kind. From these cases one might extract a definition: a system of signs is a language if and only if it has a vocabulary, a grammar, and is used by a community for the purpose of communicating propositional content.

But one must consider the system of musical notation employed in Western music since the seventeenth century. This system has a vocabulary — a finite stock of meaningful symbols.
