The note-heads denote pitch and duration; the rests denote silence; the clefs assign pitches to the lines and spaces of the staff; the time signatures specify the metre; the dynamic markings indicate volume; the expression indications specify the character of performance. These symbols are as determinate in their meanings as any words in a natural language, and a competent musician reads them with the same fluency with which a literate person reads English. The system has a grammar — a set of conventions governing the combination of symbols into well-formed scores. Notes must be placed correctly upon the staff; the durations within a bar must sum to the value prescribed by the time signature; harmonic notation follows established principles. A score that violates these conventions is malformed, just as a sentence that violates the rules of English grammar is malformed. And the system is used by a community — composers, performers, conductors, musicologists — who employ it to communicate musical ideas with great precision across centuries and continents. Yet musical notation is not a language in the ordinary sense. A musical score does not assert truths, ask questions, or give commands. It conveys instructions for the production of sounds, and those sounds may express emotions, embody formal structures, or occasion aesthetic experiences of the most profound sort, but they do not say anything that could be true or false. The third condition of the definition fails. The background cases — English, French, Latin — were all natural languages used for propositional communication, and the definition treated this function as essential. Musical notation reveals that a system may possess vocabulary and grammar, and may serve as a powerful instrument of communication within a community, without communicating anything propositional whatsoever.

background_cases: A hammer is a tool; it is a physical object, manufactured for a specific purpose — the driving of nails — and it is held in the hand and operated by the muscular effort of its user.
A screwdriver is a tool; it is a physical object designed for turning screws, held in the hand, and operated by the rotation of the wrist.
A saw is a tool; it is a manufactured object designed for cutting wood, held and guided by the hands of the carpenter who uses it.
definition: An object O is a tool if and only if (i) O is a portable physical object that can be held in the hand, (ii) O is designed and manufactured for a single, specific practical purpose, and (iii) O amplifies the muscular force of its user — that is, O enables the user to exert more force upon a material than the user could exert with the bare hand.
conditions: O is a portable physical object that can be held in the hand
O is designed and manufactured for a single, specific practical purpose
O amplifies the muscular force of its user
counterexample: Consider a magnifying glass. A magnifying glass is a tool; opticians, jewellers, and naturalists refer to it as a tool of their trade, and it is listed among the tools of any workshop concerned with fine work. It is a portable physical object that can be held in the hand. But it is not designed for a single specific purpose; it is used for reading fine print, for examining specimens, for inspecting hallmarks, for starting fires, and for a dozen other purposes. And it does not amplify the muscular force of its user. The magnifying glass exerts no force upon any material; it amplifies not force but vision — it enlarges the apparent size of small objects by the refraction of light through a convex lens. The second and third conditions fail, yet the magnifying glass is a tool.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the hammer, the screwdriver, the saw — were all hand-held instruments that apply mechanical force to a material for a specific purpose. The definition abstracted portability, single purpose, and force amplification as the essential features of tools. But the background cases were drawn from the narrow domain of the carpenter's or the builder's toolkit — instruments of mechanical force — and the definition mistook the features of that domain for the features of tools as such. The magnifying glass reveals that a tool may amplify a human capacity other than muscular force — in this case, the capacity of vision — and may serve a variety of purposes rather than a single one. The definition confused the mechanical tool with the tool in general.
passage:
Consider three objects that are paradigmatic tools. A hammer is a physical object, portable, held in the hand, designed and manufactured for the specific purpose of driving nails, and it amplifies the muscular force of its user — the head of the hammer, swung through an arc, delivers a blow far more powerful than the fist could deliver unaided. A screwdriver is held in the hand, designed for the single purpose of turning screws, and it amplifies the force of the wrist through the mechanical advantage of its shaft and tip. A saw is held and guided by the hands, designed for the purpose of cutting wood, and its toothed blade enables the carpenter to sever timber that his bare hands could not break. From these cases one might extract a definition: an object is a tool if and only if it is a portable object held in the hand, designed for a single specific purpose, and amplifies the muscular force of its user.

But one must consider a magnifying glass. The magnifying glass is called a tool by opticians, by jewellers, by watchmakers, by naturalists, and by craftsmen of every description. It is listed among the tools of the workshop and the laboratory, and no one would hesitate to call it a tool. It is a portable physical object that can be held in the hand, and in this respect it satisfies the first condition of the definition. But it is not designed for a single specific purpose. The jeweller uses it to examine the setting of a stone; the naturalist uses it to inspect the wing of an insect; the philatelist uses it to read a postmark; the boy uses it to focus the rays of the sun upon a piece of paper and set it alight. It serves many purposes, not one. And it does not amplify the muscular force of its user. The magnifying glass exerts no force upon any material whatsoever. It does not enable the user to strike harder, to cut more deeply, or to turn more firmly. What it amplifies is not force but vision. By refracting the light that passes through its convex lens, it enlarges the apparent size of small objects and reveals details that the unaided eye cannot discern. The second and third conditions fail. The background cases were all drawn from the domain of mechanical hand-tools — hammers, screwdrivers, saws — instruments that apply force to a resistant material, and the definition abstracted force amplification as essential to the concept of a tool. The magnifying glass reveals that tools may amplify capacities other than muscular force, and that the concept of a tool encompasses instruments of perception and inspection as well as instruments of mechanical action.

background_cases: Chess is a game: two players sit across a board, follow a set of rules governing how each piece may move, and attempt to checkmate the opponent's king. The game ends when one player wins.

Tennis is a game: two players (or two pairs) hit a ball back and forth across a net according to rules governing service, scoring, and boundaries. The game ends when one player has won a specified number of sets.

Poker is a game: several players are dealt cards, place bets according to the rules, and attempt to win the pot by holding the best hand or by inducing the others to fold. The game ends when the stakes are distributed.
definition: An activity A is a game if and only if (i) A is governed by rules that define what counts as a legal move and what does not, (ii) A involves competition — two or more parties each attempt to win, and the success of one diminishes the prospects of the others, and (iii) A produces a determinate outcome — a winner and a loser (or losers).
conditions: A is governed by rules defining legal and illegal moves
A involves competition — the success of one party diminishes the others' prospects
A produces a determinate outcome — a winner and a loser
counterexample: Consider the activity of children building a sandcastle together on a beach. The children cooperate; they assign roles — one fills the bucket, another pats the turrets, a third digs the moat — and they follow informal conventions about how the castle should look. They call this activity a game; their parents call it a game; and it is a game in any ordinary sense of the word. Yet it involves no competition. The children are not trying to defeat one another; the success of one does not diminish the prospects of the others; there is no winner and no loser. The activity does not produce a determinate outcome in the sense of a victory; it ends when the tide comes in or the children grow tired, not when someone has won. The second and third conditions fail, yet the activity is a game.
abductive_insight: The background cases — chess, tennis, poker — were all competitive games with clear winners and losers. The definition abstracted competition and determinate outcome as essential features of all games. But the background cases were drawn from the domain of formal, competitive contests, and the definition mistook the features of that domain for the features of games as such. The sandcastle reveals that an activity may be a game without involving any competition whatever, and that games may be cooperative, open-ended, and without winners or losers.
passage:
Consider three activities that are paradigmatically games. Chess is a game: two players follow elaborate rules governing the movement of pieces upon a board, and each attempts to checkmate the other's king; the game ends in victory for one and defeat for the other. Tennis is a game: the players hit a ball back and forth across a net according to detailed rules of service and scoring, and the game ends when one player has won the required number of sets. Poker is a game: the players are dealt cards, place bets, and compete to hold the best hand; the pot goes to the winner. From these cases one might extract a definition: an activity is a game if and only if it is governed by rules that define legal moves, it involves competition in which the success of one party diminishes the others' prospects, and it produces a determinate outcome — a winner and a loser.

But one must consider the activity of children building a sandcastle together on a beach. Three or four children gather at the water's edge with buckets and spades. They set about constructing a castle: one child fills the buckets with wet sand and upends them to form turrets; another digs a moat around the growing structure; a third decorates the walls with shells and pebbles. They confer about the design — 'Shall we have a drawbridge?' 'The moat should go all the way round' — and they assign and reassign roles as the work progresses. They call this activity a game, and so do their parents: 'Go and play a game on the beach.' It is a game in any ordinary sense of the word; it is play, it is voluntary, it is absorbing, and it is pursued for its own sake. Yet it involves no competition. The children are not trying to defeat one another; there are no opposing sides. The success of one child — the successful construction of a turret, the excavation of a satisfactory moat — does not diminish the prospects of the others; on the contrary, it advances the common enterprise. And the activity produces no determinate outcome in the sense of a victory. There is no winner and no loser; the game ends when the tide comes in, or when the children are called to tea, or when they simply tire of the activity. The second and third conditions of the definition fail. The background cases were all competitive games, drawn from the domain of formal contests, and the definition abstracted competition and determinate outcome as essential. The sandcastle reveals that games may be cooperative, open-ended, and indeterminate, and that the concept of a game is far broader than the domain of competitive contests from which the definition was drawn.

background_cases: A man says to his friend, 'I promise to return your book by Friday,' looking him in the eye and speaking with evident sincerity. The friend lends the book, relying on the promise.
A woman says to her employer, 'I promise to complete the report by the end of the week,' in a formal meeting where the undertaking is recorded. The employer relies on the commitment.
A father says to his child, 'I promise to take you to the zoo on Saturday,' and the child, trusting the promise, tells her friends that she will visit the zoo.
definition: A person P makes a promise if and only if (i) P utters the words 'I promise' (or an equivalent formula) to another person, (ii) P is sincere — that is, P intends at the time of utterance to fulfil the commitment, and (iii) the person to whom the promise is addressed relies upon it — that is, the promisee arranges their or their affairs in the expectation that the promise will be kept.
conditions: P utters 'I promise' or an equivalent formula
P is sincere — P intends to fulfil the commitment
the promisee relies upon the promise
counterexample: Consider a person who says to his creditor, 'I promise to repay you next month,' fully intending, at the moment of speaking, not to repay a penny. He uses the formula 'I promise'; the creditor relies upon the promise and forbears from taking legal action. The man is insincere — he has no intention of fulfilling the commitment. Yet he has made a promise. If he fails to repay, the creditor will rightly say not merely that the man failed to pay but that he broke his promise. One can break only what one has made; and a promise made insincerely is still a promise, albeit a dishonest one. The second condition fails, yet a promise has been made.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all instances of sincere promising — cases in which the promisor genuinely intended to do what he or she undertook to do. The definition abstracted sincerity as a necessary condition of promising. But the background cases were drawn from the domain of honest, well-intentioned commitments, and the definition confused sincerity — a moral quality of the promisor — with a constitutive condition of the promise itself. The insincere debtor reveals that promising is a social and linguistic act whose existence depends upon the utterance of the appropriate words in the appropriate circumstances, not upon the inner state of the speaker. A promise may be dishonest, but it is a promise nonetheless.
passage:
Consider three cases of promising. A man says to his friend, 'I promise to return your book by Friday,' looking him in the eye and speaking with evident sincerity; the friend lends the book in reliance upon the promise. A woman says to her employer in a formal meeting, 'I promise to complete the report by the end of the week'; the employer records the commitment and relies upon it. A father says to his young daughter, 'I promise to take you to the zoo on Saturday'; the child, trusting her father, tells her friends of the planned excursion. In each case, the promisor uses the formula 'I promise,' the promisor is sincere — genuinely intending to do what has been undertaken — and the promisee relies upon the commitment. From these cases one might extract a definition: a person makes a promise if and only if the person utters 'I promise' or an equivalent formula, the person is sincere, and the promisee relies upon the promise.

But one must consider the case of a debtor who says to his creditor, 'I promise to repay you next month,' with no intention whatever of repaying. The debtor is a dishonest man; he has spent the money and has no means of repaying it, and he knows this perfectly well as he utters the words. He uses the formula 'I promise' in the full hearing of the creditor, and the creditor, taking him at his word, forbears from pursuing legal remedies for another month. When the month has passed and no payment is forthcoming, the creditor is entitled to say not merely that the debtor has failed to pay but that the debtor has broken his promise. And this accusation is just; for one can break only what one has made, and the debtor did make a promise — a dishonest promise, a promise made in bad faith, but a promise nonetheless. The second condition of the definition — sincerity — fails. The debtor did not intend to fulfil the commitment at the time of utterance. Yet the promise was made. The background cases were all instances of honest promising, and the definition abstracted sincerity as a constitutive condition. The insincere debtor reveals that a promise is a social and linguistic act — an act performed by uttering the appropriate words in the appropriate circumstances — and that the inner state of the speaker, however important it may be to the moral evaluation of the promise, is not a condition of its existence. A promise made dishonestly is a bad promise, but it is a promise.

background_cases: A carpenter uses a hammer to drive nails into wood: the hammer extends the force of his arm, enabling him to accomplish what his bare hand could not.
A seamstress uses a needle to draw thread through fabric: the needle's point penetrates the cloth where her fingers could not, extending her ability to join pieces of material.
A farmer uses a plough to turn the soil: the plough's blade cuts through earth that his hands could not break, extending his capacity to prepare the ground for planting.
definition: An object O is a tool if and only if (i) O is a solid, portable artefact that can be held in one or both hands, (ii) O amplifies or extends a specific physical capacity of the human body, and (iii) O was designed — that is, it was created with the intention that it should serve the function it performs.
conditions: O is a solid, portable artefact holdable in the hands
O amplifies or extends a specific physical capacity of the body
O was designed with the intention of serving its function
counterexample: Consider a man walking along a riverbank who needs to crack open a hard-shelled nut. He picks up a smooth, heavy stone from the ground and uses it to strike the nut, cracking the shell. The stone amplifies the force of his hand; it extends a specific physical capacity — the ability to deliver a concentrated blow — that his bare fist could not match. It is solid and portable; he holds it in his hand. Yet the stone was not designed. No one created it with the intention that it should serve as a nutcracker; it was shaped by the river over thousands of years, and its suitability for the task at hand is a happy accident. The third condition fails. Yet the stone, in the man's hand, functions as a tool — it is used as a tool, it serves as a tool, and the man would not hesitate to call it one.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the hammer, the needle, the plough — were all manufactured artefacts, deliberately designed to serve specific functions. The definition abstracted design and intentional manufacture as essential to tool-hood. But the background cases were drawn from the domain of crafted implements, and the definition mistook the designed origin of those implements for a necessary condition of being a tool. The river stone reveals that an object may function as a tool — extending human capacity in the performance of a task — without having been designed for that purpose, and that what makes something a tool is not its origin but its use.
passage:
Consider three objects that are paradigmatic tools. A carpenter uses a hammer to drive nails into wood: the steel head, mounted upon a wooden handle, delivers a concentrated blow that the carpenter's bare hand could never produce, extending his capacity to join pieces of timber. A seamstress uses a needle to draw thread through fabric: the slender point penetrates the weave of the cloth where her fingers could not enter, extending her ability to stitch and mend. A farmer uses a plough to turn the soil: the iron blade cuts through compacted earth, extending his capacity to prepare the ground for seed. In each case, a solid, portable artefact, held in the hand, amplifies a specific physical capacity, and the object was designed — deliberately fashioned by a craftsman — to serve the function it performs. From these cases one might extract a definition: an object is a tool if and only if it is a solid, portable artefact holdable in the hands, it amplifies or extends a physical capacity, and it was designed with the intention of serving its function.

But one must consider a man walking along a riverbank on an autumn afternoon. He has gathered a handful of walnuts from a tree but cannot open them; the shells are too hard for his fingers. He looks about, picks up a smooth, heavy stone from the water's edge — a stone worn into a convenient shape by the current over thousands of years — and strikes the nut against a flat rock, using the stone to deliver the blow. The shell cracks; the kernel is exposed; the man eats the nut and cracks another. The stone amplifies the force of his hand as surely as the carpenter's hammer amplifies his; it extends a specific physical capacity — the ability to deliver a concentrated blow of sufficient force. It is solid, it is portable, and it is held in the hand. But the stone was not designed. No craftsman fashioned it; no intelligence shaped it for the purpose of cracking nuts. It was formed by the action of water upon rock, and its suitability for the man's purpose is entirely adventitious. The third condition of the definition fails. Yet the stone, in the man's hand, functions as a tool. He uses it as a tool; it serves him as a tool; and if asked what he is using to open the walnuts, he would answer, without hesitation, 'this stone' — identifying it as the instrument of his task. The background cases were all manufactured implements, and the definition treated deliberate design as essential. The river stone reveals that what makes something a tool is not how it came into existence but how it is put to use.

background_cases: A child, asked by his mother whether he has eaten the biscuits, says 'No,' though he has in fact eaten them. He lies in order to avoid punishment.
A salesman, asked whether the motorcar he is selling has been in an accident, says 'No,' though he knows it has been badly damaged. He lies in order to make the sale.
A student, asked by her tutor whether she has read the assigned text, says 'Yes,' though she has not opened it. She lies in order to avoid the tutor's disapproval.
definition: A person P tells a lie if and only if (i) P makes a declarative statement S, (ii) P believes S to be false, and (iii) P's motive for making S is to gain some personal advantage — to avoid a punishment, to secure a benefit, or to escape an unpleasant consequence.
conditions: P makes a declarative statement
P believes the statement to be false
P's motive is personal advantage
counterexample: Consider a doctor who tells a terminally ill patient, 'You are making excellent progress,' when in truth the patient is dying. The doctor believes the statement to be false; she knows the patient is not improving. She makes a declarative statement to the patient. Yet her motive is not personal advantage. She does not lie to avoid punishment, to secure a benefit for herself, or to escape an unpleasant consequence. She lies out of compassion — out of a desire to spare the patient the anguish of knowing that death is near, out of a wish to preserve the patient's hope and peace of mind during the weeks that remain. The third condition fails, yet the doctor's statement is a lie. It is, perhaps, a merciful lie, but it is a lie nonetheless.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the child, the salesman, the student — were all instances of self-serving dishonesty, in which the liar sought to gain some personal advantage. The definition abstracted self-interested motivation as essential to lying. But the background cases were drawn from the domain of selfish deception, and the definition mistook the motive of the liars in those cases for a necessary condition of lying as such. The doctor's compassionate falsehood reveals that a person may lie for entirely altruistic reasons — out of compassion, out of kindness, out of a desire to protect another from suffering — and that what makes a statement a lie is not the speaker's motive but the combination of assertion, disbelief, and the intention to produce a false impression.
passage:
Consider three cases of lying. A child, asked by his mother whether he has eaten the biscuits from the tin, says 'No,' though crumbs are still on his fingers. He lies to avoid punishment. A salesman, asked whether the motorcar he is offering has been in an accident, says 'No,' though he knows it was badly damaged in a collision and repaired. He lies to make the sale. A student, asked by her tutor whether she has completed the assigned reading, says 'Yes,' though she has not opened the book. She lies to avoid disapproval. In each case, the liar makes a declarative statement, believes the statement to be false, and is motivated by personal advantage — the avoidance of punishment, the securing of a sale, the escape from censure. From these cases one might extract a definition: a person tells a lie if and only if the person makes a declarative statement, believes it to be false, and is motivated by personal advantage.

But one must consider the case of a doctor attending a terminally ill patient. The patient asks, with evident anxiety, 'Am I getting better?' The doctor knows that the patient is not getting better — that the illness is progressing, that the treatments have failed, and that the patient has, at most, a few weeks to live. Yet the doctor says, gently, 'You are making excellent progress. The latest results are very encouraging.' The doctor makes a declarative statement. She believes it to be false; she knows the patient is dying. But her motive is not personal advantage. She does not lie to avoid punishment or to secure a benefit for herself. She lies out of compassion — out of a deeply felt desire to spare the patient the anguish of knowing the truth, to preserve what hope and peace of mind remain in the time that is left. Her lie is altruistic; it is motivated entirely by concern for the welfare of another. The third condition of the definition fails. The background cases were all instances of self-serving dishonesty, and the definition abstracted self-interested motivation as essential to lying. The doctor's compassionate falsehood reveals that the motive for a lie may be entirely unselfish, and that what constitutes a lie is not the speaker's purpose but the act of deliberately asserting what one believes to be false in circumstances where the listener is entitled to expect the truth.

background_cases: Chess is a game: it has explicit rules, two players compete to checkmate the other's king, and the activity is pursued for its intrinsic interest rather than for material necessity.
Football is a game: it has codified rules, two teams compete to score goals, and the players engage in the activity for the enjoyment it provides and the challenge it poses.
Poker is a game: it has rules governing the deal, the betting, and the ranking of hands; players compete to win the pot; and the activity is undertaken for recreation.
Hide-and-seek is a game: one player counts while the others hide, the seeker then searches for the hidden players, and the activity is pursued for the pleasure of the chase.
definition: An activity A is a game if and only if (i) A is governed by explicit rules that define what moves or actions are permissible, (ii) A involves competition between two or more participants, each striving to achieve a specified outcome, and (iii) A is engaged in voluntarily for recreation rather than out of material necessity.
conditions: A is governed by explicit rules
A involves competition between participants
A is engaged in voluntarily for recreation
counterexample: Consider a game of solitaire — the card game played by a single person who lays out the cards and attempts to arrange them in a specified order according to fixed rules. Solitaire is governed by explicit rules defining permissible moves. It is engaged in voluntarily for recreation. Yet it involves no competition between participants: there is only one player, and she strives not against an opponent but against the arrangement of the cards. The second condition is not met, yet solitaire is called a game without hesitation by any ordinary speaker, and it is sold as a game, discussed as a game, and experienced as a game.
abductive_insight: The background cases — chess, football, poker, hide-and-seek — all happened to involve competition between two or more participants, and this common feature was elevated to a necessary condition. But the feature that all the background cases truly shared was not competition as such but a structure of challenge — an obstacle to be overcome, whether that obstacle is an opponent, the arrangement of the cards, or the difficulty of the puzzle. The definition captured the competitive form of challenge but missed the broader category.
passage:
If one surveys the activities most readily called games — chess, football, poker, hide-and-seek — certain common features emerge. Each is governed by explicit rules. Each involves competition between two or more participants striving toward a specified outcome. And each is pursued voluntarily for the recreation and challenge it provides. From these cases one might extract the definition: an activity is a game if and only if it is governed by explicit rules, involves competition between participants, and is engaged in voluntarily for recreation.

Yet this definition, natural as it seems, is insufficient. Consider the card game of solitaire, known also as patience. Solitaire is governed by explicit rules — rules specifying how the cards may be laid out, which cards may be moved where, and what constitutes a successful conclusion. It is engaged in voluntarily and for recreation — no one plays solitaire under compulsion or out of material necessity. But it involves no competition between participants: there is only one player. She plays not against an opponent but against the arrangement of the cards, attempting to bring order out of the random deal. The second condition of the definition is plainly not satisfied, yet solitaire is a game by any ordinary reckoning. The background cases all happened to involve competition, and this feature was abstracted into a necessary condition. But what the background cases truly shared was not competition in the narrow sense but a structure of challenge — something to be overcome, whether an opponent, the cards, or the puzzle. Competition between persons is one form of challenge, but not the only form. The definition captured the particular form exhibited by the background cases and missed the general principle that underlies all of them.

background_cases: A merchant tells a customer that a garment is made of silk when the merchant privately regards it as a synthetic fabric. The merchant asserts a proposition he does not hold to be true, intending the customer to accept it as true, and thereby deceives the customer for financial gain.

A child tells his mother that he did not eat the biscuits when he privately recalls having eaten them. The child asserts a proposition he does not hold to be true, intending his mother to accept it, and thereby avoids punishment.

A diplomat tells a foreign official that her government has no plans to impose tariffs when she privately knows that such plans are well advanced. She asserts a proposition she does not hold to be true, intending the official to accept it, and thereby gains a negotiating advantage.
definition: A person S tells a lie if and only if (i) S asserts a proposition P to a hearer H, (ii) S does not hold P to be true, and (iii) S intends H to accept P as true.
conditions: S asserts a proposition P
S does not hold P to be true
S intends H to accept P as true
counterexample: Consider a case of what might be called a 'bald-faced lie.' A student is caught plagiarising an essay by a professor who has incontrovertible evidence — identical passages, matching metadata, a confession from the original author. The student, fully aware that the professor has this evidence, looks the professor in the eye and says, 'I wrote every word myself.' The student asserts a proposition she does not hold to be true. But she does not intend the professor to accept it as true — she knows the professor will not and cannot be deceived. Her assertion is a performance of defiance, a refusal to concede, or perhaps an attempt to make the formal proceedings more difficult. All three conditions appear relevant, but the third is not satisfied: there is no intention to produce a false acceptance. Yet the student's utterance is, by ordinary standards, a lie — indeed, a brazen one.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the merchant, the child, the diplomat — all involved situations in which the speaker had a reasonable expectation that the hearer would be deceived. The definition abstracted from these cases the feature of intending the hearer to accept the false proposition. But it missed the possibility of lies told without any realistic expectation of deception — lies that function as acts of defiance, manipulation of formal processes, or assertions of power, where the speaker's intent is not to deceive but to present a falsehood as if it were true, regardless of whether anyone is actually taken in.
passage:
The paradigmatic lies are cases in which a speaker asserts something she regards as false with the intention of deceiving her hearer. The merchant who misrepresents the fabric, the child who denies eating the biscuits, the diplomat who conceals her government's plans — each asserts a false proposition, each knows it to be false, and each intends the hearer to accept it. From these cases, one might extract the definition: a person tells a lie if and only if she asserts a proposition she does not hold to be true with the intention that the hearer accept it as true.

Yet this definition, when tested against a wider range of cases, reveals an unexpected insufficiency. Consider a student caught plagiarising an essay by a professor who possesses irrefutable evidence — identical passages, matching metadata, a confession from the original author. The student, fully aware of the evidence, looks the professor in the eye and says, 'I wrote every word myself.' The student asserts a proposition she does not hold to be true. But she cannot intend the professor to accept it — she knows perfectly well that the professor will not be deceived. Her lie is what one might call a bald-faced lie: an assertion of a falsehood made without any realistic expectation of deception, functioning instead as an act of defiance, a refusal to concede, or a tactic for complicating the formal proceedings. The background cases all involved speakers who had a reasonable prospect of deceiving their hearers; the definition extracted from these cases the intention to deceive. But in the case of the bald-faced lie, the intention to deceive is absent, and yet the utterance is plainly a lie — a particularly brazen one. What the definition has missed is that lying may consist in presenting a falsehood as true regardless of whether the speaker expects anyone to be taken in.

background_cases: A friend promises to help one move house on Saturday. She utters the words, one understands them as a commitment, and the mutual understanding creates a moral obligation that she acknowledges and intends to fulfil.

A contractor promises to complete the renovations by December. He signs a written commitment, the client understands it as a binding pledge, and the expectation of performance is grounded in the contractor's competence and goodwill.

A parent promises a child a trip to the seaside if the child finishes her homework. The parent utters the words with clear intent, the child understands them as a commitment, and the parent intends to follow through.
definition: A person S makes a genuine promise to a person R if and only if (i) S utters words to the effect that S will perform a future action A, (ii) R understands S's utterance as a commitment to perform A, and (iii) S intends, at the time of utterance, to perform A.
conditions: S utters words committing to a future action
R understands the utterance as a commitment
S intends at the time of utterance to perform the action
counterexample: Consider a person who promises a friend to attend her wedding, sincerely intending to do so. The words are uttered, the friend understands them as a commitment, and the promisor genuinely intends to attend. But the promisor later discovers that she must undergo emergency surgery on the day of the wedding and is physically unable to attend. At the time of the promise, all three conditions were met. Yet we should hesitate to say that the promisor has broken her promise in any morally significant sense — she was prevented by circumstances entirely beyond her control. The definition captures the making of a promise but says nothing about the conditions under which failure to perform is excusable, leaving it unclear whether the promise retains its binding force when performance becomes impossible through no fault of the promisor.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved situations in which the promisor had the ability to perform the promised action and was not impeded by extraordinary circumstances. The definition abstracted the intention to perform but did not consider what happens when the intention is genuine yet the ability is destroyed by unforeseen events. What the background cases shared, but the definition did not capture, was the tacit presupposition that the promisor would remain able to perform — that the normal conditions of agency would persist.
passage:
When one examines the clearest cases of promising — a friend pledging help, a contractor signing a commitment, a parent assuring a child — a common structure appears. The promisor utters words of commitment; the hearer understands them as such; and the promisor intends to follow through. From these cases, one might extract the definition: a genuine promise is made when a person utters words committing to a future action, the hearer understands the utterance as a commitment, and the speaker intends to perform.

Yet this definition, though it captures the act of promising, leaves unexplored a dimension that becomes apparent only when one considers promises that collide with unforeseen impossibility. Suppose a person promises her friend to attend the friend's wedding, sincerely intending to be there. The words are spoken, the friend takes them as a commitment, and the intention is genuine. But the promisor later learns that she requires emergency surgery on the very day of the wedding and cannot possibly attend. At the time of the promise, all three conditions were satisfied — the promise was, by the definition, genuine. Yet the promisor cannot fulfil it, and her failure is due entirely to circumstances beyond her control. We should hesitate to say that she has broken her promise in the morally loaded sense — she has not betrayed a trust or acted in bad faith. The background cases all presupposed that the promisor would remain able to perform the promised action. The definition abstracted the features visible in those cases — utterance, understanding, intention — but failed to capture the tacit condition that the promisor's agency remain intact. What is missing is an account of the conditions under which inability excuses non-performance, so that the moral force of the promise is properly delimited.

background_cases: Michelangelo's ceiling of the Sistine Chapel is a work of art: it was produced by an artist through sustained creative effort, it was intended as an object of contemplation and devotion, and it displays extraordinary formal mastery — composition, colour, draughtsmanship.

Beethoven's Ninth Symphony is a work of art: it was composed by a musician through deliberate creative labour, it was intended for aesthetic and emotional engagement, and it possesses formal qualities — structure, harmonic richness, melodic invention — of the highest order.

Keats's 'Ode on a Grecian Urn' is a work of art: it was written by a poet through careful composition, it was intended as an object of literary contemplation, and it exhibits formal excellence — metre, imagery, unity of theme.
definition: An object O is a work of art if and only if (i) O is produced by a human being through sustained creative effort, (ii) O is intended by its maker to engage the aesthetic sensibility of an audience, and (iii) O displays a high degree of formal mastery — skill in composition, technique, or craftsmanship.
conditions: O is produced through sustained creative effort
O is intended to engage aesthetic sensibility
O displays a high degree of formal mastery
counterexample: Consider the found poetry composed by collecting fragments of overheard conversation and arranging them on a page with minimal alteration. The 'poet' exercises little sustained creative effort — the words are not her own but are gathered from the speech of strangers. Whether the resulting text displays formal mastery is doubtful — its power, if it has any, derives from the raw quality of the found material rather than from the arranger's skill. Yet the intention to engage the aesthetic sensibility of an audience is present, and works of found poetry have been exhibited, published, and discussed as works of art. The first and third conditions are not clearly met, yet the object functions as art within the relevant community.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the Sistine Chapel ceiling, the Ninth Symphony, the Grecian Urn ode — all exemplified art at its most traditional: high-effort, high-skill, intention-laden production. The definition abstracted creative effort and formal mastery as necessary conditions. But these cases concealed the possibility that art might consist not in the production of new material through skilled labour but in the selection, arrangement, or recontextualisation of existing material, where the artistic act lies in the choosing and framing rather than in the making.
passage:
The works most readily acknowledged as art — the Sistine Chapel ceiling, Beethoven's Ninth Symphony, Keats's 'Ode on a Grecian Urn' — share certain prominent features. Each was produced through sustained creative effort by a gifted individual. Each was intended to engage the aesthetic sensibility of an audience. And each displays formal mastery of the highest order. One might distil from these cases the definition: an object is a work of art if and only if it is produced through sustained creative effort, intended to engage aesthetic sensibility, and displays a high degree of formal mastery.

Yet this definition, abstracted from the high peaks of the artistic tradition, proves too narrow when one descends to cases that the tradition did not foresee. Consider the practice of found poetry — the collection of fragments of overheard conversation, advertising copy, or bureaucratic prose, arranged on a page with minimal alteration. The practitioner exercises little sustained creative effort in the traditional sense: the words are not hers, and the labour of composition is largely replaced by the labour of attention and selection. Whether the result displays formal mastery is questionable — its force, if it possesses any, derives from the raw texture of the found material rather than from the arranger's technical skill. Yet found poems have been published, exhibited, and discussed as works of art, and the intention to engage the reader's aesthetic sensibility is plainly present. The background cases all exemplified art as high-effort, high-skill production — a model in which the artist is a maker who shapes raw material through trained ability. But they concealed the possibility that the artistic act might lie not in the making but in the selecting and framing — in the eye that recognises the poetic potential of overheard speech and the hand that arranges it for contemplation. The definition captured making but missed choosing, and thereby excluded a form of art that dispenses with traditional craftsmanship while retaining the power to illuminate.

background_cases: Chess is a game: it is governed by precise rules, involves strategic competition between two players, has a definite outcome (win, lose, or draw), and is played voluntarily for intellectual enjoyment.

Football is a game: it is governed by codified rules, involves physical competition between two teams, has a definite outcome determined by the score, and is played voluntarily for recreation and sport.

Poker is a game: it is governed by rules specifying the ranking of hands and the sequence of betting, involves competition among several players, has definite outcomes (each hand is won or lost), and is played voluntarily for entertainment.

Hide-and-seek is a game: it is governed by informal but clear rules (one person counts, the others hide, the seeker searches), involves a mild form of competition (the hider tries to avoid being found), has a loose outcome (being found or remaining hidden), and is played voluntarily by children for amusement.
definition: An activity A is a game if and only if (i) A is governed by rules that define permissible and impermissible actions, (ii) A has a definite outcome — a state of affairs that constitutes winning or losing, and (iii) A is freely undertaken — participation is voluntary and not compelled.
conditions: A is governed by rules defining permissible and impermissible actions
A has a definite outcome constituting winning or losing
A is freely undertaken
counterexample: Consider a group of children playing make-believe in a garden, pretending to be pirates on a desert island. The activity is governed by rules — there are things one may and may not do within the fiction (one cannot simply declare oneself invincible without the consent of the others; one must respect the agreed-upon geography of the imaginary island). And the activity is freely undertaken; no child is compelled to play. But there is no definite outcome. No one wins, no one loses; the play continues until the children tire of it or are called indoors. The second condition fails. Yet make-believe play is universally classified as a game by children and adults alike, and the concept of a game that admits of no definitive conclusion is thoroughly familiar.
abductive_insight: The background cases — chess, football, poker, hide-and-seek — all happened to have definite outcomes, and the definition generalised from this feature. But the feature was incidental to the paradigm cases rather than essential to the concept of a game. What the background cases truly share is not the presence of a winning condition but the presence of a structured, voluntary, rule-governed activity that engages the participants' imagination, skill, or both. Make-believe satisfies this deeper description even though it lacks a winning condition.
passage:
Chess, football, poker, and hide-and-seek are all games, and they share certain features: each is governed by rules, each has a definite outcome, and each is freely undertaken. From these cases one might extract the definition: an activity is a game if and only if it is governed by rules, has a definite outcome constituting winning or losing, and is freely undertaken.

Yet this definition, though it accommodates the paradigm cases, excludes activities that are no less clearly games. Consider children playing make-believe — pretending to be pirates on a desert island. The activity is governed by rules: the children negotiate the terms of the fiction, and a child who violates the agreed-upon premises ('You can't fly — pirates don't fly!') is corrected. The activity is freely undertaken; no child is compelled to play. But there is no definite outcome. No one wins, no one loses; the play unfolds without any terminal condition and continues until the participants lose interest or are summoned to tea. The second condition fails entirely. Yet make-believe is as plainly a game as any activity in childhood — indeed, developmental psychologists regard it as among the most important forms of play. The background cases all happened to involve competition and determinate outcomes, and the definition elevated this coincidental feature to a necessary condition. But the essential character of a game lies not in the presence of a winning condition but in the structured, voluntary, rule-governed engagement of the participants' capacities — whether those capacities be strategic, physical, imaginative, or some combination of the three. Make-believe engages the imagination within a rule-governed structure, and that is sufficient to make it a game.

background_cases: A hammer is a tool: it is a physical object, deliberately designed and manufactured for the purpose of driving nails, and it amplifies the user's ability to exert concentrated force on a small area.

A pair of scissors is a tool: it is a physical object, manufactured for the purpose of cutting, and it amplifies the user's ability to sever materials by concentrating force along two converging blades.

A lever is a tool: it is a physical object — a rigid bar resting on a fulcrum — used for the purpose of lifting heavy loads, and it amplifies the user's ability to exert force by trading distance for mechanical advantage.
A needle is a tool: it is a physical object, manufactured for the purpose of sewing, and it amplifies the user's ability to pass thread through fabric by concentrating effort at a fine point.
definition: An object O is a tool if and only if (i) O is a physical object, (ii) O was deliberately designed for a specific practical purpose, and (iii) O amplifies or extends a physical capacity of the user.
conditions: O is a physical object
O was deliberately designed for a specific practical purpose
O amplifies or extends a physical capacity of the user
counterexample: Consider a flat stone picked up from a riverbed and used to crack open a walnut. The stone is a physical object. It amplifies the user's ability to apply concentrated force — the user's bare hand could not crack the shell, but the stone can. But the stone was not deliberately designed for any purpose; it is a natural object, shaped by erosion, and no human hand or intention played any role in its formation. The second condition fails. Yet the stone, in this context, is functioning as a tool. It is being used as a tool, and the distinction between a designed tool and an improvised tool does not mark a distinction between tools and non-tools.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the hammer, the scissors, the lever, the needle — were all manufactured artefacts, and the definition generalised from this to require deliberate design. But the design requirement was incidental: what made each of these objects a tool was not the fact that someone had designed it but the fact that someone was using it to extend a physical capacity for a practical purpose. The stone, though undesigned, serves precisely this function. The definition has confused the typical origin of tools with the essence of toolhood.
passage:
The paradigmatic tools — the hammer, the scissors, the lever, the needle — are all physical objects that were deliberately designed for specific practical purposes and that amplify or extend the physical capacities of their users. From these cases one might extract the definition: an object is a tool if and only if it is a physical object, was deliberately designed for a practical purpose, and amplifies a physical capacity.

Yet this definition, abstracted from manufactured artefacts, proves too narrow. Consider a flat stone picked up from a riverbed and used to crack open a walnut. The stone is a physical object. It amplifies the user's capacity to apply concentrated force — the user's bare fist cannot crack the shell, but a sharp blow with the stone can. Yet the stone was not designed for any purpose; it is a natural object, the product of geological processes, and no human intention played any part in shaping it. The second condition fails. But the stone is functioning as a tool. The person who wields it is using it to extend a physical capacity — the ability to crack hard shells — for a practical purpose, and if asked what they were doing, they would say they were using a stone as a tool. The background cases were all designed artefacts, and the definition assumed that design was essential. But what made the hammer, the scissors, the lever, and the needle into tools was not the fact of their having been designed but the fact of their being used — employed by a human agent to amplify a capacity for a practical end. The stone, though shaped by erosion rather than by intention, serves this function just as effectively. The definition has mistaken the characteristic origin of tools for their defining feature.

background_cases: A friend promises to collect you from the railway station at six o'clock: the promise is made face to face, in a private conversation, and is understood by both parties as creating an obligation. If the friend fails to appear, you are entitled to feel aggrieved.

A builder promises to complete the extension by September: the promise is made in a professional context, perhaps confirmed in writing, and creates an expectation on which you rely in planning your affairs. If the builder defaults, you have grounds for complaint.

A parent promises a child an outing to the seaside on Saturday: the promise creates a specific expectation in the child, and the parent recognises that breaking it will cause disappointment and damage the child's trust.
A colleague promises to keep a confidence: the promise creates an obligation of secrecy, and the colleague recognises that disclosure would constitute a betrayal.
definition: An utterance U constitutes a promise if and only if (i) U expresses the speaker's commitment to perform a specific future action, (ii) U is addressed to a particular hearer who understands U as a commitment, and (iii) U is made in circumstances in which the speaker could reasonably be expected to fulfil the commitment.
conditions: U expresses a commitment to a specific future action
U is addressed to a particular hearer who understands it as a commitment
U is made in circumstances in which fulfilment is reasonably expected
counterexample: Consider a political candidate who, at a rally before thousands of supporters, declares: 'I promise to eliminate poverty within my first term.' The statement expresses a commitment to a specific future action. It is addressed to an audience that understands it as a commitment — the supporters cheer, and the promise is reported in newspapers the following day. But the circumstances are such that no reasonable person could expect the candidate to fulfil the commitment; the elimination of poverty within a single political term is beyond the power of any individual officeholder. The third condition fails. Yet the candidate has made a promise. It may be a rash promise, an unrealistic promise, even a cynical promise, but it is a promise nonetheless — and the candidate will be held to account for it, precisely because it was made in the form of a promise.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the friend at the station, the builder, the parent, the colleague — all involved promises that could reasonably be fulfilled, and the definition generalised from this to require reasonable expectation of fulfilment. But this feature was a mark of good promises, not of promises as such. What constituted each utterance as a promise was the public act of commitment and the hearer's understanding of it, not the likelihood of fulfilment. The definition confuses the conditions under which a promise is prudent with the conditions under which an utterance counts as a promise at all.
passage:
The most familiar promises — a friend's assurance to meet you at the station, a builder's commitment to finish on time, a parent's pledge to take a child to the seaside, a colleague's undertaking to keep a confidence — share certain features. Each involves a speaker expressing a commitment to a specific future action, addressed to a particular hearer who understands it as binding, and made in circumstances in which fulfilment is reasonably expected. From these cases one might extract the definition: an utterance is a promise if and only if it expresses a commitment to a future action, is understood as such by the hearer, and is made in circumstances in which fulfilment is reasonably expected.

Yet this definition, shaped by the paradigm of everyday personal promises, proves too restrictive when applied to the wider range of promissory utterances. Consider a political candidate who declares before thousands of supporters: 'I promise to eliminate poverty within my first term.' The utterance expresses a commitment. The audience understands it as a commitment — they applaud, and the newspapers report it as a promise the following morning. But no reasonable person expects the candidate to eliminate poverty within a single term; the task is beyond the capacity of any individual officeholder. The third condition fails. Yet the candidate has plainly made a promise — a reckless and perhaps cynical one, but a promise all the same. The candidate will be criticised for breaking it, and the criticism will take the form 'You promised.' The background cases were all instances in which the promise was within the promisor's power to fulfil, and the definition elevated this to a necessary condition. But what makes an utterance a promise is the public act of commitment and the hearer's recognition of it, not the probability of fulfilment. The definition has confused the prudential conditions under which a promise is well-made with the constitutive conditions under which an utterance counts as a promise at all.

background_cases: A motor car is a vehicle: it is a physical conveyance, designed to transport persons along roads, propelled by an internal combustion engine, and operated by a driver who steers it.
A horse-drawn carriage is a vehicle: it is a physical conveyance, designed to transport persons along roads, propelled by horses, and directed by a coachman.

A bicycle is a vehicle: it is a physical conveyance, designed to transport a person along roads and paths, propelled by the rider's own muscular effort through a pedal mechanism, and steered by the rider.

A railway locomotive is a vehicle: it is a physical conveyance, designed to transport persons and goods along rail tracks, propelled by steam, diesel, or electrical power, and operated by an engineer.
definition: An object O is a vehicle if and only if (i) O is a physical conveyance designed to transport persons or goods, (ii) O moves along a surface — a road, a track, a path, or the like, and (iii) O is operated by a human being who directs its movement.
conditions: O is a physical conveyance for transporting persons or goods
O moves along a surface
O is operated by a human who directs its movement
counterexample: Consider a hot-air balloon of the sort used for aerial excursions. A hot-air balloon is a physical conveyance designed to transport persons. It is operated — in the limited sense of controlling altitude and, to some degree, lateral movement — by a human pilot who manages the burner and the vent. But it does not move along a surface; it floats through the air, unsupported by any road, track, or path. The second condition fails. Yet a hot-air balloon is recognised in law, in common usage, and in the history of transport as a vehicle — it was, indeed, the first vehicle to carry human beings into the sky, and the word 'vehicle' was applied to it from the moment of its invention.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the motor car, the carriage, the bicycle, the locomotive — all move along surfaces, and the definition generalised from this to require surface travel. But this was an accidental feature of the paradigm cases, not a defining feature of vehicles as such. What made each of these conveyances a vehicle was its capacity to transport persons or goods from one place to another under human direction — not the medium through which it moved. The hot-air balloon transports persons through the air under human direction, and this is sufficient. The definition has mistaken the medium of the familiar cases for a necessary condition of vehiclehood.
passage:
The familiar vehicles — motor cars, carriages, bicycles, locomotives — share a common pattern. Each is a physical conveyance designed to transport persons or goods; each moves along a surface; each is operated by a human who directs its movement. From these cases one might extract the definition: an object is a vehicle if and only if it is a physical conveyance for transporting persons or goods, moves along a surface, and is directed by a human operator.

Yet this definition, abstracted from ground-based transport, proves too narrow when one considers the full range of conveyances that the concept of 'vehicle' encompasses. Consider a hot-air balloon. A balloon is a physical conveyance designed to transport persons — the Montgolfier brothers built theirs for precisely this purpose. It is operated by a human pilot who controls altitude by managing the burner and the vent and who can, within limits, influence lateral movement by ascending or descending to catch different wind currents. But a balloon does not move along a surface. It floats freely through the air, supported by buoyancy, in contact with no road, track, or path. The second condition fails. Yet a hot-air balloon is a vehicle by any ordinary or legal standard; it has been classified as such since the eighteenth century, and the regulations governing its operation are found in the same body of law that governs other vehicles. The background cases — the car, the carriage, the bicycle, the locomotive — all happened to travel along surfaces, and the definition took this shared feature to be essential. But what was essential in each case was the capacity to transport persons or goods from one place to another under human direction, regardless of the medium through which the transport occurs. The balloon satisfies this deeper description, even though it satisfies it in the air rather than on the ground.

background_cases: Chess is a paradigmatic game: it is governed by precise rules, the participants engage voluntarily, and there is a clear objective — checkmate — by which the game is won or lost. The activity is self-contained; the value of playing chess lies in the playing itself, not in any external product.

Football is a paradigmatic game: rules define the pitch, the number of players, and the conditions of victory. Participation is voluntary, and the activity is valued for its own sake — the enjoyment, the exercise, the drama — rather than for the production of any external good.

Bridge is a paradigmatic game: the rules of bidding and play are elaborate and precise, the participants choose to play, and the satisfaction lies in the exercise of skill and the enjoyment of the activity, not in anything the activity produces.
definition: An activity A is a game if and only if (i) A is governed by constitutive rules that define the activity and determine what counts as a permissible move, (ii) A is entered into voluntarily by the participants, and (iii) A has a determinate goal — a condition of winning — that the participants aim to achieve.
conditions: A is governed by constitutive rules
A is entered into voluntarily
A has a determinate goal or winning condition
counterexample: Consider the activity of free-form imaginative play among young children — making up stories, pretending to be pirates, inventing a world. The children participate voluntarily. There are constitutive norms of a sort — 'You be the pirate and I'll be the captain' — that shape the activity. But there is no determinate goal, no condition of winning. The play has no end-point, no score, no victor. It continues until the children lose interest or are called to supper. The third condition is not met. Yet children and adults alike unhesitatingly call this activity a game: 'Let's play a game of make-believe.'
abductive_insight: The background cases — chess, football, bridge — all possess determinate winning conditions that structure the activity and provide its characteristic tension. The definition abstracts from these cases the requirement of a determinate goal. But children's imaginative play reveals that an activity may possess rules, voluntariness, and every other mark of a game without possessing a winning condition. The definition mistakes the competitive structure of the paradigm cases for a necessary condition, when many games are constituted by the sheer pleasure of structured play without any aim beyond the playing itself.
passage:
Chess, football, and bridge are the cases to which one instinctively turns when analysing the concept of a game, and each exhibits a determinate winning condition that provides the activity with its characteristic structure and suspense. In chess, the aim is checkmate; in football, the aim is to score more goals than the opposing side; in bridge, the aim is to fulfil one's contract. The winning condition shapes every decision within the game, and much of the pleasure of playing derives from the pursuit of this goal under the constraints imposed by the rules.

One might distil from these cases the following definition: an activity is a game if it is governed by constitutive rules, entered into voluntarily, and directed toward a determinate goal or winning condition. This captures the paradigm cases admirably. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of the definition is furnished by the free-form imaginative play of young children. When children agree to play pirates — one as captain, another as navigator, a third as stowaway — they engage in an activity that is voluntary and governed by constitutive norms (you cannot be the captain and the stowaway at the same time; the ship must be imagined as having a deck and a hold). But there is no winning condition. No one wins at playing pirates. There is no score, no checkmate, no final whistle. The play continues until interest wanes or external circumstances intervene. Yet children call this activity a game without hesitation, and ordinary usage endorses the classification. The definition, built from cases in which a competitive structure was present, mistakes that structure for a necessary feature. What the background cases all possessed but the children's play lacks is a determinate goal; what the children's play nevertheless possesses is every other feature we associate with games — rules, voluntariness, and the distinctive pleasure of structured, imaginative activity.

background_cases: A Rembrandt self-portrait is a work of art: it was made by an artist working within the tradition of European painting, it was intended for aesthetic contemplation, and it is displayed in galleries where it is received as art by audiences conversant with that tradition.

A Beethoven string quartet is a work of art: it was composed by a musician working within the tradition of Western classical music, it was intended for aesthetic appreciation, and it is performed in concert halls where it is received as art.

A Keats ode is a work of art: it was written by a poet working within the English literary tradition, it was intended for contemplation of its formal and expressive qualities, and it is read and studied as a work of literature.
definition: An object O is a work of art if and only if (i) O is an artefact — a product of intentional human making, (ii) O is made by a person working within a recognised artistic tradition, and (iii) O is intended by its maker to be appreciated for its aesthetic or expressive qualities.
conditions: O is an artefact
O is made within a recognised artistic tradition
O is intended for aesthetic or expressive appreciation
counterexample: Consider the cave paintings at Lascaux, created approximately seventeen thousand years ago. These paintings are artefacts — they were intentionally made by human hands. They display extraordinary aesthetic and expressive qualities — the bison are rendered with vivid dynamism, the horses with graceful economy of line. Yet they were not made within a 'recognised artistic tradition' in any sense available to us, for we have no understanding of the social or cultural framework within which the painters worked. We do not know whether the paintings were made for aesthetic contemplation, ritual purposes, or some function entirely alien to our categories. The second and third conditions are indeterminate, yet the paintings are among the most celebrated works of art in human history.
abductive_insight: The background cases — Rembrandt, Beethoven, Keats — are all embedded in well-documented artistic traditions with clear intentional structures. The definition captures this embeddedness as a necessary condition. But the Lascaux paintings predate any tradition we can identify and were produced with intentions we cannot recover. The definition cannot accommodate art that precedes or falls outside documented traditions, revealing that the concept of art may not depend upon the institutional and intentional framework that the familiar cases make salient, but upon qualities of the object itself that compel aesthetic response regardless of context.
passage:
The works most confidently identified as art tend to be embedded in recognisable traditions. A Rembrandt self-portrait was painted within the European tradition of portraiture; its maker intended it for aesthetic contemplation; and audiences received it as such. A Beethoven quartet was composed within the tradition of Western classical music and performed for audiences prepared to appreciate its formal and expressive qualities. A Keats ode was written within the tradition of English Romantic poetry and intended for the kind of attentive reading that tradition sanctions. In each case, the work is situated within a tradition that provides the framework for its creation and reception.

One might codify this by defining a work of art as an artefact made within a recognised artistic tradition and intended for aesthetic or expressive appreciation. This captures the background cases neatly. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of the definition is provided by the cave paintings at Lascaux. These paintings, created some seventeen thousand years ago, are indisputably artefacts — they were made by human hands with deliberation and skill. They display aesthetic qualities of a high order: the bison charge across the cave walls with a vitality that moves the modern viewer; the horses are rendered with an economy of line that many a trained draughtsman might envy. But we cannot say that they were made within a recognised artistic tradition, for we possess no understanding of the cultural framework in which the painters operated. We do not know whether the paintings served a ritual function, a didactic purpose, or something we have no category for. The second and third conditions of the definition are radically indeterminate. Yet the Lascaux paintings are among the most celebrated works of art in the world. The definition, fashioned from cases in which tradition and intention are well documented, cannot accommodate art that precedes all known traditions and whose purposes are irrecoverable.

background_cases: A merchant who tells a buyer that a counterfeit watch is genuine, knowing it to be false and intending the buyer to believe it, tells a lie. The falsehood is asserted — stated as a matter of fact — and the hearer is expected to accept it as such.

A witness who tells a court that she was not present at the scene, knowing that she was present and intending the court to believe her denial, tells a lie. The falsehood is asserted under oath and presented as testimony.

A student who tells his tutor that his essay was submitted on time, knowing it was late and intending the tutor to accept the claim, tells a lie. The falsehood is asserted in the context of an institutional expectation of truthfulness.
definition: A speaker A tells a lie if and only if (i) A makes a statement S that A believes to be false, (ii) A intends the hearer to believe S, and (iii) A asserts S — that is, A presents S as a sincere expression of what A takes to be the case.
conditions: A makes a statement A believes to be false
A intends the hearer to believe the statement
A asserts the statement as a sincere expression
counterexample: Consider a novelist who writes, in the voice of a narrator, that the protagonist crossed the Channel on a stormy night. The novelist believes this to be false — there is no protagonist and no crossing. She intends the reader to entertain the proposition — to imagine it vividly and perhaps to be moved by it. And the sentence is presented in declarative form. Yet the novelist is not lying. The context of fiction signals that the statement is not an assertion in the relevant sense; the reader understands that the conventions of fiction are in play and does not take the novelist to be reporting what she believes to be true.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the merchant, the witness, the student — all involve assertions made in contexts where the hearer is entitled to expect sincerity: commercial transactions, legal testimony, academic submission. The definition captures the assertoric character of these cases but does not attend to the contextual conditions under which an utterance counts as a genuine assertion rather than a fiction, a jest, or a performance. The novel case shows that the same propositional content, believed false and intended to be entertained by a hearer, fails to be a lie when the context is one in which sincerity is not expected, because the utterance is not a genuine assertion despite its declarative form.
passage:
The clearest cases of lying share a contextual feature that is easily overlooked. When a merchant tells a buyer that a counterfeit watch is genuine, the falsehood is embedded in a commercial transaction where the buyer is entitled to expect sincerity. When a witness denies under oath what she knows to be true, the falsehood is embedded in a legal proceeding where truthfulness is formally required. When a student misrepresents the submission date of an essay, the falsehood is embedded in an academic context where honest reporting is presupposed. In each case, the speaker exploits a context of expected sincerity to produce a false belief in the hearer.

One might codify these observations by defining a lie as a statement believed false by the speaker, intended to produce belief in the hearer, and presented as a sincere assertion. This definition captures the assertoric and deceptive dimensions of the paradigm cases. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency is furnished by the activity of novel-writing. A novelist writes: 'He crossed the Channel on a night of violent storm, the deck pitching beneath him.' The novelist believes this to be false — there is no such person and no such crossing. She intends the reader to entertain the proposition — to imagine the scene, to feel the pitch of the deck. And the sentence has the grammatical form of an assertion. But the novelist is not lying. The context of fiction — the title page, the publisher's imprint, the conventions of narrative — signals to the reader that the sincerity condition is suspended. The reader does not take the novelist to be reporting what she believes. The background cases all occurred in contexts where sincerity was expected; the novel exists in a context where it is not. The definition captures the form of assertion but not the contextual conditions under which an utterance counts as a genuine assertion, and without that contextual dimension, fiction is indistinguishable from falsehood.

background_cases: When a borrower promises a lender to repay a debt by a certain date, the promise creates an obligation because the lender relies upon it — she arranges her finances on the assumption that the money will be returned. The promise is performatively effective because its acceptance by the hearer generates legitimate expectations.

When a friend promises to attend another's wedding, the promise creates an obligation because the host relies upon it — she arranges seating and catering on the assumption of attendance. The promisee's reliance gives the promise its binding force.

When a contractor promises to complete renovations by a certain date, the promise creates an obligation because the homeowner relies upon it — she arranges to vacate and return on the basis of the promised date.
definition: An utterance U by a speaker A constitutes a binding promise if and only if (i) U expresses A's undertaking to perform a specific future action, (ii) U is communicated to a hearer B, and (iii) B accepts U and forms an expectation that A will perform the action.
conditions: U expresses A's undertaking to perform a future action
U is communicated to a hearer B
B accepts U and forms an expectation
counterexample: Consider a person who, in solitude and with the utmost sincerity, promises herself that she will give up smoking. The promise expresses an undertaking to perform a specific future action — abstaining from tobacco. It is not communicated to any hearer. Yet the person feels genuinely bound by her resolution; she regards the failure to keep it as a kind of self-betrayal, not as a mere change of plan. The promise exerts a normative force upon her conduct even in the absence of any hearer, any acceptance, and any external expectation.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the loan, the wedding, the renovation — all involve promises whose binding force derives from the reliance of a hearer. The definition captures this reliance structure. But the case of a promise to oneself shows that a promise may generate a sense of obligation in the absence of any hearer or external reliance. The definition mistakes the interpersonal structure of the paradigm cases for a necessary condition of promissory obligation, when the phenomenon of self-promising suggests that the normative force of a promise may derive, in part, from the integrity of the promisor rather than solely from the expectations of the promisee.
passage:
The promises that most clearly generate binding obligations share a reliance structure that invites generalisation. When a borrower promises to repay a debt, the lender relies on the promise and adjusts her affairs accordingly. When a friend promises to attend a wedding, the host relies on the promise and makes practical arrangements. When a contractor promises to meet a deadline, the homeowner relies on the promise and plans her life around it. In each case, the promisee's reliance gives the promise its normative bite: to break the promise is to betray a reliance that one has invited.

One might codify this by defining a binding promise as an undertaking communicated to a hearer who accepts it and forms an expectation.
