This captures the interpersonal structure of the background cases. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency is the promise a person makes to herself. Consider a woman who, alone in her study, resolves with the utmost sincerity to give up smoking. She frames the resolution explicitly as a promise — 'I promise myself I shall not smoke again' — and she regards the failure to keep it not as a mere change of mind but as a kind of self-betrayal. The promise expresses an undertaking to perform a specific future action. But it is not communicated to any hearer; no one accepts it; no external expectation is formed. Yet it exerts a genuine normative force. The woman feels bound, and her sense of obligation is not an illusion. The definition, built from cases in which the promisee's reliance generates the obligation, cannot accommodate promises whose force derives from the promisor's own integrity. What the background cases all possessed — and what the definition treats as essential — is an interpersonal reliance structure; but the self-promise case suggests that the normative force of promising may have a deeper source in the commitments of the promisor herself.

background_cases: A father gives his daughter a bicycle for her birthday. The transfer is voluntary, motivated by affection, and the father expects nothing in return. The bicycle becomes the daughter's property; she may use it, lend it, or give it away as she pleases. The gift is complete upon delivery, and the father relinquishes all claim to the bicycle.

A friend gives another friend a book that he enjoyed, hoping the recipient will enjoy it as well. The transfer is voluntary, prompted by the wish to share a pleasure, and no return is expected. The book becomes the recipient's property, and the giver has no further claim upon it.

A philanthropist donates a large sum of money to a hospital. The transfer is voluntary, motivated by a concern for the public good, and no personal return is expected. The money becomes the hospital's to use as it sees fit, and the donor relinquishes control over it.

A grandmother gives her grandson a pocket watch that belonged to his grandfather. The transfer is voluntary, motivated by affection and a wish to preserve family continuity, and no return is expected. The watch becomes the grandson's property.
definition: A transfer of an object O from a person A to a person B constitutes a gift if and only if (i) A voluntarily relinquishes possession and all claims to O, (ii) B is free to use, dispose of, or further transfer O without restriction, and (iii) A expects no material return from B.
conditions: A voluntarily relinquishes possession and all claims to O
B is free to use, dispose of, or transfer O without restriction
A expects no material return
counterexample: Consider a person who gives a rare painting to a museum on the condition that it be displayed permanently in a particular gallery, never lent to other institutions, and never sold. The donor voluntarily relinquishes possession and expects no material return. The transfer is universally described as a gift — a generous gift, indeed. But the recipient is not free to use, dispose of, or further transfer the painting without restriction. The conditions attached to the gift are severe: the museum may not move the painting, may not lend it, and may not sell it even if maintaining it becomes burdensome. The second condition of the definition fails, yet the transfer is a gift.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involve unconditional transfers: the father places no conditions on how the daughter uses the bicycle, the friend places no conditions on the book, the philanthropist places no conditions on the money. The definition captures this unconditionality as a necessary feature. But many gifts — especially valuable or culturally significant ones — are given with conditions attached, and the practice of conditional gift-giving is ancient and widespread. What makes a transfer a gift is not the absence of conditions but the absence of material return; conditions constrain the use of the gift but do not transform it into a transaction.
passage:
When one considers the gifts that seem most paradigmatic, a common feature stands out: the gift is given freely and received without restriction. A father gives his daughter a bicycle, and the bicycle is hers to ride, to lend, to give away. A friend gives a book, and the book is the recipient's to read, to shelve, to pass along. A philanthropist donates money to a hospital, and the hospital is free to spend it as it judges best. A grandmother gives a pocket watch, and the grandson may wear it, display it, or put it in a drawer. In each case, the transfer is voluntary, no return is expected, and the recipient acquires full and unrestricted control over the gift.

One might codify these observations by defining a gift as a voluntary transfer in which the giver relinquishes all claims, the recipient is free to use or dispose of the object without restriction, and no material return is expected. This definition captures the background cases admirably. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency is the conditional gift to a cultural institution. Consider a collector who gives a rare painting to a museum, stipulating that the painting must be displayed permanently in a particular gallery, must never be lent to another institution, and must never be sold. The donor relinquishes possession voluntarily and expects no material return. The transfer is universally described as a gift — by the donor, by the museum, by the press. Yet the recipient is not free to use the painting without restriction. The conditions are detailed and binding: the museum cannot move the painting to a different room, cannot lend it for an exhibition abroad, cannot sell it even if maintenance costs become prohibitive. The second condition of the definition fails. What the background cases all shared — the unconditional character of the transfer — was not a necessary feature of gifts but merely a characteristic of simple, personal gifts. In the world of philanthropy, of cultural donation, and of bequests, gifts with conditions are the norm rather than the exception, and the definition, by requiring unrestricted use, excludes a vast and important category of giving.

background_cases: A hammer is a tool: it was designed and manufactured for the purpose of driving nails, and its form — the weighted head, the handle — is shaped by that purpose. One uses a hammer because one has a task that requires it, and the hammer's design anticipates that task.

A saw is a tool: it was designed for the purpose of cutting wood, and its form — the toothed blade, the handle — is dictated by that purpose. The saw exists because the task of cutting wood exists, and the saw's design is a response to the requirements of that task.

A needle is a tool: it was designed for the purpose of sewing, and its form — the sharp point, the eye for thread — is adapted to that purpose. The needle's design is inseparable from the task it was made to perform.

A telescope is a tool: it was designed for the purpose of observing distant objects, and its form — the lenses, the tube — is shaped by that purpose. The telescope was created to extend the capacity of the human eye.
definition: An object O is a tool if and only if (i) O was designed or manufactured for a specific practical purpose, (ii) O's physical form is adapted to that purpose, and (iii) O is used in the performance of the purpose for which it was designed.
conditions: O was designed for a specific practical purpose
O's physical form is adapted to that purpose
O is used for the purpose for which it was designed
counterexample: Consider a flat stone picked up from a riverbed and used to crack open walnuts. The stone was not designed or manufactured for any purpose; it is a natural object, shaped by the river. Its physical form is not adapted to the purpose of cracking nuts — it happens to be flat and heavy enough to serve, but its flatness is an accident of erosion, not a design feature. And it is being used for a purpose that no designer intended. All three conditions fail. Yet the person using the stone to crack walnuts is using a tool — a crude tool, certainly, but a tool nonetheless, and the distinction between this improvised tool and a manufactured nutcracker is one of degree rather than of kind.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the hammer, the saw, the needle, the telescope — are all manufactured artefacts whose forms are dictated by their intended purposes. The definition captures the designed, purpose-built character of these objects but cannot accommodate the improvised tools that human beings constantly fashion from found objects. What makes something a tool is not its design history but its functional role in an agent's purposive activity — the fact that it is taken up and used as a means to an end.
passage:
The objects most readily recognised as tools share a common origin: they were designed for a purpose, and their physical form reflects that purpose. The hammer's weighted head is shaped for driving nails. The saw's toothed blade is shaped for cutting wood. The needle's sharp point and threaded eye are shaped for sewing. The telescope's lenses and tube are shaped for observing distant objects. In each case, the tool was brought into existence by the requirements of a task, and its form is a response to those requirements.

One might codify these observations by defining a tool as an object designed for a specific practical purpose, whose form is adapted to that purpose, and which is used for the purpose for which it was designed. This definition captures the paradigm cases without difficulty. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency is the improvised tool — the object pressed into service from the natural world. Consider a person walking in a wood who finds a flat stone by a riverbed and uses it to crack open walnuts. The stone was not designed for any purpose. Its form — flat, smooth, of a convenient weight — is the product of geological processes, not of human intention. It is being used for a purpose that no one intended when the stone was formed. All three conditions of the definition fail. Yet the person is using a tool. The stone, in the moment of its use, functions as a nutcracker: it amplifies the force of the hand and enables a task that bare fingers could not accomplish. One might observe that the background cases led us to identify tool-hood with design, but design is characteristic only of manufactured tools. The improvised tools of daily life — the stick used as a lever, the stone used as a hammer, the folded paper used as a funnel — are tools by virtue of their functional role in purposive activity, not by virtue of any design. The definition has mistaken the origin of a tool for its essence, when what makes an object a tool is not how it came to exist but how it is taken up and used.

background_cases: A builder promises a client that the house will be finished by December. The promise is made to a specific person, concerns a definite future action, and creates an expectation that the builder acknowledges and accepts. If the builder fails to deliver, the client has grounds for complaint — the builder bound himself by his word.

A friend promises to return a borrowed book by Friday. The promise is addressed to a specific person, concerns a definite action, and creates an expectation. The friend has put himself under an obligation, and failure to return the book on time is a breach of that obligation.

A doctor promises a patient that the operation will be performed with the utmost care. The promise is made to a specific person, concerns a definite standard of conduct, and creates a legitimate expectation. The patient relies upon the doctor's word, and the doctor is bound by it.
definition: An utterance U constitutes a promise if and only if (i) U commits the speaker to a specific future action or standard of conduct, (ii) U is made sincerely — that is, the speaker intends at the time of utterance to fulfil the commitment, and (iii) the person to whom U is addressed relies upon the commitment.
conditions: U commits the speaker to a specific future action or standard of conduct
U is made sincerely — the speaker intends to fulfil the commitment
The addressee relies upon the commitment
counterexample: Consider a kidnapper who promises a captive that no harm will come to him if he cooperates. The captive cooperates, relying entirely upon the kidnapper's word. The kidnapper's utterance commits him to a specific future standard of conduct — not harming the captive. Let us suppose the kidnapper is sincere at the moment he speaks: he genuinely intends to release the captive unharmed. The captive relies upon the commitment. All three conditions are met. Yet we should hesitate to say that the kidnapper has made a genuine promise, for the context — the kidnapper has created the very peril from which he offers to refrain — vitiates the moral force that we associate with promising. The captive's reliance is not the free reliance of one who trusts another's word but the desperate reliance of one who has no alternative.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the builder, the friend, the doctor — all involve promises made in circumstances of mutual freedom, where the promisee's reliance is a genuine choice based on trust. The definition captures the elements of commitment, sincerity, and reliance but takes no account of the circumstances in which the promise is made. When the promisor has created the very conditions that compel reliance — as the kidnapper creates the peril that makes cooperation the captive's only option — the moral force of the promise is undermined in a way the definition cannot detect.
passage:
The promises that function most straightforwardly in social life share a common structure that invites generalisation. A builder promises to finish a house by a certain date, and the client relies upon this commitment in planning his affairs. A friend promises to return a book, and the lender relies upon the friend's word. A doctor promises to operate with care, and the patient submits to the procedure in reliance upon this assurance. In each case, the speaker commits himself to a definite course of action, speaks sincerely, and the hearer relies upon the commitment.

One might codify these cases by defining a promise as an utterance that commits the speaker to a future action, is made sincerely, and is relied upon by the addressee. The definition captures the background cases admirably. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency is the promise made under conditions of duress — specifically, the promise of a kidnapper to his captive. Suppose a kidnapper tells his captive: 'Cooperate and I give you my word that you will not be harmed.' Let us assume the kidnapper is sincere at the time of speaking — he genuinely intends to release the captive unharmed if the captive cooperates. The captive cooperates, relying entirely upon the kidnapper's word. The utterance commits the speaker to a specific standard of conduct. The speaker is sincere. The addressee relies upon the commitment. All three conditions of the definition are met. But we should hesitate to say that a genuine promise has been made — or, at least, that this promise carries the moral weight that promises characteristically carry. For the kidnapper has himself created the very circumstances that compel the captive's reliance. The captive does not rely upon the kidnapper's word as a free person relies upon the word of a trustworthy friend; he relies upon it because he has no alternative, because the kidnapper has placed him in a situation where cooperation is his only rational course. The background cases all involved promises made in conditions of mutual freedom, where reliance was a genuine choice. The definition captures the formal elements of promising but omits the requirement that the promise be made in circumstances that permit the addressee's reliance to be freely given rather than coerced.

background_cases: A farmer owns the field he has cleared and cultivated: he transformed unused land into productive soil through his own physical effort, and the product of that effort — the cultivated field — is his. His ownership is grounded in the labour he has invested.
A potter owns the vases she has made: she took raw clay and, through skill and effort, shaped it into beautiful and useful objects. The vases are hers because they are the product of her labour.
A fisherman owns the fish he has caught: he went out upon the sea, expended effort and took risk, and the fish he brought back are his by right of his exertion.
A woodcutter owns the timber he has felled: he entered the forest, selected trees, and by his own strength brought them down and carried them out. The timber is his because his labour produced it.
definition: A person A owns an object O if and only if (i) A has mixed their labour with O — that is, A has expended effort to create, extract, or transform O, (ii) O was not previously owned by another person, and (iii) A's appropriation of O does not leave others worse off than they were before.
conditions: A has mixed labour with O
O was not previously owned by another
A's appropriation does not leave others worse off
counterexample: Consider a person who walks along a beach and finds a large, beautiful gemstone washed up by the tide. He picks it up and puts it in his pocket. He has not mixed any labour with the gemstone beyond the trivial act of bending down — he did not create it, extract it, or transform it. The gemstone was not previously owned. And his picking it up does not leave others worse off, for no one knew the gemstone was there. The first condition fails — no meaningful labour has been mixed — yet most people would say the finder owns the gemstone. He found it; it is his.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the farmer, the potter, the fisherman, the woodcutter — all involve substantial labour that transforms or extracts a resource. The definition captures this labour-based mode of acquisition but cannot accommodate the acquisition of objects by finding, where ownership arises not from labour but from the act of taking possession of an unowned thing. The concept of ownership is broader than the labour theory allows: one may come to own a thing not only by making it but also by finding it, receiving it, or being the first to claim it.
passage:
The acquisitions of property that seem most morally straightforward share a common feature: the owner has worked for what he owns. The farmer cleared the land and tilled the soil; the field is his. The potter shaped the clay; the vases are hers. The fisherman braved the sea; the fish are his. The woodcutter felled the trees; the timber is his. In each case, the owner's labour transformed or extracted something from the common stock of nature, and the product of that labour belongs to the labourer.

One might codify these cases by defining ownership as arising when a person mixes his labour with an unowned object, provided that the appropriation does not leave others worse off. This definition has a noble pedigree and captures the background cases with precision. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency is the simplest and most ancient mode of acquisition: finding. Consider a person walking along a deserted beach after a storm. A large gemstone, washed ashore by the tide, lies gleaming on the sand. He picks it up and puts it in his pocket. He has not mixed any labour with the gemstone in any meaningful sense. He did not create it, did not extract it from the earth, did not transform it. The act of bending down and picking up the stone is so minimal that it hardly qualifies as labour. The gemstone was not previously owned — it lay on a public beach, deposited by the sea. His taking it does not leave others worse off — no one knew it was there, and no one else's resources are diminished. Yet most people would say, without much reflection, that the gemstone is his. He found it; it belongs to him. The background cases all involved substantial labour — the clearing of land, the shaping of clay, the hauling of nets, the felling of trees. The definition made labour the foundation of ownership. But the case of the found gemstone suggests that ownership may also arise from the mere act of first appropriation — of taking possession of an unowned thing — without any admixture of labour beyond the act of grasping. The definition has elevated one important ground of ownership to the status of a necessary condition and has thereby excluded the equally ancient and equally intuitive ground of finding.

background_cases: Chess is a game: it is governed by constitutive rules that define legal moves and winning conditions, it involves competition between two players, and its outcome depends substantially on the skill and strategic thinking of the players.

Football is a game: it is governed by the Laws of the Game that define permissible actions and scoring, it involves competition between two teams, and its outcome depends on the physical skill, coordination, and tactical acumen of the players.

Tennis is a game: it is governed by rules that define legal serves, strokes, and scoring, it involves competition between two players or two pairs, and its outcome depends on the agility, precision, and mental discipline of the players.
definition: An activity A is a game if and only if (i) A is governed by constitutive rules that define what counts as a legal move and what counts as winning, (ii) A involves competition between two or more participants, and (iii) the outcome of A depends at least in part on the skill or effort of the participants.
conditions: A is governed by constitutive rules defining legal moves and winning conditions
A involves competition between two or more participants
The outcome depends at least in part on skill or effort
counterexample: Consider a child playing solitaire — the card game played alone. Solitaire is governed by constitutive rules that define which cards may be moved and what counts as winning. The outcome depends on the player's skill in sequencing her moves. But there is no competition between participants: the game is played by one person against the arrangement of the cards. The second condition fails. Yet solitaire is indisputably a game — it is called a game, it is sold as a game, it appears in every compendium of card games.
abductive_insight: The background cases — chess, football, tennis — are all competitive games involving two or more participants. The definition captures the competitive structure common to these paradigmatic cases but mistakes this competitive element for a necessary feature of games as such, overlooking the entire class of single-player games in which the challenge is posed not by an opponent but by the arrangement of the materials or by the structure of the puzzle.
passage:
The games that most readily come to mind when one considers the concept share a common structure. Chess is governed by constitutive rules, involves competition between two players, and rewards skill. Football has its laws, sets two teams against each other, and rewards physical and tactical prowess. Tennis defines its serves and volleys, pits one player or pair against another, and rewards agility and placement. In each case, rules govern the activity, competition animates it, and skill determines its outcome.

One might therefore define a game as a rule-governed activity involving competition between two or more participants, the outcome of which depends at least partly on skill or effort. This definition accommodates the background cases admirably. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when one turns to the solitary game. Consider solitaire, the card game played by one person alone. The game is governed by precise constitutive rules: certain cards may be placed upon certain others, and the game is won when all cards have been arranged in their proper sequences. The outcome depends on the player's skill in choosing which moves to make and in what order. But there is no competition — no opponent, no second player, no team. The player contends with the arrangement of the cards, not with another human being. The second condition of the definition fails. Yet solitaire is manifestly a game. It has been called a game for centuries; it is included in every collection of card games; no one who plays it doubts that she is playing a game. The background cases were all competitive; the definition mistook this competitive character for an essential feature. But games may be solitary, and the concept must be broad enough to accommodate the puzzle, the patience, and the single-player challenge alongside the match and the tournament.

background_cases: A man promises his wife that he will collect the children from school. He utters the words 'I promise' in her presence; she hears and understands; the promise creates a mutual expectation — she expects him to collect the children, and he recognises that she expects it. The promise is made, received, and understood by both parties.

A builder promises a client that the renovation will be completed by December. The promise is communicated in a letter, which the client reads and acknowledges. A mutual expectation is established: the client expects completion by December, and the builder recognises this expectation.

A friend promises to keep a secret. The promise is spoken aloud, heard by the confider, and accepted. Both parties understand that the confider expects silence and that the friend has undertaken to maintain it.
definition: An utterance U constitutes a promise if and only if (i) U is communicated by the promisor to the promisee, (ii) U creates an expectation in the promisee that the promisor will perform a specific future action, and (iii) the promisor recognises that U has created this expectation in the promisee.
conditions: U is communicated by the promisor to the promisee
U creates an expectation in the promisee that the promisor will perform a specific future action
The promisor recognises that U has created this expectation
counterexample: Consider a person who, standing alone at his mother's grave, promises her that he will care for his younger siblings. There is no living promisee to whom the utterance is communicated; the mother is dead and cannot form expectations. The first and second conditions fail. Yet the man regards his words as a genuine promise, feels bound by it with the deepest moral seriousness, and would consider himself to have broken faith if he neglected his siblings. It appears that a promise can be made even when there is no living recipient to hear it or form expectations upon it.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involve living, present promisees who hear the promise and form expectations. The definition captures this communicative structure — the exchange between promisor and promisee — but cannot accommodate the promissory declarations that people make to the dead, to the absent, or to themselves, where no living promisee is present to receive the communication or form an expectation, yet the moral force of the promise is no less real.
passage:
The promises that first come to mind when one reflects upon the concept share a distinctive communicative structure. A man promises his wife to collect the children: the words are spoken, she hears them, she forms an expectation, and he recognises that she has done so. A builder promises a client that the work will be finished by December: the promise is put in writing, the client reads it, an expectation is created and acknowledged. A friend promises to keep a secret: the words are uttered, the confider receives them, and both parties understand the commitment that has been undertaken. In each case, the promise is communicated to a living, present person who forms an expectation that the promisor recognises.

One might codify this pattern by defining a promise as an utterance communicated by the promisor to the promisee, which creates an expectation in the promisee, and which the promisor recognises as having created that expectation. The definition accommodates the background cases well. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when one considers the promise made at a graveside. A man stands alone before his mother's grave and promises her that he will care for his younger siblings. There is no living promisee to hear the words; the mother cannot form an expectation; the communicative exchange that the definition requires is absent. The first and second conditions both fail. Yet the man regards his words as a genuine promise — as binding as any promise he has ever made. He feels the weight of it; he organises his life around it; he would regard himself as having broken faith if he neglected his siblings. The background cases all involved living promisees, and the definition captured this bilateral communicative structure as essential. But the graveside promise, though unilateral and uncommunicated, possesses the moral force and binding character that we associate with promising. The definition mistakes the typical context of promising for its essential nature.

background_cases: A witness in court testifies that she was at home on the night of the crime, though she was in fact at the scene. She asserts a proposition she believes false, with the aim of creating a false impression in the minds of the jury. The assertion is made in a context — the courtroom — where truth-telling is expected and enforced by oath.

A child tells his mother that he has not eaten any biscuits, though crumbs are visible on his face. He asserts something he believes to be false, intending to create in his mother the false impression that the biscuit tin is undisturbed. The assertion is made in the context of a parent-child relationship where honesty is the operative expectation.

A politician tells a journalist that the government has no plans to raise taxes, though she has just attended a cabinet meeting at which a tax increase was approved. She asserts a proposition she believes false, intending the journalist and the public to form a false impression of the government's fiscal intentions.
definition: A statement S is a lie if and only if (i) the speaker asserts S, (ii) the speaker believes S to be false, and (iii) the speaker intends, by asserting S, to create a false impression in the audience.
conditions: The speaker asserts S
The speaker believes S to be false
The speaker intends to create a false impression in the audience
counterexample: Consider a guest at a dinner party who, upon being served an unappetising dish, says to the host: 'This is delicious.' The guest believes the statement to be false — the dish is bland and overcooked. She asserts it aloud. But she does not intend to create a false impression in the host's mind about the quality of the food. The host, she believes, is well aware that polite guests praise mediocre dishes, and that such praise is a social convention rather than a report of gastronomic fact. The guest's intention is not to deceive but to observe a social courtesy. All three conditions are, on the face of it, satisfied — she asserts what she believes false with the surface appearance of intending to produce a false impression. Yet one should hesitate to call this a lie; it is a conventional pleasantry, and both parties understand it as such.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the court testimony, the child's denial, the politician's misrepresentation — all occur in contexts where sincere assertion is the expected norm and where the audience has no reason to suspect that the speaker is being anything other than truthful. The definition captures the structure of deception that operates in these truth-expectant contexts but cannot distinguish genuine lies from conventional insincerites — polite fictions, social pleasantries, diplomatic courtesies — in which the audience is not truly deceived because both parties understand the assertion as a social performance rather than a report of fact.
passage:
The clearest cases of lying share a recognisable structure. The courtroom witness asserts what she believes false, intending the jury to form a mistaken view of the facts. The child denies eating the biscuits, intending his mother to believe the tin undisturbed. The politician misrepresents the government's plans, intending the public to form a false impression of fiscal policy. In each case, the speaker asserts a proposition she believes false, with the aim of creating in the audience an impression that does not correspond to the facts.

One might define a lie as an assertion the speaker believes false, made with the intention of creating a false impression. This definition accommodates the background cases well. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises in the domain of social convention. Consider a guest at a dinner party who, upon being served an indifferent dish, says to the host: 'This is delicious.' The guest believes the statement false — the dish is bland and poorly seasoned. She asserts it aloud. On the surface, she appears to intend the host to believe that the food is good. All three conditions seem to be satisfied. Yet one should hesitate to call this a lie. The guest's purpose is not deception but courtesy. She observes a social convention that both she and the host understand: polite guests praise the cooking, regardless of its quality, and hosts recognise such praise as conventional rather than sincere. The host is not truly deceived — she may even suspect that the food is not to the guest's taste — and the guest does not intend genuine deception. The background cases all occurred in contexts where sincere assertion was the operative norm: the courtroom, the family home, the public sphere. The definition captures the structure of deception in such contexts but cannot distinguish the genuine lie from the conventional insincerites that lubricate social life.

background_cases: A carpenter's hammer is a tool: it is a physical object used to drive nails, accomplishing a task that bare hands could not manage; it extends the carpenter's strength and precision beyond the limits of his body.

A magnifying glass is a tool: it is a physical object that enables a jeweller to examine fine details invisible to the unaided eye; it extends the jeweller's perceptual capacity beyond its natural limits.
A lever is a tool: it is a physical object that enables a labourer to move a boulder that her unaided strength could not shift; it amplifies force, extending the labourer's physical capacity.
definition: An object O is a tool if and only if (i) O is a physical object external to the user's body, (ii) O extends the user's capacities — strength, precision, or perception — beyond their natural limits, and (iii) the user employs O with the intention of accomplishing a specific task.
conditions: O is a physical object external to the user's body
O extends the user's capacities beyond their natural limits
The user employs O with the intention of accomplishing a specific task
counterexample: Consider a pair of eyeglasses worn by a short-sighted scholar. The glasses extend her perceptual capacity — her ability to see clearly — far beyond its unaided limits. She employs them with the intention of accomplishing a specific task: reading. But are eyeglasses external to her body in the relevant sense? She wears them continuously, on her face, as an intimate part of her daily existence. She sees through them as naturally as a normally sighted person sees through her own corneas. They function, for all practical purposes, as part of her perceptual apparatus rather than as an implement she picks up and puts down. The first condition, on a strict reading, is satisfied — the glasses are not literally part of her body. But the case exposes the arbitrariness of the boundary between tool and prosthetic, between external aid and integral extension of the self.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the hammer, the magnifying glass, the lever — are all objects that the user picks up, employs for a discrete task, and sets aside. They stand in a clear relation of externality to the body: the carpenter takes up his hammer and puts it down; the jeweller peers through the glass and then removes it from her eye. The definition captures this relation of discrete, task-specific employment but cannot accommodate objects that extend capacities in a continuous, intimate, and quasi-bodily manner, blurring the boundary between the tool as external instrument and the prosthetic as incorporated extension of the self.
passage:
The objects that most readily come to mind as tools share a characteristic relation to the body of the user. The carpenter's hammer is picked up, used to drive nails, and set down. The jeweller's magnifying glass is held to the eye, used to examine a stone, and replaced in its case. The labourer's lever is positioned beneath a boulder, used to shift it, and discarded. Each is a physical object external to the body, employed with specific intention, extending the user's capacities beyond their natural limits. The relation between user and tool is one of discrete, task-specific employment: the tool is taken up for a purpose and laid aside when the purpose is fulfilled.

One might codify this pattern by defining a tool as a physical object external to the user's body that extends the user's capacities and is employed with the intention of accomplishing a specific task. The definition accommodates the background cases admirably. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when one considers the eyeglasses of the short-sighted scholar. Her spectacles extend her perceptual capacity far beyond its unaided limits — without them she cannot read, cannot recognise faces, cannot navigate her environment with confidence. She wears them with the intention of accomplishing specific tasks: reading, lecturing, walking through the city. And they are, strictly speaking, external to her body. But the glasses are not employed in the manner of the hammer or the magnifying glass. She does not pick them up for a task and set them aside; she wears them from morning to night, as continuously and as intimately as her own skin. They function not as an instrument she uses but as an extension of her perceptual apparatus. The background cases all involved discrete, intermittent employment — tools taken up and put down. The definition captures this pattern but cannot accommodate objects that extend capacities continuously and intimately, occupying a borderland between the external tool and the incorporated prosthetic.

background_cases: Chess is a game involving two players, explicit rules governing the movement of pieces, a definite criterion of victory, and no element of physical skill. The game is purely intellectual and is played for the pleasure of strategic competition.
Football is a game involving two teams, codified rules, a criterion of victory, and a large component of physical skill. It is played for amusement but also for prizes and professional remuneration.
Solitaire is a game played by a single person with a deck of cards, following explicit rules, with a criterion of success but no competition against another player. It is played to pass the time.

Ring-a-ring-o'-roses is a game played by young children who join hands and move in a circle while singing. There are no explicit rules, no criterion of victory, no competition, and no element of skill. It is played for the sheer delight of movement and song.
definition: An activity A is a game if and only if (i) A is governed by a definite set of rules, (ii) A involves a criterion of winning and losing, and (iii) A is engaged in voluntarily and for the sake of amusement.
conditions: A is governed by a definite set of rules
A involves a criterion of winning and losing
A is engaged in voluntarily and for the sake of amusement
counterexample: Consider a group of children playing at make-believe, pretending to be pirates sailing a cardboard ship across an imaginary ocean. The activity is engaged in voluntarily and for the sake of amusement. Yet there are no definite rules governing the play — the children improvise freely, and any child may introduce new elements at will. There is no criterion of winning or losing; the game has no objective and no end-condition. The first two conditions fail entirely. Yet the children, if asked what they are doing, would say without hesitation that they are playing a game.
abductive_insight: The background cases — chess, football, solitaire, ring-a-ring-o'-roses — progressively strip away the features that the definition treats as essential. Chess has all three conditions; football satisfies them but adds the complication of professional play; solitaire lacks competition; ring-a-ring-o'-roses lacks rules, criteria of victory, and competition alike. The make-believe case completes the pattern: it possesses none of the conditions and yet is unquestionably a game. What the background cases jointly reveal is that the concept of a game is not captured by any conjunction of necessary and sufficient conditions; it is instead a family-resemblance concept, in which diverse activities are grouped together not because they share a common essence but because they are connected by a network of overlapping similarities.
passage:
The concept of a game appears, at first approach, to be a simple matter. Chess is a game: it has rules, a criterion of victory, and is played for amusement. Football is a game: it has codified rules, a criterion of winning, and is played voluntarily. Yet as one surveys further instances, complications multiply. Solitaire is a game played alone, without competition. Ring-a-ring-o'-roses is a game without rules, without a criterion of victory, and without any element of skill. These background cases progressively strip away the features that seem essential.

One might attempt to define a game as an activity governed by definite rules, involving a criterion of winning and losing, and engaged in voluntarily for amusement. This definition captures chess and football but falters with solitaire, which lacks a competitor, and collapses entirely with ring-a-ring-o'-roses. Now consider the make-believe play of children: a group of children pretending to be pirates, sailing a cardboard ship across an imaginary ocean. The activity is voluntary and pursued with evident delight. Yet there are no rules — the children improvise freely. There is no criterion of success — the game has no objective and no end-condition. No one wins or loses. The first two conditions fail entirely. Yet the children would say, without the slightest hesitation, that they are playing a game. The background cases, taken together with the make-believe case, reveal that the concept of a game does not admit of definition by a set of necessary and sufficient conditions. What connects chess, football, solitaire, ring-a-ring-o'-roses, and pirate make-believe is not a common essence but a network of overlapping similarities — a family resemblance that binds diverse activities under a single concept without any one feature running through them all.

background_cases: A wooden kitchen chair has four legs, a flat seat, a vertical back, and is designed for a single person to sit at a table. It is the prototypical chair — the object one pictures when the word is first spoken.

A bean-bag chair has no legs, no rigid structure, and no fixed form. It conforms to the body of the sitter and rests directly on the floor. Yet it is called a chair and is marketed, purchased, and used as one.

A dentist's chair reclines to a nearly horizontal position, has armrests, a headrest, and various mechanical adjustments. It is designed not primarily for sitting but for positioning a patient during dental procedures. Yet it is universally called a chair.
A wheelchair has wheels rather than legs, is mobile, and is designed for persons who cannot walk. Its function is transportation as much as seating. Yet it is called a chair.
definition: An artefact X is a chair if and only if (i) X has a rigid structure comprising a seat, a back, and legs, (ii) X is designed for a single person to sit upon in an upright position, and (iii) X is a freestanding piece of domestic furniture.
conditions: X has a rigid structure comprising a seat, a back, and legs
X is designed for a single person to sit upon in an upright position
X is a freestanding piece of domestic furniture
counterexample: Consider a hanging egg chair — a woven pod suspended from a ceiling by a chain, with a cushioned interior. It has no legs; it hangs from above. It is not rigid; it sways when the sitter moves. It is not freestanding; it depends upon the ceiling for support. And it is not strictly domestic; such chairs are found in gardens, hotels, and cafes. All three conditions fail. Yet it is sold as a chair, recognised as a chair, and used as a chair by everyone who encounters it.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the kitchen chair, the bean-bag, the dentist's chair, the wheelchair — progressively undermine each condition of the definition. The bean-bag lacks rigidity and legs. The dentist's chair is not domestic and is not primarily for upright sitting. The wheelchair is mobile and serves a transportation function. The hanging egg chair completes the pattern by violating all three conditions simultaneously. What the cases jointly reveal is that the concept of a chair is organised around a functional core — the provision of a supported sitting surface for one person — and that the structural features cited in the definition are merely typical accompaniments of this function rather than essential conditions.
passage:
When one considers the range of objects that are called chairs, the diversity is remarkable. The kitchen chair — with its four legs, flat seat, and straight back — is the prototype. But the bean-bag chair has no legs and no rigidity. The dentist's chair reclines to a near-horizontal position and belongs not to the domestic sphere but to a medical clinic. The wheelchair has wheels, moves through space, and serves a transportation function alongside its seating function. Each of these objects is called a chair despite failing one or more of the features that seem central to the concept.

One might attempt to capture the essence of chairs by defining a chair as a rigid, freestanding piece of domestic furniture with a seat, back, and legs, designed for a single person to sit upon in an upright position. This definition fits the kitchen chair perfectly. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency in the most thoroughgoing manner is the hanging egg chair — a woven pod suspended from a ceiling by a chain, with a cushioned interior. It has no legs. It is not rigid; it sways with the sitter's movements. It is not freestanding; it depends upon the ceiling for its support. And it is not strictly domestic; such chairs are found in gardens, hotel lobbies, and seaside cafes. Every condition of the definition fails. Yet the object is manufactured, marketed, purchased, and used as a chair, and no competent speaker of English would hesitate to call it one. The background cases, taken together, suggest that what unites all chairs is not a set of structural features but a functional core: the provision of a supported sitting surface for one person. The structural features — legs, rigidity, a back — are common accompaniments of this function but not constitutive of it. The definition, by elevating typical features to essential conditions, mistakes the prototype for the essence.

background_cases: A child who has broken a vase, when asked by his mother whether he broke it, says 'No.' The child asserts a falsehood, believes it to be false, and intends to deceive his mother. This is a paradigmatic lie: assertion, falsehood, belief in falsehood, and intention to deceive all coincide.

A witness in court, under oath, testifies that the defendant was not present at the scene of the crime, despite having seen the defendant there. The witness asserts a falsehood, believes it to be false, and intends to mislead the court. The institutional gravity of the setting heightens the moral weight of the lie but does not alter its logical structure.

A diplomat, asked by a journalist whether his country is developing a certain weapon, replies 'We have no such programme.' The diplomat knows this to be false. He asserts it with the intention of deceiving the journalist and, through her, the public. Again, all the hallmarks of lying are present.
definition: A person P lies if and only if (i) P makes an assertion, (ii) the content of the assertion is false, and (iii) P intends the hearer to form a false belief on the basis of the assertion.
conditions: P makes an assertion
The content of the assertion is false
P intends the hearer to form a false belief on the basis of the assertion
counterexample: Consider a student who has not done her homework. When the teacher asks, 'Have you done your homework?', the student replies, 'Oh, I left it at home by mistake.' In fact, the homework is at home — she left it on the kitchen table. But she left it there because she never completed it, and her reply is designed to create the impression that the homework was completed and merely forgotten. The content of the assertion — 'I left it at home' — is literally true. The second condition fails. Yet the student's intention is wholly deceptive, and one might observe that the student has lied, despite the literal truth of her words.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the child, the witness, the diplomat — all involve straightforward assertions of outright falsehoods. The homework case reveals that the same deceptive intention can be enacted through a literally true statement that is designed to create a false impression. What the background cases share with the homework case is the intention to induce a false belief; what they do not share is the falsity of the assertion itself. The definition, by requiring that the asserted content be false, captures lies-by-falsehood but cannot capture lies-by-misleading-truth — cases in which the liar exploits the literal truth to convey a falsehood more effectively than an outright falsehood would.
passage:
The clearest cases of lying share a common structure. A child denies having broken a vase. A witness falsely testifies in court. A diplomat denies the existence of a weapons programme. In each case, the liar asserts a falsehood, believes it to be false, and intends to mislead the hearer. These background cases suggest a straightforward definition: a person lies when she makes a false assertion with the intention that the hearer form a false belief.

Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this definition arises in a familiar scholastic setting. A student has not completed her homework. When the teacher asks whether she has done it, the student replies, 'Oh, I left it at home by mistake.' In point of literal fact, the homework is indeed at home — it sits on the kitchen table where the student abandoned it the previous evening. But the student never completed the homework; she left it at home precisely because it was unfinished. Her reply is designed to create the impression that the homework was completed and merely forgotten. The content of the assertion is literally true — she did leave it at home. The second condition of the definition fails. Yet the student's intention is wholly deceptive, and one might observe, with considerable justification, that she has lied. The background cases, taken together with the homework case, reveal that lying does not require the assertion of a literal falsehood. It is sufficient that the speaker intend to create a false impression, and a literally true statement may serve that purpose as effectively as — or more effectively than — an outright falsehood. The definition, by requiring falsity of content, cannot accommodate the liar who exploits truth to deceive.

background_cases: A friend says, 'I promise I shall return your book by Friday.' She intends to return it, she utters the words in a context where both parties understand the commitment being made, and the hearer relies upon the promise in making his plans. The promise is a speech act that creates an obligation through the conjunction of sincere intention, mutual understanding, and reliance.

A merchant says to a customer, 'I promise this watch is genuine Swiss manufacture.' The customer relies upon the merchant's word and pays accordingly. The promise functions as a guarantee, and the merchant's sincerity is tested by his willingness to stand behind it.

A parent says to a child, 'I promise we shall go to the seaside on Saturday.' The child relies upon the promise, tells her friends, and makes plans. The parent recognises that the promise creates an obligation that cannot be lightly set aside, even if circumstances change.
definition: An utterance U by a person P constitutes a promise if and only if (i) P asserts that they will perform a future action A, (ii) P sincerely intends to perform A at the time of utterance, and (iii) the utterance is made in a context where both P and the hearer recognise that a commitment is being undertaken.
conditions: P asserts that they will perform a future action A
P sincerely intends to perform A at the time of utterance
The utterance is made in a context where both P and the hearer recognise that a commitment is being undertaken
counterexample: Consider a couple exchanging wedding vows. Each promises to love, honour, and cherish the other for the rest of their lives. Each sincerely intends to do so. The context is one in which both parties and all witnesses recognise that a commitment of the deepest kind is being undertaken. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet 'to love' is not a future action that can be performed at will — love is not an act but a complex emotional and dispositional state that may wax and wane beyond the control of the will. One might observe that the wedding vow is a paradigmatic promise, and yet what it promises is not an action but a state of character that cannot be summoned by resolution alone.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the book, the watch, the seaside trip — all involve promises to perform discrete, voluntary actions: returning a book, guaranteeing a product, taking a trip. The wedding vow reveals that the most solemn promises may concern not discrete actions but enduring states of character that are not fully within the promisor's voluntary control. What the definition captures is the promising of actions; what it misses is the promising of dispositions, attitudes, and modes of being. The background cases and the wedding vow together suggest that promising is a broader phenomenon than the performance of future acts — it encompasses commitments to ways of being that transcend the category of voluntary action.
passage:
The most familiar promises concern discrete future actions. A friend promises to return a book. A merchant promises that his wares are genuine. A parent promises a trip to the seaside. In each background case, the promise is a commitment to perform a specific voluntary action at a future time. The promiser intends to act, the context is one of recognised commitment, and the hearer relies upon the promiser's word.

One might attempt to characterise promising by holding that a person promises when she asserts that she will perform a future action, sincerely intends to do so, and utters the promise in a context where both parties recognise the commitment. This definition captures the book, the watch, and the seaside trip with precision. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises with the most solemn promise in the repertoire of human social life: the wedding vow. A bride and groom promise to love, honour, and cherish one another for the rest of their lives. Each sincerely intends to keep the promise. The context — a ceremony before witnesses, often before a religious authority — is one in which the gravity of the commitment is unmistakable. All three conditions are satisfied if we construe 'to love' as a future action. But one might observe that loving another person is not an action that can be performed at will. It is a complex emotional and dispositional state that may wax, wane, and transform in ways that elude the control of the will. The background cases, taken together with the wedding vow, reveal that the concept of promising is broader than the definition allows. The most important promises are not promises to act but promises to be — commitments to enduring dispositions and modes of character that transcend the category of voluntary action. The definition, by restricting promises to future actions, cannot accommodate the very promises that matter most.

background_cases: A game of chess is characterised by elaborate rules governing the movements of pieces, the conditions of check and checkmate, and the alternation of turns. Players pursue a determinate goal — the capture of the opponent's king — within a framework of constraints that distinguish permissible from impermissible moves. The activity is bounded, structured, and purposeful within its own domain.

A game of hide-and-seek among children is characterised by simple rules — one child counts while others hide, and then seeks them out — and by the mutual understanding that the activity is play rather than earnest concealment. There is a goal (finding or remaining hidden), but the rules are informal and the pleasure lies in the process rather than the outcome.

A game of poker is characterised by an elaborate system of betting rules, hand rankings, and conventions of play. Chance plays a large role, but skill in assessing probabilities and reading opponents is also essential. The game is competitive, rule-governed, and pursued for stakes — whether monetary or merely social.
definition: An activity A is a game if and only if (i) A is governed by rules that the participants accept, (ii) A involves a contest between two or more parties for a determinate outcome, and (iii) A is undertaken for the purpose of recreation or amusement.
conditions: A is governed by accepted rules
A involves a contest between two or more parties
A is undertaken for recreation or amusement
counterexample: Consider a child playing alone with a set of building blocks, constructing towers and then knocking them down, inventing rules as she goes — 'the tower must be at least ten blocks high before I may topple it.' She is governed by rules she has devised for herself. She is engaged in play, undertaken for amusement. But there is no contest between two or more parties; she plays alone, against no opponent. Yet one would not hesitate to say that she is playing a game — a game of her own invention, but a game nonetheless.
abductive_insight: The background cases span a wide range — chess with its formal competition, hide-and-seek with its informal mutual play, poker with its mixture of chance and skill — yet the definition distils from them only the feature of contest between parties. But hide-and-seek, though it involves multiple children, is often played in a spirit of cooperative delight rather than genuine contest; and the child building towers engages in a structured, rule-governed, playful activity that resembles the background cases in every respect except the presence of an opponent. What the background cases share with the solitary child is the creation of a structured space of play governed by accepted constraints — not necessarily the presence of an adversary. The definition mistakes a frequent feature of games (competition between parties) for a necessary one, when what is essential is the voluntary acceptance of constraining rules within a context of play.
passage:
When one surveys the activities we call games, their variety is striking. Chess involves elaborate rules, alternating turns, and a determinate goal pursued in direct competition. Hide-and-seek involves simple rules and mutual play among children, where the delight is in the process more than the outcome. Poker involves chance, skill, and competition for stakes. Despite this diversity, certain features recur: the acceptance of rules, the pursuit of goals within constraints, and the spirit of play.

One might attempt to characterise a game as a rule-governed activity involving a contest between two or more parties, undertaken for recreation. This definition captures many instances. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency is provided by solitary play. Consider a child playing alone with building blocks. She has devised a rule for herself: the tower must reach at least ten blocks in height before she may knock it down. She builds with concentration, counts carefully, and then topples her creation with gleeful satisfaction before beginning again. The activity is governed by a rule she has accepted. It is undertaken for amusement. But there is no contest between two or more parties — she plays alone, contending against nothing but the instability of her construction. Yet one would not hesitate to call this a game. What the background cases and the solitary child share is the voluntary acceptance of constraining rules within a context of play — the creation of a structured space in which certain moves are permissible and others are not, pursued in a spirit of amusement. The definition, by insisting on contest between parties, elevates a frequent feature of games to the status of a necessary condition and thereby excludes a great deal of what common usage unhesitatingly includes.

background_cases: Bread is regarded as food in virtually every culture: it is prepared from grain, it provides nourishment, and it is consumed with the intention of sustaining the body. Its status as food is uncontroversial because it is digestible, nutritious, and conventionally recognised as an item of diet.

A roasted joint of beef is regarded as food: it is an animal product prepared by cooking, it provides substantial nourishment in the form of protein and fat, and it is consumed as a central element of a meal. Its status as food is secured by both its nutritional value and its cultural recognition.

A fresh apple is regarded as food: it is a natural product consumed without elaborate preparation, it provides vitamins and sugars, and it is eaten to satisfy hunger or appetite. Its status as food is secured by its digestibility, its nutritional content, and its conventional recognition as something one eats.
definition: A substance S is food if and only if (i) S is digestible by human beings, (ii) S provides nutritional value — energy, vitamins, minerals, or other substances required by the body, and (iii) S is conventionally recognised as an item of diet in the society in which it is consumed.
conditions: S is digestible by human beings
S provides nutritional value
S is conventionally recognised as a dietary item
counterexample: Consider a decorative sugar sculpture of the kind displayed at banquets in the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries. The sculpture is made entirely of sugar, which is digestible and provides caloric energy. Sugar is conventionally recognised as a foodstuff. All three conditions are met. Yet the sculpture is not intended to be eaten — it is created for display, admired as an ornament, and often preserved rather than consumed. One would hesitate to call a decorative sugar sculpture 'food,' even though the substance of which it is composed is a paradigmatic food item. The object's purpose is aesthetic, not alimentary.
abductive_insight: The background cases share a feature absent from the sugar sculpture: in each, the substance is not merely digestible and nutritious but is prepared, presented, and consumed with the intention of nourishing the body. Bread is baked to be eaten. Beef is roasted to be eaten. An apple is picked to be eaten. The sugar sculpture is made of an edible substance but is fashioned for display and admiration, not for consumption. The definition captures the material and social conditions of being food but omits the intentional condition — that the substance be prepared or offered for the purpose of being eaten. The paradigmatic cases reveal that food is not merely a nutritious substance but a nutritious substance oriented towards consumption.
passage:
The items most readily recognised as food share a feature that goes beyond digestibility and nutritional content. Bread is baked to be eaten — it is prepared from grain with the express purpose of providing nourishment at table. A roasted joint of beef is cooked to be eaten — its preparation is governed by the intention that it will be consumed as part of a meal. A fresh apple is picked to be eaten — one reaches for it with the intention of satisfying hunger or appetite. In each case, the substance is not merely capable of nourishing but is oriented towards nourishment by the intentions of those who prepare and present it.

One might attempt to characterise food as any substance that is digestible, nutritionally valuable, and conventionally recognised as a dietary item. This definition captures the material and social dimensions of the concept. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency is provided by the decorative sugar sculpture of the kind once displayed at grand banquets. Such a sculpture is made entirely of sugar — a substance that is digestible, provides caloric energy, and is universally recognised as a foodstuff. All three conditions are met by the material of which the sculpture is composed. Yet the sculpture is not food. It is created for display, admired as an ornament, and often preserved under glass rather than consumed. No one at the banquet would dream of breaking off a piece to eat. The substance is edible but the object is not food. What the background cases possess and the sculpture lacks is the orientation towards consumption — the intention that the substance be eaten. The definition, by attending only to the material properties of the substance and its conventional recognition, cannot distinguish a substance prepared for eating from the same substance fashioned for an entirely different purpose.

background_cases: Gold coins served as money in many societies because they were durable, portable, divisible, and recognised by all parties to a transaction as possessing value. Their status as money depended not only on their intrinsic properties but on a widely shared acceptance that they could be exchanged for goods and services.

Paper banknotes issued by a national bank serve as money because they are accepted throughout the economy as a medium of exchange. They possess no intrinsic value — the paper itself is nearly worthless — but the issuing authority guarantees their redemption, and public confidence sustains their purchasing power.

Cowrie shells served as money in parts of West Africa and South Asia for centuries. They are durable, portable, and difficult to counterfeit, but their value as money derived entirely from the conventional agreement of the communities that used them. Outside those communities, a cowrie shell was merely a shell.
definition: An item M functions as money if and only if (i) M serves as a medium of exchange — it is accepted in transactions for goods and services, (ii) M serves as a store of value — it retains its purchasing power over time, and (iii) M serves as a unit of account — it provides a common measure in which prices are expressed.
conditions: M serves as a medium of exchange
M serves as a store of value
M serves as a unit of account
counterexample: Consider cigarettes as used in prisoner-of-war camps during the Second World War. Cigarettes were accepted in exchange for food, clothing, and other goods — they served as a medium of exchange. Prices were quoted in cigarettes — they served as a unit of account. But cigarettes are consumable; they degrade, are smoked, and lose value rapidly as supply fluctuates. They did not reliably store value over time. The second condition was poorly satisfied at best. Yet cigarettes functioned as money in those camps — they were the principal currency, and economic life revolved around them.
abductive_insight: The background cases — gold coins, banknotes, cowrie shells — share the feature of durability, which underwrites their capacity to store value. But the definition elevates the store-of-value function to the status of a necessary condition, when the cigarette case shows that an item can function as money even when this function is substantially impaired. What gold coins, banknotes, cowrie shells, and cigarettes all share is widespread acceptance as a medium of exchange within a community — this is the feature that is present in every case. The store-of-value and unit-of-account functions, while typical of well-functioning monetary systems, are not essential to the basic phenomenon of money. The definition mistakes the characteristics of stable money for the characteristics of money as such.
passage:
The items that have functioned as money across different societies and periods are remarkably diverse — gold coins, paper banknotes, cowrie shells — yet they share certain features. Gold coins were durable, portable, and universally recognised. Banknotes, though intrinsically worthless, were sustained by state guarantee and public confidence. Cowrie shells owed their monetary status entirely to conventional agreement within the communities that used them. In each case, the item was accepted as a medium of exchange, served to store value over time, and provided a common measure in which prices were expressed.

One might attempt to characterise money as an item that serves as a medium of exchange, a store of value, and a unit of account. This three-function definition is widely employed and captures the standard cases well. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency is provided by the use of cigarettes as currency in prisoner-of-war camps during the Second World War. Cigarettes were accepted in exchange for food, clothing, and services — they served admirably as a medium of exchange. Prices were quoted in cigarettes — a tin of bully beef might cost twenty cigarettes, a bar of chocolate five. They served as a unit of account. But cigarettes are fragile, consumable, and subject to rapid fluctuations in supply; they were smoked, they degraded, and their value was unstable. The store-of-value function was substantially impaired. Yet no one who has studied the camp economies would deny that cigarettes functioned as money — they were the currency around which economic life was organised. What the background cases and the cigarette case have in common is widespread acceptance as a medium of exchange within a community. The store-of-value function, while characteristic of stable monetary systems, is not essential to the basic phenomenon. The definition mistakes the features of well-functioning money for the features of money as such.

background_cases: A merchant who tells a customer that a flawed garment is free of defects, knowing it to be defective, has lied. He asserted something he believed to be false with the intention of causing the customer to accept a falsehood. The assertion was direct, the belief in its falsity was clear, and the intent to deceive was manifest.

A witness in court who testifies that she did not see the accused at the scene, knowing full well that she did, has lied. She made a direct assertion she believed to be false with the intention of misleading the tribunal. The setting makes the deceptive intent unambiguous.

A child who, when asked whether he ate the biscuits, says 'No,' knowing full well that he did, has lied. He directly asserted what he believed to be false in order to avoid punishment. The assertion was simple and its falsity was known to the speaker.
definition: A statement S made by a speaker A is a lie if and only if (i) A asserts S, (ii) A believes S to be false, and (iii) A intends by asserting S to cause the hearer to accept S as true.
conditions: A asserts S
A believes S to be false
A intends to cause the hearer to accept S as true
counterexample: Consider a person who, knowing his wife has arranged a surprise party for him, tells her: 'I have no idea what you are planning.' He asserts a proposition he believes to be false. He intends her to accept the assertion as true — for if she suspects he knows, the surprise will be spoiled, and her pleasure will be diminished. All three conditions are met. Yet one might hesitate to call this a lie, for the intention is not to harm or to gain an unfair advantage but to preserve a shared social pleasure. The statement has the logical form of a lie but not, it seems, the moral character of one.
abductive_insight: The background cases share a feature absent from the surprise-party case: in each, the speaker's deceptive assertion is aimed at securing an advantage or avoiding accountability at the hearer's expense. The merchant deceives to profit from the sale of a flawed garment. The witness deceives to obstruct the course of proceedings. The child deceives to escape punishment. In the surprise-party case, the deception is undertaken not for the speaker's advantage but for the hearer's delight. The definition captures the formal structure of lying — false assertion with deceptive intent — but omits the orientation towards the hearer's disadvantage that is present in all the paradigmatic cases. What we ordinarily condemn as lying involves not merely deception but deception that works against the interests of the deceived.
passage:
The clearest cases of lying share a common orientation. A merchant who misrepresents the quality of a garment deceives the customer to secure a sale — the deception works against the buyer's interests. A witness who falsely denies seeing the accused deceives the tribunal to obstruct its proceedings — the deception works against the interests of those seeking a truthful account. A child who denies eating the biscuits deceives the parent to avoid punishment — the deception serves the child's interests at the expense of the parent's desire for honest communication. In each case, the false assertion is directed against the hearer's interests.

One might attempt to characterise a lie as an assertion the speaker believes to be false, made with the intention of causing the hearer to accept it as true. This definition captures the formal features of lying. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises in the domain of social kindness. Suppose a man knows that his wife has arranged a surprise party for him. To preserve her pleasure and the success of the surprise, he tells her: 'I have no idea what you are planning.' He asserts what he believes to be false. He intends her to accept the assertion as true. All three conditions are met. Yet one might hesitate to call this a lie — or at least to condemn it as one. The man's intention is not to gain an advantage at his wife's expense but to preserve a shared joy. The deception works in the hearer's favour rather than against it. What the paradigmatic cases possess and the surprise-party case lacks is the orientation of the deception against the hearer's interests. The definition captures the logical structure of lying but not the adversarial character that distinguishes the lies we condemn from the benign deceptions we tolerate or even applaud.

background_cases: When a grandmother knits a jumper for her grandchild and presents it at Christmas, we recognise the transfer as a gift because it is motivated by affection and a desire to bring the child pleasure; the grandmother expects nothing in return and would be surprised by any suggestion that the jumper creates an obligation.

When a neighbour brings a cake to a family that has just moved into the street, we recognise the gesture as a gift because it is an expression of welcome and goodwill; the neighbour does not expect a cake in return but simply wishes to extend kindness.

When a retiring professor gives her favourite books to a promising student, we recognise the transfer as a gift because it is motivated by a desire to support the student's intellectual development; the professor does not seek any reciprocal benefit.
definition: A transfer of an object O from person A to person B constitutes a gift if and only if (i) A voluntarily relinquishes O to B, (ii) A does not receive material compensation in return, and (iii) B accepts O.
conditions: A voluntarily relinquishes O to B
A does not receive material compensation in return
B accepts O
counterexample: Suppose a senior partner at a law firm sends an expensive hamper of food to a junior associate who has just been assigned to a case in which the partner has a personal interest. The transfer is voluntary. No material compensation is exchanged. The associate accepts the hamper. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the hamper is not a gift in the genuine sense; it is a strategic gesture designed to create a sense of personal obligation in the associate and thereby influence his handling of the case. The motive is manipulation, not generosity.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that the transfer is motivated by genuine benevolence or affection toward the recipient — a concern for the recipient's good that is independent of any advantage to the giver. The definition fails to require that the motive for the transfer be one of goodwill, and thereby cannot exclude transfers that are strategic, manipulative, or self-serving.
passage:
When we consider paradigmatic gifts, a common motivational feature becomes apparent. The grandmother knitting a jumper for her grandchild is moved by affection and a desire to bring pleasure. The neighbour presenting a cake to newcomers is moved by goodwill and a spirit of welcome. The retiring professor giving her books to a student is moved by a desire to support the student's development. In each case, the transfer is motivated by genuine benevolence — a concern for the recipient's good that is independent of any benefit to the giver.

One might attempt to characterise a gift as a voluntary transfer of an object without material compensation, accepted by the recipient. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be drawn from professional life. Suppose a senior partner at a law firm sends an expensive hamper of food to a junior associate who has just been assigned to a case in which the partner has a personal financial interest. The transfer is voluntary. No material compensation changes hands. The associate, flattered, accepts the hamper. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet one might observe that this is not a genuine gift. The partner's motive is not benevolence but strategy: by creating a sense of personal obligation in the associate, the partner hopes to influence the associate's handling of the case in a manner favourable to the partner's interests. What the background cases share, and what the definition fails to require, is that the transfer be motivated by genuine goodwill toward the recipient — a concern for the other's good that is not instrumental to the giver's own purposes.
