background_cases: When a father tells his daughter that he will attend her school play on Friday evening, the daughter understands this as a promise because the father speaks sincerely, is free to choose whether to attend, and recognises that his declaration creates a moral obligation that he will feel bound to honour even at personal inconvenience.

When a friend says she will help you move house next Saturday, you understand this as a promise because the friend speaks voluntarily, intends you to rely upon her word, and accepts that she has placed herself under an obligation from which she cannot withdraw without moral cost.

When a craftsman tells a customer that the repaired chair will be ready by Tuesday, the customer understands this as a promise because the craftsman speaks in a context of mutual respect, freely undertakes the commitment, and acknowledges that failing to meet it would constitute a breach of trust.
definition: An utterance U by person A to person B constitutes a promise if and only if (i) A declares an intention to perform a future act, (ii) B understands U as a commitment by A, and (iii) A's utterance is voluntary.
conditions: A declares an intention to perform a future act
B understands U as a commitment by A
A's utterance is voluntary
counterexample: Suppose a man, held at gunpoint by a robber, declares that he will hand over his wallet. He declares an intention to perform a future act. The robber understands this as a commitment. And one might say the utterance is voluntary in the minimal sense that no one physically forced the man's lips to move. All three conditions appear satisfied. Yet this is not a promise; it is a capitulation to threat. The man does not regard himself as morally bound by his words once the danger has passed, and no reasonable person would hold him to them.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that the speaker's declaration arises from a context of freedom and mutual respect — the speaker freely takes on an obligation that he regards as morally binding, and both parties understand the commitment as creating a genuine moral bond. The definition fails to require that the voluntariness extend to the full moral context — that the speaker be free from coercion and that both parties recognise the moral seriousness of the undertaking.
passage:
When we examine paradigmatic promises, a common feature of their moral context emerges. The father promising to attend his daughter's play speaks freely and recognises that his word creates an obligation he will honour even at personal cost. The friend promising to help with a house move accepts that withdrawing would constitute a moral failure. The craftsman promising to finish a repair by Tuesday acknowledges that missing the date would breach the trust between him and his customer. In each case, the promise arises from a context of freedom and mutual respect, and both parties understand the commitment as morally binding.

One might attempt to characterise a promise as a voluntary declaration of intention to perform a future act, understood by the hearer as a commitment. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be drawn from the experience of coercion. Suppose a man is held at gunpoint by a robber and declares that he will hand over his wallet. He declares an intention to perform a future act. The robber understands the declaration as a commitment. And the utterance is voluntary in the narrow sense that the man moved his lips of his own accord. All three conditions appear to be met. Yet one might observe that this is not a promise in any genuine sense. The man does not regard himself as morally bound by his words once the danger has passed, and no reasonable person would hold him to them. What the background cases share, and what the definition fails to require, is that the declaration arise from a context of genuine freedom — that the speaker be free from coercion and that both parties recognise the moral seriousness of the undertaking, rather than one party extracting a declaration by force.

background_cases: When we regard a motor car as a vehicle, our judgement rests on the fact that it is a discrete, self-contained unit that carries its own source of motive power and can travel along any suitable road at the direction of its operator; its movement is not constrained by a fixed track or cable.

When we regard a sailing boat as a vehicle, our judgement rests on the fact that it is a discrete unit carrying persons across water under the direction of its crew, capable of navigating freely among different routes and destinations.

When we regard a horse-drawn carriage as a vehicle, our judgement rests on the fact that it is a self-contained conveyance — carrying passengers under the direction of a driver — capable of travelling along any road the horse can pull it.
definition: An object V is a vehicle if and only if (i) V is designed to transport persons or goods, (ii) V is capable of locomotion, and (iii) V operates by means of a recognisable source of motive power.
conditions: V is designed to transport persons or goods
V is capable of locomotion
V operates by means of a recognisable source of motive power
counterexample: Consider a goods lift in a warehouse — a platform that moves vertically between floors to transport heavy crates. It is designed to transport goods. It is capable of locomotion — it moves up and down. It operates by means of a recognisable source of motive power — an electric motor. All three conditions are met. Yet we should hesitate to call a goods lift a vehicle; it is a piece of fixed machinery, permanently installed in the warehouse, incapable of travelling from one place to another in the manner we associate with vehicles.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that the vehicle is a discrete, self-contained, mobile unit capable of travelling freely between different locations at the direction of an operator. The definition fails to require this autonomous mobility — the capacity to move from place to place along routes chosen by the operator, rather than being confined to a single fixed path within a building or installation.
passage:
When we consider paradigmatic vehicles — a motor car, a sailing boat, a horse-drawn carriage — a common feature emerges. Each is a discrete, self-contained, mobile unit that can travel freely between different locations at the direction of its operator. The motor car travels along any road its driver chooses. The sailing boat navigates freely across open water. The carriage goes wherever the horse and driver take it. In each case, the vehicle possesses autonomous mobility — the capacity to move from place to place along routes that are not predetermined by its own physical installation.

One might attempt to characterise a vehicle as an object designed to transport persons or goods, capable of locomotion, and operating by means of a recognisable source of motive power. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be drawn from the world of industrial machinery. Consider a goods lift in a large warehouse — a sturdy platform that moves vertically between floors to transport heavy crates. The lift is designed to transport goods: the first condition is met. It is capable of locomotion — it ascends and descends: the second condition is met. And it operates by means of an electric motor: the third condition is met. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet one might observe that a goods lift is not a vehicle in any ordinary sense. It is a piece of fixed machinery, permanently installed within the structure of the warehouse, incapable of travelling from one location to another. What the background cases share, and what the definition fails to require, is autonomous mobility — the capacity of the object to move freely between different locations at the direction of an operator, rather than being confined to a single fixed axis of motion within a building.

background_cases: When a carpenter spends the day building a bookcase, we recognise his activity as work because it is purposive — directed toward the production of a useful object — and sustained by discipline; the carpenter shapes the wood not for the pleasure of the activity itself but to bring a bookcase into existence.

When a teacher prepares lessons and instructs her pupils, we recognise her activity as work because it is directed toward a definite productive end — the education of the young — and is sustained by a sense of responsibility rather than by the mere enjoyment of talking.

When a farmer ploughs his field, we recognise the activity as work because it is directed toward the production of a crop and is sustained by the necessity of the task, not by any pleasure inherent in the physical exertion of walking behind a plough.
definition: An activity A constitutes work if and only if (i) A requires sustained effort, (ii) A is performed regularly, and (iii) A produces some identifiable outcome or product.
conditions: A requires sustained effort
A is performed regularly
A produces some identifiable outcome or product
counterexample: Consider a person who plays chess every evening for three hours, exerting intense mental effort, following a regular schedule, and producing identifiable outcomes — completed games, improved ratings, annotated analyses. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet we should hesitate to call his chess-playing work; it is a hobby — an activity pursued for its own sake and for the pleasure it affords, not for any productive purpose beyond itself.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that the activity is directed toward an end that is valued independently of the pleasure the activity itself affords — the bookcase, the education, the crop — and is sustained by purpose or necessity rather than by enjoyment. The definition fails to require that the activity be purposive in this sense, and thereby cannot distinguish work from hobbies and leisure pursuits that happen to be effortful, regular, and productive.
passage:
When we reflect upon clear instances of work — a carpenter building a bookcase, a teacher instructing her pupils, a farmer ploughing his field — a common feature emerges. Each activity is directed toward an end that is valued independently of any pleasure the activity itself may afford. The carpenter shapes wood not for the joy of planing but to produce a useful object. The teacher prepares lessons not for the pleasure of speaking but to educate the young. The farmer ploughs not for the physical thrill of exertion but to bring forth a crop. In each case, the activity is sustained by purpose or necessity rather than by enjoyment.

One might attempt to characterise work as any activity that requires sustained effort, is performed regularly, and produces an identifiable outcome. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be drawn from the sphere of leisure. Consider a person who plays chess every evening for three hours. The mental effort is intense and sustained. The schedule is as regular as any worker's. And the activity produces identifiable outcomes — completed games, improved ratings, carefully annotated analyses of positions. All three conditions appear satisfied. Yet one might observe that the man's chess-playing is not work in any serious sense. It is a hobby, pursued for its own sake and for the pleasure it affords. The man would not describe himself as working when he sits down to play, and we should not either. What the background cases share, and what the definition fails to require, is that the activity be directed toward an end valued independently of the activity's own pleasures — that it be sustained by purpose or necessity rather than by the enjoyment of the pursuit itself.

background_cases: Chess is a game: it has explicit rules governing the movement of pieces, a clearly defined goal (checkmate), two players in competition, and the outcome is determined by skill and strategy rather than by chance alone.

Football is a game: it has rules enforced by referees, two competing teams, a clearly defined goal (scoring more goals than the opponent), and the outcome is determined by a combination of skill, strategy, and physical prowess.

Poker is a game: it has rules governing the dealing of cards, the placing of bets, and the ranking of hands; players compete against each other; there is a defined goal (winning the pot); and the outcome depends upon a mixture of skill, bluffing, and luck.
definition: An activity A is a game if and only if (i) A is governed by fixed and explicit rules, (ii) A has a clearly defined goal or winning condition, and (iii) A involves competition between two or more players.
conditions: A is governed by fixed and explicit rules
A has a clearly defined goal or winning condition
A involves competition between two or more players
counterexample: Consider the activity of children engaged in imaginative or pretend play — playing 'house,' for instance, in which one child is the parent, another the child, and a third the family dog. There are no fixed or explicit rules, no winning condition, and no competition. The activity is cooperative, improvisational, and open-ended. Yet children and adults alike call this 'playing a game,' and the activity falls comfortably within the ordinary extension of the concept.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that they are structured competitive activities with rules and goals, but the definition mistakes this cluster of features for the essence of gamehood, overlooking the vast domain of unstructured, cooperative, and open-ended play that is equally entitled to the name 'game.'
passage:
Chess, football, and poker are paradigmatic games, and they share a common structure: each is governed by fixed and explicit rules, has a clearly defined goal or winning condition, and involves competition between players. These cases suggest a definition: a game is a rule-governed competitive activity with a defined goal.

Yet a case which tells against this characterisation may be drawn from the play of children. Consider a group of children engaged in imaginative play — playing 'house,' in which one child assumes the role of the parent, another the child, and a third the family dog. There are no fixed or explicit rules: the scenario unfolds through improvisation and mutual negotiation. There is no winning condition: no one wins or loses, and the activity has no defined endpoint. There is no competition: the children cooperate to sustain and elaborate the fiction. The activity violates all three conditions. Yet children and adults alike describe this as 'playing a game,' and the description seems perfectly natural rather than metaphorical. It appears that the background cases — chess, football, poker — are structured competitive games, and that the definition captures the features of this particular class whilst excluding the vast domain of unstructured, cooperative, and open-ended play that the ordinary concept of game encompasses equally well.

background_cases: When a witness in court swears that the accused was present at the scene of the crime, knowing full well that the accused was elsewhere, the witness lies — she asserts what she knows to be false with the intention of creating a false impression in the minds of the jury.

When a merchant tells a customer that a vase is a genuine Ming dynasty artefact, knowing it to be a modern reproduction, the merchant lies — he asserts what he knows to be false in order to deceive the customer into paying a higher price.

When a student tells her teacher that the dog ate her homework, knowing that she simply neglected to complete the assignment, the student lies — she asserts what she knows to be false to avoid the consequences of her negligence.
definition: A person P lies if and only if (i) P asserts a proposition that P believes to be false, (ii) P intends by the assertion to create a false belief in the hearer, and (iii) the hearer is unaware that P's assertion is false.
conditions: P asserts a proposition that P believes to be false
P intends to create a false belief in the hearer
The hearer is unaware that P's assertion is false
counterexample: Consider a case in which a known compulsive liar tells his friend, 'I caught a fish this large,' gesturing extravagantly, and both parties know that the claim is false. The speaker asserts what he believes to be false and intends, in a sense, to create an impression of his exploits. But the hearer is fully aware that the assertion is false — she knows her friend lies habitually. The third condition is violated. Yet the speaker has lied. The statement is a lie regardless of whether the hearer is taken in by it. A detected lie is still a lie.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that the lie succeeds — the hearer does not know the assertion is false and is therefore vulnerable to deception. The definition mistakes the success of the deception for a constitutive condition of lying, overlooking the fact that a lie is constituted by the speaker's act of insincere assertion, not by the hearer's credulity.
passage:
The perjuring witness, the dishonest merchant, and the negligent student each lie by asserting what they know to be false with the intention of deceiving an unsuspecting hearer. In each case, the hearer is unaware of the falsity and is thus vulnerable to forming a false belief. These cases suggest a definition: lying is the assertion of a believed falsehood, intended to deceive, where the hearer is unaware of the deception.

Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of the third condition may be drawn from the phenomenon of the detected lie. Consider a person widely known for habitual exaggeration who tells his friend, 'I caught a fish this large,' accompanying the claim with an extravagant gesture. He asserts what he believes to be false; he intends, at least in a certain sense, to create an impression of his prowess. But his friend is fully aware that the claim is false — she knows his reputation and discounts everything he says. The third condition is violated: the hearer is aware of the falsity. Yet the speaker has lied. The statement is a lie regardless of whether anyone is deceived by it. A lie that is detected remains a lie; a lie told to a sceptical audience does not cease to be a lie by virtue of the audience's scepticism. It appears that the background cases are cases of successful deception, and that the definition has mistaken the success of the deception for a necessary condition of the lying itself. Lying, one might observe, is constituted by the speaker's act of insincere assertion, not by the hearer's credulity.

background_cases: When a friend says 'I promise to return your book by Friday,' the promise creates an obligation: the friend is now bound to return the book, and failure to do so would constitute a breach of the commitment. The promise is voluntary, explicit, and directed at a specific person.

When a doctor says to a patient, 'I promise to keep your medical information confidential,' the promise creates an obligation of confidentiality. The doctor is bound by the commitment, which is voluntary, explicit, and directed at the patient.

When a contractor says to a homeowner, 'I promise to complete the renovation by December,' the promise creates an obligation to complete the work by the specified date. The commitment is voluntary, explicit, and mutually understood.
definition: A promise is binding if and only if (i) the promisor voluntarily and explicitly commits to performing a specified action, (ii) the promisee understands and accepts the commitment, and (iii) the promisor is capable of performing the promised action at the time of the promise.
conditions: The promisor voluntarily and explicitly commits to performing a specified action
The promisee understands and accepts the commitment
The promisor is capable of performing the promised action at the time of the promise
counterexample: Consider a person who promises her dying mother that she will never sell the family home. At the time of the promise, she is financially capable of keeping the house. Years later, through misfortune, she is no longer capable of maintaining it — the cost of upkeep exceeds her income, and she faces destitution. She was capable at the time of the promise, the promise was voluntary and explicit, and her mother understood and accepted it. All three conditions were satisfied. Yet many would hold that the binding force of the promise is diminished or nullified by the change in circumstances — that a promise whose fulfilment would result in serious harm to the promisor ceases to be binding.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that the promisor's ability to fulfil the promise persists from the time of making through the time of expected fulfilment. The definition requires capability only at the time of the promise, overlooking the fact that the binding force of a promise may be affected by subsequent changes in circumstance that make fulfilment impossible, ruinously costly, or morally unconscionable.
passage:
The friend's promise to return a book, the doctor's promise of confidentiality, and the contractor's promise to complete a renovation are all binding commitments in which the promisor voluntarily and explicitly undertakes an obligation, the promisee understands and accepts it, and the promisor is capable of performing the promised action. These cases suggest a definition of binding promise that requires voluntariness, explicit commitment, mutual understanding, and capability at the time of the promise.

Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of the capability condition may be constructed from the ethics of deathbed promises. Suppose a daughter promises her dying mother that she will never sell the family home. At the time of the promise, she is financially capable of keeping the house — her income is sufficient for its maintenance. The promise is voluntary and explicit; her mother understands and accepts it. All three conditions are satisfied. But years later, through misfortune, the daughter's circumstances change drastically. The cost of maintaining the house now exceeds her income; keeping the promise would reduce her to destitution. She was capable at the time of the promise, yet she is no longer capable at the time when fulfilment is required. Many would hold that the promise's binding force is diminished by this change — that no one is bound to fulfil a promise whose fulfilment would result in serious harm. It appears that the definition, by requiring capability only at the time of the promise, fails to account for the way that the moral force of a promise may be affected by changes in circumstances that could not have been foreseen.

background_cases: A motor car is a vehicle: it is a self-propelled machine designed to transport persons and goods over roads, powered by an internal combustion engine or electric motor, and operated by a human driver.
A railway locomotive is a vehicle: it is a self-propelled machine designed to transport persons and goods along rails, powered by steam, diesel, or electricity, and operated by an engineer.
An aeroplane is a vehicle: it is a self-propelled machine designed to transport persons and goods through the air, powered by jet engines or propellers, and operated by a pilot.
definition: An object O is a vehicle if and only if (i) O is a self-propelled machine, (ii) O is designed to transport persons or goods, and (iii) O is operated by a human being.
conditions: O is a self-propelled machine
O is designed to transport persons or goods
O is operated by a human being
counterexample: Consider a horse-drawn carriage. It is designed to transport persons from one place to another. It is regarded as a vehicle by law, by custom, and by common usage. Yet it is not a self-propelled machine — it is propelled by the horse, a living animal. And the horse is not a machine. The first condition is violated. Yet the carriage is unquestionably a vehicle. The history of transport is largely a history of animal-drawn vehicles that preceded the age of the machine.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that they are all products of the mechanical age — machines powered by engines and operated by trained human operators. The definition generalises from these modern cases, overlooking the fact that vehicles existed for millennia before the invention of the engine, and that the concept of vehicle is defined by the function of conveyance rather than by the means of propulsion.
passage:
The motor car, the railway locomotive, and the aeroplane are all vehicles, and they share a common structure: each is a self-propelled machine designed to transport persons or goods and operated by a human being. These cases suggest a definition of vehicle as a self-propelled, human-operated machine for transportation.

Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be drawn from the history of transport. Consider a horse-drawn carriage — a conveyance of the kind that dominated overland travel for centuries before the invention of the internal combustion engine. It is designed to transport persons from one place to another. It is regarded as a vehicle by law, by custom, and by the common usage of every language. Yet it is not a self-propelled machine. It is propelled by a horse — a living animal, not a machine. The first condition is violated. If we insist that vehicles must be self-propelled machines, we are committed to the curious conclusion that nothing was a vehicle before the age of mechanical propulsion — that the stagecoaches, chariots, and ox-carts that carried persons and goods for millennia were not vehicles. This conclusion seems plainly absurd. It appears that the background cases, being drawn from modern mechanical transport, share the feature of self-propulsion, and that the definition has mistaken this historically contingent feature for a necessary condition of vehiclehood. The concept of vehicle, one might observe, is defined by the function of conveyance rather than by the mechanism of propulsion.

background_cases: When children play hide-and-seek, the activity qualifies as a game not merely because rules are followed and enjoyment is had, but because there is a shared understanding among the participants that the activity is set apart from ordinary life — the child who hides behind the curtain is not genuinely concealing herself from danger but occupying a role within a bounded, self-contained sphere of activity governed by its own internal logic.

When two chess players sit down to a match, the activity is a game because the movements of the pieces are meaningful only within the artificial framework of the rules, which create a self-enclosed domain of significance — the knight's L-shaped movement has no meaning outside the game, and the entire contest is understood by both players to be a voluntary entry into a sphere of rule-governed activity distinct from the concerns of ordinary practical life.

When a group of friends plays charades, the activity qualifies as a game because the participants adopt roles and perform gestures that are understood to be meaningful only within the bounded context of the game — the exaggerated mime of a friend acting out a film title is not a genuine attempt at communication but a playful performance within an agreed-upon framework of rules.
definition: An activity A is a game if and only if (i) A is governed by a set of explicit rules agreed upon by the participants, (ii) A involves competition — there are winners and losers, and (iii) A is engaged in for the purpose of entertainment or enjoyment.
conditions: A is governed by a set of explicit rules agreed upon by the participants
A involves competition — there are winners and losers
A is engaged in for the purpose of entertainment or enjoyment
counterexample: Consider a group of children playing ring-around-the-rosy. They hold hands, dance in a circle, sing the rhyme, and all fall down together. The activity is governed by a set of implicit rules — the children know the movements, the song, and the prescribed conclusion. It is engaged in for enjoyment. Yet there is no competition: no one wins or loses. All three conditions are not jointly met, since the second condition requires competition. Yet ring-around-the-rosy is paradigmatically a game — indeed, it is among the first activities that children learn to call by that name.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that the activity constitutes a voluntary entry into a bounded, self-contained sphere of rule-governed activity set apart from the ordinary practical concerns of life — a sphere whose internal logic is understood by all participants to be distinct from the logic of everyday affairs. The definition, by requiring competition, excludes cooperative and non-competitive activities that are games in every other respect.
passage:
The concept of a game has proved remarkably resistant to satisfactory analysis, yet certain cases illuminate the structural features that any adequate account must accommodate. When children play hide-and-seek, the activity qualifies as a game because the participants share an understanding that they have entered a bounded sphere of activity set apart from ordinary life — the hiding child is not genuinely fleeing danger but occupying a role within a self-contained domain governed by its own rules. When chess players contest a match, the significance of each move is entirely internal to the game — the knight's L-shaped trajectory has no meaning outside the artificial framework of the rules. When friends play charades, the exaggerated gestures are understood by all to be meaningful only within the agreed-upon context of the game, not as genuine attempts at practical communication.

These cases invite a general characterisation: a game is a rule-governed activity involving competition and engaged in for enjoyment. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be drawn from the nursery. Consider a group of small children playing ring-around-the-rosy. They hold hands, dance in a circle, chant the familiar rhyme, and tumble to the ground together at the appointed moment. The activity is governed by rules — the children know the movements, the song, and the prescribed ending. It is engaged in for pure enjoyment. Yet there is no competition whatsoever. No one wins; no one loses. The children act in unison, and the pleasure of the game lies precisely in the shared, cooperative movement, not in any contest between participants. The second condition of the proposed definition is not met. Yet ring-around-the-rosy is paradigmatically a game. One might observe that what the background cases share — and what the definition fails to capture — is the voluntary entry into a bounded, self-contained sphere of rule-governed activity set apart from the practical concerns of ordinary life, a characterisation that does not require competition.

background_cases: When a witness in a court of law testifies that she saw the defendant at the scene of the crime, knowing that she was not present and saw nothing, the testimony constitutes a lie because the witness asserts a proposition she believes to be false in a context in which her utterance is understood by all parties as an assertion — a declaration offered as the truth, subject to the norms of sincere assertion that govern the testimonial context.

When a child tells his mother that he has finished his homework, knowing that he has not, the statement is a lie because the child asserts something he believes false within a conversational context in which his utterance is understood by the hearer as a sincere assertion — the mother takes the child to be offering a truthful report of the state of his homework.

When a salesman tells a customer that a car has never been in an accident, knowing that it has, the statement is a lie because it is an assertion — a declaration presented as true — made within a context governed by the norms of honest commercial dealing, and the salesman believes the content of his assertion to be false.
definition: A statement S made by a speaker A is a lie if and only if (i) A believes S to be false, (ii) A intends the hearer to accept S as true, and (iii) S is uttered in a serious conversational context.
conditions: A believes S to be false
A intends the hearer to accept S as true
S is uttered in a serious conversational context
counterexample: Consider an actor who, in the course of a theatrical performance, delivers the line 'I have murdered the king.' The actor believes this to be false — he has murdered no one. He intends the audience to accept, within the dramatic framework, that the character has murdered the king. And the theatrical performance, though fictional, is a serious context — the actors and audience treat the production with gravity, and the line is delivered with full dramatic weight in a professional theatre. All three conditions appear to be met. Yet the actor has not lied; he has performed a role in a play. No member of the audience has been deceived, and no norm of truthfulness has been violated.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that the speaker's utterance is governed by the norms of assertion — by the tacit understanding that what is said is offered as the truth and may be relied upon as such. The definition, by requiring only a serious context, fails to distinguish genuine assertions from performances, recitations, and other speech acts that may be delivered with full seriousness yet are not governed by the norms of sincere assertion.
passage:
The analysis of lying has long been a matter of philosophical interest, and certain paradigmatic cases illuminate the structural features that any adequate account must capture. When a witness in court testifies falsely, her testimony is a lie because it is an assertion governed by the norms of truthful testimony — she declares what she knows to be false in a context where her words are taken as a sincere report. When a child falsely tells his mother that he has finished his homework, the lie consists in the insincere assertion within a context governed by the expectation of honest communication. When a salesman falsely declares that a car has never been in an accident, the lie derives its character from the norms of honest dealing that govern commercial assertions.

These cases invite a general characterisation: a lie is a statement that the speaker believes false, intends the hearer to accept as true, and utters in a serious conversational context. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be drawn from the theatre. Consider an actor who, in the course of a dramatic production, delivers the line, 'I have murdered the king.' The actor believes this to be false — he has murdered no one. He intends the audience to accept, within the framework of the drama, that the character he portrays has committed the act. And the theatrical context is serious in every relevant sense: the performance is a professional production, treated with gravity by actors and audience alike. All three conditions appear to be met. Yet the actor has manifestly not lied. No member of the audience has been deceived, and no norm of truthfulness has been violated. One might observe that what the background cases share — and what the definition fails to require — is that the speaker's utterance be governed by the norms of assertion, the tacit understanding that what is said is offered as truth and may be relied upon as such. Theatrical performance, however serious, is not governed by these norms, and the definition cannot distinguish the actor's line from the witness's perjury.

background_cases: When a friend promises to meet one for dinner at seven o'clock, the promise derives its binding force from the fact that it is made within a context of mutual understanding — both parties recognise that the utterance creates an obligation, that the friend has committed herself to a course of action, and that the promisee is entitled to rely upon the commitment and to feel aggrieved if it is broken.

When a tradesman promises to complete a repair by Friday, the promise creates a genuine obligation because both parties understand the utterance as generating a commitment that goes beyond a mere prediction or expression of intent — the customer is entitled to plan his affairs on the assumption that the repair will be completed, and the tradesman would owe an explanation if he failed to fulfil his word.

When a parent promises a child that they will visit the seaside on Saturday, the promise binds because it is understood by both parties as the creation of a commitment upon which the child may rely — the child's anticipation and planning are grounded in the expectation that the parent has undertaken a genuine obligation.
definition: An utterance U by a speaker A constitutes a promise if and only if (i) U expresses A's intention to perform a future action, (ii) U is understood by the hearer B as creating a commitment, and (iii) A sincerely intends to perform the action at the time of utterance.
conditions: U expresses A's intention to perform a future action
U is understood by the hearer B as creating a commitment
A sincerely intends to perform the action at the time of utterance
counterexample: Consider a confidence trickster who tells an elderly victim, 'I promise to invest your savings and return them with interest within a month.' The victim understands this as a genuine commitment. The utterance expresses the trickster's purported intention to invest the savings and return them. Yet the trickster has no sincere intention whatsoever to perform the action — he plans to abscond with the money. The third condition is not met. Nevertheless, the victim has been promised; the utterance functions as a promise in every social and moral respect, creating an obligation that the trickster culpably violates.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that the utterance functions within a normative framework of commitment — it creates an obligation upon the speaker and a correlative entitlement in the hearer, regardless of the speaker's private mental states. The definition, by requiring sincere intention, mistakes a feature of honest promises for a constitutive condition of promising itself and thereby fails to classify insincere promises — which are still promises, albeit broken or fraudulent ones — as genuine promises.
passage:
The nature of promising has attracted sustained philosophical attention, and certain representative cases illuminate the structural features essential to any adequate analysis. When a friend promises to meet one for dinner, the utterance creates a binding commitment recognised by both parties — the promisee is entitled to rely upon the assurance and to feel aggrieved if it is broken. When a tradesman promises to complete a repair by a certain date, the promise creates an obligation upon which the customer may plan his affairs. When a parent promises a child a visit to the seaside, the child's anticipation is grounded in the understanding that a genuine commitment has been undertaken.

These cases invite a general characterisation: a promise is an utterance expressing the speaker's intention to perform a future action, understood by the hearer as creating a commitment, and made with the sincere intention to perform. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be drawn from the practice of deception. Consider a confidence trickster who tells an elderly victim, 'I promise to invest your savings and return them with generous interest within a month.' The victim understands this as a genuine commitment. The utterance expresses an intention to perform a future action. Yet the trickster has no sincere intention whatsoever to invest the money or to return it — he plans to disappear with the funds. The third condition of the proposed definition is not satisfied. Yet the victim has undeniably been promised. The utterance functions as a promise in every social and moral respect: it creates an obligation that the trickster is culpable for violating, and it is precisely the broken promise — not the mere broken prediction — that constitutes the moral injury. One might observe that what the background cases share — and what the definition fails to capture — is that promising is a normative act that creates obligations and entitlements through its social and communicative function, regardless of the speaker's private sincerity. The definition, by requiring sincere intention, excludes insincere promises, which are still promises, albeit culpably dishonest ones.

background_cases: When Duchamp submitted a porcelain urinal to an exhibition under the title 'Fountain,' the object became a work of art not because of any intrinsic aesthetic property but because it was presented within the institutional framework of the art world — selected by an artist, submitted to an exhibition, and offered for critical appraisal within a context that conferred upon it the status of art.

When a jazz musician improvises a solo during a live performance, the improvisation becomes a work of art not by virtue of its conformity to any pre-existing aesthetic standard but because it is produced and received within a tradition — the tradition of jazz performance — that provides the framework within which the improvisation is recognised, interpreted, and evaluated as art.

When an anonymous medieval craftsman carved a gargoyle for a cathedral, the gargoyle was not created as art in the modern sense — it served an architectural and symbolic function. Yet it is now displayed in museums and studied as art, its status having been conferred retrospectively by the evaluative practices of later generations who recognised in it qualities worthy of aesthetic attention.
definition: An object or performance X is a work of art if and only if (i) X possesses aesthetic properties — beauty, expressiveness, or formal complexity, (ii) X is the product of skilled human craftsmanship, and (iii) X is created with the intention that it be regarded as art.
conditions: X possesses aesthetic properties — beauty, expressiveness, or formal complexity
X is the product of skilled human craftsmanship
X is created with the intention that it be regarded as art
counterexample: Consider a found object — an oddly shaped stone picked up from a beach — that an artist places on a gallery pedestal with no modification whatsoever. The stone may possess a certain rough beauty, but it is not the product of skilled human craftsmanship — no human hand shaped it. Moreover, the stone itself was not created with the intention that it be regarded as art; it is a natural object. The second and third conditions are not met. Yet once it is placed within the institutional context of the gallery, selected and presented by an artist, critics and visitors may regard it as art, discuss it as art, and treat it as possessing artistic significance.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that the status of art is conferred upon an object or performance by its placement within an institutional and evaluative framework — the art world, a performance tradition, or the retrospective judgement of later generations — rather than by any intrinsic property of the object itself. The definition, by requiring aesthetic properties, skilled craftsmanship, and creative intention, cannot account for cases in which art status is conferred upon objects that lack one or more of these features.
passage:
The question of what makes something a work of art has proved among the most resistant to philosophical analysis. When Duchamp submitted a mass-produced urinal to an exhibition, the object became art not by virtue of any intrinsic aesthetic quality but because it was presented within the institutional framework of the art world — selected, submitted, and offered for critical evaluation. When a jazz musician improvises a solo, the improvisation is art because it is produced and received within a tradition that provides the framework for its recognition and appraisal. When a medieval gargoyle is displayed in a modern museum, its status as art has been conferred retrospectively by later evaluative practices.

These cases invite a general characterisation: a work of art is an object possessing aesthetic properties, produced by skilled craftsmanship, and created with the intention of being regarded as art. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be readily constructed. Consider an oddly shaped stone, picked up from a beach by an artist who places it, entirely unmodified, upon a pedestal in a gallery. The stone may possess a certain rough beauty, but it is emphatically not the product of skilled human craftsmanship — no human hand shaped it. Nor was it created with the intention that it be regarded as art; it is a natural object, formed by the action of wind and water over millennia. The second and third conditions of the proposed definition are not met. Yet once the stone is placed within the institutional context of the gallery, selected and presented by an artist whose act of selection is itself a creative gesture, critics and visitors may discuss it as art, interpret it as art, and attribute to it artistic significance. One might observe that what the background cases share — and what the definition fails to capture — is that art status is conferred by placement within an institutional and evaluative framework, rather than by the possession of intrinsic aesthetic properties, craftsmanship, or authorial intention.

background_cases: A wooden dining chair in a household consists of a flat seat, four legs, and a backrest. It was constructed by a carpenter for the express purpose of seating one person at a dining table. It is recognisably a chair, and its classification as such presents no difficulty.

A leather office chair has a padded seat, a high backrest, armrests, and a wheeled base. It was manufactured for the purpose of seating one person at a desk. Despite its differences from the dining chair — the wheels, the padding, the adjustable height — it is recognisably a chair.

A folding metal chair used at outdoor events has a flat seat and a low backrest, with legs that collapse for storage. It was designed for temporary seating of one person. Despite its impermanence and its minimal construction, it is recognisably a chair.
definition: An object O is a chair if and only if (i) O has a seat designed to support a single sitting person, (ii) O has a backrest, and (iii) O was constructed or manufactured for the purpose of providing seating.
conditions: O has a seat designed to support a single sitting person
O has a backrest
O was constructed or manufactured for the purpose of providing seating
counterexample: Consider a traditional three-legged milking stool used by dairy farmers. It has a seat designed to support a single sitting person, and it was constructed for the purpose of providing seating during the milking of cows. Yet it has no backrest whatsoever. Condition (ii) is not satisfied, and the definition therefore excludes it from the class of chairs. Many speakers would indeed deny that a stool is a chair. But now consider a beanbag chair, sold and marketed under that very name: it has no rigid backrest, no legs, and no discernible structure beyond a fabric shell filled with polystyrene beads. It conforms to the sitter's body, providing incidental back support only when the sitter leans backward. It was manufactured for the purpose of seating. Whether it has a 'backrest' depends entirely on how the sitter arranges the bag. The definition must either include the beanbag — stretching the notion of 'backrest' beyond recognition — or exclude it, despite its universal classification as a chair.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that each possesses a structurally distinct, identifiable backrest as a separable component of the object. The definition, generalised from these cases, treats the backrest as a necessary feature of chairs. Yet the beanbag chair reveals that common usage extends the concept of chair to objects that provide back support only incidentally and contingently, through the deformability of the material rather than through a dedicated structural element. The inductive generalisation from paradigmatic chairs overestimates the structural determinacy of the concept, which in practice admits objects whose form is fluid and whose back support is an emergent feature of use rather than of construction.

The most familiar instances of chairs share a recognisable structure. A wooden dining chair has a flat seat, four legs, and a backrest; it was constructed for the purpose of seating a person at table. A leather office chair has a padded seat, a high backrest, and a wheeled base; it was manufactured for desk work. A folding metal chair has a flat seat and a low backrest, designed for temporary outdoor seating. In each case, the presence of a seat for one person, a backrest, and construction for the purpose of seating is plainly evident.

These cases invite the generalisation that a chair is an object with a seat for one, a backrest, and construction for seating. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be drawn from the class of objects commonly called chairs in everyday life. Consider a beanbag chair — a fabric shell filled with polystyrene beads, sold and universally marketed under the name 'chair.' It has a seat in the sense that a person sits upon it. It was manufactured for the purpose of providing seating. Yet it has no structurally distinct backrest: the fabric shell deforms under the sitter's weight, and back support arises only incidentally when the sitter leans backward, reshaping the bag. Whether the beanbag possesses a 'backrest' depends entirely on how the sitter arranges the material, and in many configurations no back support is provided at all. The definition must either include the beanbag by stretching the concept of backrest beyond structural recognition, or exclude it despite its universal classification as a chair. One might observe that the background cases, from which the definition was generalised, each possess a structurally discrete, identifiable backrest. The concept of chair, however, extends in common usage to objects whose back support is an emergent feature of the sitter's interaction with a deformable material rather than a dedicated structural element of the object itself.

background_cases: A merchant tells a customer that a piece of cloth is pure silk when he knows it to be a blend of silk and cotton. The merchant believes his statement to be false, asserts it with the intention that the customer accept it as true, and the customer is thereby deceived. We recognise this as a lie without hesitation.

A student tells her teacher that she was ill on the day of an examination when she knows she was not. The student believes the statement to be false, asserts it with the intention that the teacher accept it, and the teacher is indeed misled. This too is unambiguously a lie.

A witness in a civil proceeding testifies that he did not see the defendant at the scene, when in fact he saw the defendant clearly. The witness believes his testimony to be false, intends the court to accept it, and the court is misled. This is a paradigmatic instance of lying.
definition: A statement S uttered by a speaker A is a lie if and only if (i) A believes that S is false, (ii) A asserts S with the intention that the hearer accept S as true, and (iii) the hearer is in fact deceived by A's assertion of S.
conditions: A believes that S is false
A asserts S with the intention that the hearer accept S as true
The hearer is in fact deceived by A's assertion of S
counterexample: Suppose a notorious confidence trickster tells a shopkeeper that a certain banknote is genuine when he knows it to be forged. The trickster believes the statement to be false and asserts it with the intention that the shopkeeper accept it as true. However, the shopkeeper, being thoroughly acquainted with the trickster's reputation, does not believe the assertion for a moment and immediately inspects the note, discovering the forgery. The hearer is not deceived. Condition (iii) is not satisfied. Yet one might observe that the trickster has plainly lied to the shopkeeper — the fact that the lie failed to deceive does not alter its character as a lie.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that the lie succeeds: the hearer is deceived. The definition, generalised from these cases, treats successful deception as a necessary condition of lying. Yet the case of the unsuccessful lie reveals that lying does not require the hearer's actual deception. A lie is constituted by the speaker's assertion of what she believes to be false with the intention to deceive, regardless of whether the deception succeeds. The inductive generalisation from the background cases conflates a frequent accompaniment of lying — the hearer's deception — with a necessary condition of it.
passage:
The most perspicuous instances of lying share a common pattern. A merchant deceives a customer about the composition of a fabric; a student misleads a teacher about the reason for an absence; a witness gives false testimony that the court accepts. In each case, the speaker believes the statement to be false, asserts it with the intention that the hearer accept it, and the hearer is in fact deceived. These cases invite the generalisation that a lie is a believed-false assertion intended to deceive, in which the hearer is actually deceived.

Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be drawn from situations in which the intended deception fails. Suppose a notorious confidence trickster tells a shopkeeper that a certain banknote is genuine, when in fact he knows it to be forged. The trickster believes his statement to be false and asserts it with the intention that the shopkeeper accept it as true. However, the shopkeeper, being well acquainted with the trickster's reputation, does not believe the assertion for a moment and immediately inspects the note, discovering the forgery. The hearer is not deceived; the third condition of the definition is not met. Yet one might observe that the trickster has plainly lied to the shopkeeper. The lie has failed in its purpose, but its failure does not alter its character as a lie. One does not cease to have lied merely because the hearer was too shrewd to be taken in. What the background cases share — successful deception — is a frequent accompaniment of lying but not a necessary condition of it. The generalisation from cases in which lies succeed conflates the typical consequence of lying with its essential nature.

background_cases: A friend says to another, 'I promise to return your book by Friday,' and both parties understand this as the creation of an obligation. The promiser intends to return the book and the promisee acquires a claim upon the return. The promise is made sincerely, in a context of mutual trust, and creates a genuine obligation.

A parent says to a child, 'I promise we shall visit the seaside on Saturday,' and the child understands this as a commitment. The parent intends to fulfil the commitment, and the child acquires a legitimate expectation. The promise is sincere and creates an obligation that the parent recognises.

A colleague says, 'I promise to review your manuscript before the conference,' and the recipient relies upon this commitment in planning her schedule. The promiser intends to carry out the review, the promisee adjusts her plans accordingly, and a genuine obligation is created.
definition: An utterance U by a speaker A constitutes a promise to a hearer B if and only if (i) U expresses A's commitment to perform a specific future action, (ii) A sincerely intends to perform the action at the time of uttering U, and (iii) B understands U as creating an obligation on A's part.
conditions: U expresses A's commitment to perform a specific future action
A sincerely intends to perform the action at the time of uttering U
B understands U as creating an obligation on A's part
counterexample: Suppose a confidence trickster says to his victim, 'I promise to double your investment within a month,' knowing full well that he has no intention whatsoever of returning any money. The utterance expresses a commitment to perform a future action. The victim understands it as creating an obligation. But the speaker does not sincerely intend to perform the action. Condition (ii) is not satisfied. Yet one might observe that the trickster has made a promise — a fraudulent promise, to be sure, but a promise nonetheless. The victim has been promised something, and the trickster has incurred an obligation that he intends to violate. If insincere promises are not promises at all, then the concept of a broken or fraudulent promise becomes incoherent.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is the sincerity of the promiser: each speaker genuinely intends to fulfil the commitment. The definition, generalised from these cases, treats sincerity of intention as a necessary condition of promising. Yet the case of the fraudulent promise reveals that the social and moral force of a promise does not depend upon the sincerity of the promiser's intention. A promise creates an obligation by virtue of the communicative act itself, not by virtue of the speaker's private mental state. The inductive generalisation from sincere promises mistakes a morally desirable feature of promising for a constitutive one.

The most familiar instances of promising share a reassuring structure. When a friend promises to return a borrowed book, both parties understand the utterance as creating an obligation: the promiser intends to return the book, and the promisee acquires a claim. When a parent promises a child a trip to the seaside, sincerity of intention is evident and the obligation is genuinely felt. When a colleague promises to review a manuscript, the promisee relies upon the commitment in planning her affairs. In each case, the promise is sincere, and its sincerity appears essential to its character as a promise.

These cases invite the generalisation that a promise is an utterance expressing commitment to a future action, made with sincere intention, and understood by the hearer as creating an obligation. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be drawn from the practice of deception. Suppose a confidence trickster says to his victim, 'I promise to double your investment within a month,' knowing full well that he has no intention of returning any money. The utterance expresses a commitment to a future action. The victim understands it as creating an obligation. But the speaker does not sincerely intend to carry out the action; the second condition is not met. Yet one might observe that the trickster has made a promise — a fraudulent one, certainly, but a promise all the same. The victim has been promised something, and the moral opprobrium attaching to the trickster's subsequent default presupposes that a genuine obligation was created. If we say that insincere promises are not promises at all, the very concept of a fraudulent or broken promise becomes incoherent. What the background cases share is the sincerity of the promiser, and the generalisation from these cases mistakes a morally desirable accompaniment of promising for a constitutive condition. The social and moral force of a promise, it appears, resides in the communicative act itself, not in the private mental state of the speaker.

background_cases: A motor car transports passengers along roads by means of an internal combustion engine. It is a discrete, self-propelled conveyance that carries persons from one location to another, and it is paradigmatically a vehicle.

A sailing yacht transports persons across bodies of water by means of wind captured in its sails. It is a discrete conveyance, propelled by an external natural force, that carries passengers between harbours. It is recognisably a vehicle.

A horse-drawn carriage transports passengers along roads by means of animal power. It is a discrete conveyance, externally propelled by the horse, that carries persons from one place to another. Despite its reliance on animal rather than mechanical power, it is recognisably a vehicle.
definition: An object V is a vehicle if and only if (i) V is a discrete, movable conveyance, (ii) V transports persons or goods from one location to another, and (iii) V is propelled by a source of energy, whether internal or external.
conditions: V is a discrete, movable conveyance
V transports persons or goods from one location to another
V is propelled by a source of energy, whether internal or external
counterexample: Consider a shopping trolley in a supermarket. It is a discrete, movable conveyance: a wheeled cart that can be moved from place to place. It transports goods — groceries — from one location (the shelves) to another (the checkout and then the car park). And it is propelled by a source of energy — the muscular effort of the shopper pushing it. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet we should hesitate to call a shopping trolley a vehicle. It operates within a single enclosed space, it does not travel between distinct geographical locations in the way that vehicles characteristically do, and it is not the kind of conveyance that one would register, insure, or classify alongside cars, ships, and aeroplanes.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that each vehicle traverses a substantial distance between distinct geographical locations — from one town to another, from one harbour to another. The definition captures the abstract structure of these cases but fails to specify the scale and context of transport. The shopping trolley satisfies the conditions in the letter but defeats the spirit of the generalisation by operating at a scale and within a context — the interior of a single building — that the background cases tacitly exclude. The inductive generalisation from paradigmatic vehicles underspecifies the spatial and contextual scope of the transport involved.
