The most familiar instances of vehicles share a common profile. A motor car carries passengers along roads between towns and cities by means of its engine. A sailing yacht transports persons across open water by the force of the wind. A horse-drawn carriage conveys passengers along highways by means of animal power. In each case, a discrete, movable conveyance transports persons or goods from one location to another, propelled by some source of energy.

These cases invite the generalisation that a vehicle is a discrete, movable conveyance that transports persons or goods between locations by means of an energy source. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be drawn from the familiar setting of a supermarket. Consider a shopping trolley: it is a discrete, movable conveyance with wheels. It transports goods — groceries — from one location (the shelves) to another (the checkout and subsequently the car park). And it is propelled by a source of energy, namely the muscular effort of the shopper who pushes it. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied. Yet one might observe that we should be most reluctant to call a shopping trolley a vehicle. It operates within a single building, it does not traverse substantial distances between distinct geographical locations, and it is not the sort of conveyance that one would register, insure, or classify alongside motor cars and ships. What the background cases share — and what the definition fails to require — is that the transport in question involves movement across a significant geographical distance, between locations that are distinct in a robust spatial sense. The inductive generalisation from paradigmatic vehicles tacitly assumes a scale and context of transport that the definition, stated in its full generality, does not capture.

background_cases: Children playing hide-and-seek follow a definite set of rules, compete against one another, and the activity terminates when all players are found — the activity has a clear structure, competition, and conclusion.
A chess match is governed by precise rules, involves strategic competition between two opponents, and ends in victory, defeat, or stalemate.
A football match involves two teams competing according to a detailed set of rules, with a definite goal — to score more than the opposing side — and a fixed duration.
definition: An activity A is a game if and only if (i) A is governed by a set of explicit rules, (ii) A involves competition between two or more participants, and (iii) A terminates in a determinate outcome — a winner, a loser, or a draw.
conditions: A is governed by a set of explicit rules
A involves competition between two or more participants
A terminates in a determinate outcome
counterexample: Consider a group of children playing with building blocks. They observe certain implicit understandings — they take turns, they do not destroy one another's constructions, they work together to build a tower as tall as possible. One might argue these are rules, albeit not explicit ones. But the activity involves no competition: no one seeks to defeat anyone else. And there is no determinate outcome — no winner, no loser, no draw. The children simply play until they grow tired or are called to supper. Yet the activity is unhesitatingly called a game by the children, their parents, and any observer. The case reveals the insufficiency of the analysis by presenting an instance of play that possesses the character of a game without competition or determinate outcome.
abductive_insight: The background cases — hide-and-seek, chess, football — are all competitive, rule-governed, and outcome-determined. The definition was inferred from a sample that happened to consist entirely of competitive games. But the concept of a game extends well beyond the competitive, encompassing cooperative, imaginative, and open-ended forms of play. The generalisation was drawn from too narrow a class, and the result was an analysis that captured competitive games specifically while failing to account for the broader phenomenon.
passage:
If one examines certain paradigmatic instances of what we call games — hide-and-seek, chess, football — a pattern seems to emerge with admirable clarity. Each is governed by a set of explicit rules that the participants accept as binding. Each involves competition between two or more parties, each seeking to outdo the other. And each terminates in a determinate outcome: someone hides and is found, a king is checkmated, a final whistle is blown and the score is tallied. From these cases, one might reasonably infer that a game is an activity governed by rules, characterised by competition, and concluding with a determinate result.

Yet let us consider a case that tells against this inference. Suppose a group of small children sit upon the nursery floor surrounded by building blocks. They take turns selecting blocks, they observe certain implicit understandings about not toppling one another's constructions, and they work together — cooperatively, not competitively — to build a tower as tall as the room will allow. There is no competition; no child seeks to defeat any other. There is no determinate outcome; the activity concludes not with a victory or a draw but simply when the children tire of it or when the tower collapses amid general laughter. One might question whether the implicit understandings constitute rules in the relevant sense, but even granting this, conditions (ii) and (iii) are plainly unsatisfied. Yet the children, their parents, and any casual observer would describe the activity without hesitation as a game. The explanation, one suspects, is that the definition was abstracted from a sample consisting entirely of competitive games. Hide-and-seek, chess, and football all involve opposition between parties, and the generalisation to competition and determinate outcomes was a natural but ultimately unwarranted extrapolation from this restricted class. The concept of a game, it appears, is broader than competition — it encompasses cooperative, imaginative, and open-ended forms of play that the proposed analysis cannot accommodate.

background_cases: A wooden dining chair has four legs, a flat seat, and a vertical back-rest — it is designed and manufactured for the purpose of seating one person at a table.
An armchair in a sitting room has a padded seat, a high back, and two armrests — it is designed for comfortable seated repose and is used by one person at a time.
An office swivel chair has a wheeled base, an adjustable seat, and a reclining back — it is purpose-built for prolonged seated work at a desk.
definition: An object O is a chair if and only if (i) O has a raised seat designed to support a seated person, (ii) O has a back-rest, and (iii) O is a manufactured artefact intended for the purpose of seating.
conditions: O has a raised seat designed to support a seated person
O has a back-rest
O is a manufactured artefact intended for the purpose of seating
counterexample: Consider a traditional Japanese zaisu — a legless floor chair used for seated dining. The zaisu has a back-rest and is a manufactured artefact intended for seating, satisfying conditions (ii) and (iii). However, it does not have a raised seat in the ordinary sense — it rests directly upon the floor, and the sitter's weight is borne by a cushion at ground level rather than by a seat elevated on legs or a pedestal. Condition (i) is not satisfied if 'raised' is taken to mean elevated above the ground. Yet the zaisu is universally called a chair, and it serves exactly the function that chairs are meant to serve.
abductive_insight: The background cases — dining chair, armchair, office chair — all possess raised seats elevated by legs, pedestals, or wheeled bases. The condition of a raised seat was inferred from a sample that happened to consist entirely of Western chair designs. The zaisu reveals that the elevation of the seat is a culturally specific feature rather than a necessary feature of chairs in general, and that the definition's reliance upon it reflects the contingent character of the sample rather than the essential nature of the concept.
passage:
The dining chair, the armchair, and the office swivel chair present a set of features that seem to define what a chair is. Each has a seat raised above the ground, a back-rest against which the sitter may lean, and each is a manufactured object designed for the express purpose of seating. From these instances, one might abstract the definition that a chair is a manufactured artefact with a raised seat and a back-rest, intended for sitting. The three conditions appear to capture the concept neatly, excluding benches, stools, and sofas while admitting the full range of what we ordinarily call chairs.

Yet one may reflect upon seating customs in other parts of the world and discover that the proposed definition is less universal than it first appeared. In Japanese domestic life, the zaisu is a common article of furniture: it consists of a cushioned seat resting directly upon the floor, with a back-rest rising behind it. The zaisu is a manufactured artefact, designed and intended for seating — conditions (ii) and (iii) are satisfied without difficulty. But the seat is not raised above the ground in the sense that a dining chair's seat is raised; it sits flush with the floor, and the sitter's legs are folded beneath or extended before him at ground level. If condition (i) requires that the seat be elevated, the zaisu fails to qualify. Yet no one familiar with the object would hesitate to call it a chair — it is, indeed, marketed and described as such in both Japanese and English. The most plausible explanation is that the condition of elevation was drawn from a sample of Western chair designs, all of which happen to employ legs, pedestals, or wheeled bases to raise the seat. The generalisation was a product of the sample's cultural specificity rather than of the concept's essential structure. The zaisu reveals that what is genuinely necessary for chairhood is the provision of a supported seating surface with a back-rest, and that the height at which this surface is placed is a variable rather than a defining feature.

background_cases: A woman tells her neighbour that she will water his plants while he is on holiday, and the neighbour relies upon this assurance — she has made a promise and both parties understand it as such.

A man tells his wife that he will attend their daughter's school play, and she arranges her schedule accordingly, trusting his word — a clear instance of a promise that generates a relied-upon obligation.

A solicitor tells a client that she will file the documents before the deadline, and the client refrains from seeking another solicitor — the assurance creates an obligation and is understood by both parties as a promise.
definition: A speech act S by a speaker A is a promise if and only if (i) A expresses an intention to perform a future action φ, (ii) the expression of this intention is directed to a specific person B who understands it as an undertaking, and (iii) B relies upon A's expressed intention in making plans or decisions.
conditions: A expresses an intention to perform a future action φ
The expression of this intention is directed to a specific person B who understands it as an undertaking
B relies upon A's expressed intention in making plans or decisions
counterexample: Consider a case in which a man says to his young daughter, as he tucks her into bed, 'I promise I shall take you to the seaside tomorrow.' The child, delighted, falls asleep at once and forms no plans or decisions based upon the assurance — she simply trusts her father and drifts off. The next morning, the father takes her to the seaside as promised. The promise was genuine: the father expressed an intention, directed it to a specific person who understood it as an undertaking, and the father felt bound by it. Yet condition (iii) is not satisfied, for the child did not rely upon the promise in making plans or decisions — she made no plans at all. The promise was nonetheless a promise, and the father was obligated to keep it.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involve adult parties who adjust their behaviour in response to the assurance — the neighbour relies on the plant-watering, the wife rearranges her schedule, the client refrains from seeking alternative counsel. The condition of reliance was abstracted from a sample in which the promisee happened to be a rational adult capable of making plans. The case of the child reveals that reliance is a common consequence of promising but not a constitutive feature of it: a promise generates an obligation by virtue of the speech act itself, not by virtue of the promisee's subsequent reliance.
passage:
The background cases suggest a clear pattern. When a woman assures her neighbour that she will water his plants, when a man tells his wife he will attend a school play, when a solicitor undertakes to file documents by a deadline — in each instance, the speaker expresses an intention, the hearer understands it as an undertaking, and the hearer relies upon it in making subsequent decisions. From these cases, one might reasonably infer that a promise is a speech act in which an intention is expressed, directed to a person who understands it as an undertaking, and relied upon by that person in planning.

Yet consider the following case, which places the third condition under considerable pressure. A father, sitting at the bedside of his young daughter, says to her with great tenderness: 'I promise I shall take you to the seaside tomorrow.' The child, who is very small — perhaps four years of age — smiles with delight and falls asleep almost immediately. She forms no plans based upon the assurance; she does not arrange her affairs, cancel alternative engagements, or make any decisions that depend upon the promised outing. She simply trusts her father and sleeps. The next morning, the father takes her to the seaside, as he said he would. Now, the promise seems genuine beyond any reasonable doubt. The father expressed an intention to perform a future action; he directed this expression to a specific person who understood it, in her childish way, as an undertaking; and he felt himself bound by it — he would have regarded himself as having broken a promise had he failed to go. Yet condition (iii) is not satisfied: the child did not rely upon the promise in making plans or decisions, for she made no plans at all. The most compelling explanation, one suspects, is that reliance was a feature common to the background cases because those cases all involved adult promisees capable of rational planning. The definition mistook a frequent accompaniment of promising for an essential ingredient. A promise generates an obligation by the speech act itself — by the undertaking — and not by the promisee's subsequent acts of reliance.

background_cases: A motor-car transports passengers along roads by means of an internal combustion engine — it is self-propelled, carries persons, and is designed for transportation.
A horse-drawn carriage conveys passengers by means of animal traction along established routes — it carries persons and is designed for the purpose of transportation.
A railway locomotive pulls carriages along tracks, transporting passengers and goods at considerable speed — it is powered, designed for transportation, and carries its load along a fixed route.
definition: An object O is a vehicle if and only if (i) O is designed for the transportation of persons or goods from one location to another, (ii) O is powered by a motive force — whether mechanical, animal, or otherwise, and (iii) O moves along a surface or track.
conditions: O is designed for the transportation of persons or goods from one location to another
O is powered by a motive force — whether mechanical, animal, or otherwise
O moves along a surface or track
counterexample: Consider a sailing boat used to transport goods across a harbour. The boat is designed for transportation, satisfying condition (i). It moves — but not along a surface or track; it moves through water, propelled by the wind acting upon its sails. The wind is a motive force of a sort, satisfying condition (ii) on a generous reading. But condition (iii) is unsatisfied: the boat does not move along a surface or track but across and through a fluid medium. Yet a sailing boat is, by any ordinary reckoning, a vehicle — one of the oldest forms of conveyance in human history.
abductive_insight: The background cases — motor-car, carriage, locomotive — all involve vehicles that move along solid surfaces or fixed tracks. The generalisation to condition (iii) was an artefact of this terrestrial sample. The sailing boat reveals that the concept of a vehicle is not restricted to surface travel; it encompasses conveyances that operate upon or through water, and by extension through the air. The definition was drawn from too narrow a range of instances and mistook a contingent feature of the sample for a necessary feature of the concept.
passage:
The motor-car, the horse-drawn carriage, and the railway locomotive present a set of features from which one might naturally abstract a definition of what it is to be a vehicle. Each is designed for transportation; each is powered by some motive force — the engine, the horse, the locomotive's boiler; and each moves along a surface or track — the road, the lane, the railway line. From these instances, one infers that a vehicle is an object designed for transportation, powered by a motive force, and moving along a surface or track.

Yet a difficulty arises when one considers conveyances of a rather different character. A sailing boat has been used since antiquity to transport goods and persons across bodies of water. It is unquestionably designed for transportation — condition (i) is satisfied. It is propelled by the wind, which acts upon its sails with considerable force — and while one might debate whether the wind constitutes a motive force in the same sense as an engine or a horse, it is, on any reasonable view, the agency by which the boat is moved, and so condition (ii) may be taken as satisfied on a generous reading. But the boat does not move along a surface or track. It moves upon and through a fluid medium — the sea — following no fixed path and supported not by a solid surface but by the buoyancy of the water. Condition (iii) is not met. Yet a sailing boat is, by universal consensus, a vehicle. The most satisfying explanation of this failure is that the definition was drawn from a sample consisting entirely of land-based conveyances, all of which happen to move along solid surfaces or fixed tracks. The generalisation to condition (iii) was an artefact of this terrestrial bias rather than a genuine requirement of the concept. The sailing boat reveals that the concept of a vehicle is not restricted to surface travel but extends to any conveyance designed for transportation and propelled by an appropriate motive force, regardless of the medium through which it moves.

definition: An object is a chair if and only if it is a free-standing piece of furniture, it has a rigid frame, and it supports the seated posture of a single person with their feet on the ground.
conditions: The object is a free-standing piece of furniture
It has a rigid frame
It supports the seated posture of a single person with their feet on the ground
background_cases: A wooden kitchen chair is free-standing, has a rigid wooden frame, and supports one person seated with feet on the floor.
A metal folding chair is free-standing when unfolded, has a rigid metal frame, and supports one person seated with feet on the ground.
A carved mahogany desk chair is free-standing, has a rigid frame of polished wood, and supports a single person seated with feet on the floor.
counterexample: Consider a case in which a large hanging egg chair, suspended from the ceiling by a chain, is examined. It supports the seated posture of a single person, and the occupant's feet may rest upon the ground. However, it is not free-standing; it depends upon the ceiling for its support. Moreover, its shell is made of flexible wicker that yields considerably under the occupant's weight, so it lacks a rigid frame in any ordinary sense. Yet it is sold, purchased, and used as a chair, and no one would hesitate to call it one.
abductive_insight: The background cases, all drawn from conventional furniture with rigid frames and independent standing, encourage the inference that chairs must be free-standing and rigid. But the hanging egg chair shows that this inference is an artefact of the limited sample: the concept of a chair is flexible enough to encompass objects that are suspended rather than free-standing and pliable rather than rigid, provided they serve the function of supporting a seated person.
passage:
If one surveys the chairs most commonly encountered in domestic and professional settings, a consistent pattern seems to present itself. The wooden kitchen chair stands freely upon the floor, possesses a rigid frame of joined timber, and supports a single person in the seated posture with feet upon the ground. The metal folding chair, when opened for use, is likewise free-standing and rigid, performing the same supportive function. A carved mahogany desk chair of the sort found in a study or library shares these features without exception. From such cases one might infer, with apparent justification, that a chair is a free-standing piece of furniture with a rigid frame that supports a single person seated with feet on the ground.

Yet we should hesitate to treat this induction as definitive. Suppose we consider the hanging egg chair — a large, enclosed seat suspended from the ceiling by a chain or bracket. Such a chair supports a single person in a seated posture, and the occupant's feet may well rest upon the ground. However, the chair is not free-standing; it depends entirely upon the ceiling for its support, and if the chain were cut it would fall. Furthermore, its shell is typically made of woven wicker or rattan that flexes considerably under the occupant's weight, so it lacks a rigid frame in any straightforward sense. Both the free-standing condition and the rigidity condition are unsatisfied. Yet this object is manufactured, marketed, and used as a chair; customers purchase it under that description, interior decorators recommend it as such, and no one encountering it in a room would hesitate to call it a chair. Our induction from conventional specimens has thus proven too restrictive, mistaking features that happen to characterise the most common chairs for features that are necessary to being a chair at all.

definition: An object is a tool if and only if it was manufactured for a specific practical purpose, it is portable enough to be carried by a single person, and it requires skill to use effectively.
conditions: The object was manufactured for a specific practical purpose
It is portable enough to be carried by a single person
It requires skill to use effectively
background_cases: A wood chisel was manufactured for the specific purpose of shaping wood, is portable enough for a single person to carry, and requires skill to use effectively without damaging the workpiece.
A surgeon's scalpel was manufactured for the specific purpose of making precise incisions, is easily carried by one person, and requires considerable skill to wield.
A mason's trowel was manufactured for spreading mortar, is carried by a single person, and requires skill to apply mortar evenly and efficiently.
counterexample: Suppose that a person walking along a beach picks up a flat stone and uses it to crack open a shellfish. The stone was not manufactured at all; it is a natural object. It satisfies none of the three conditions: it was not manufactured for any purpose, its portability is incidental rather than a design feature, and cracking a shellfish with a stone requires no particular skill. Yet the person is plainly using it as a tool, and we would describe the stone as a tool in that context without hesitation.
abductive_insight: The background cases, all involving manufactured implements requiring trained skill, encourage the generalisation that tools must be manufactured, portable by design, and skill-dependent. But the case of the beach stone reveals the limits of this induction: an unmanufactured natural object, used opportunistically and without special skill, can function as a tool. The concept of a tool is grounded in use and purpose-in-context rather than in the properties inferred from paradigm manufactured implements.
passage:
When one reflects upon the most familiar examples of tools, a clear pattern seems to emerge. The wood chisel is manufactured for the specific purpose of shaping timber, is readily carried by a single craftsman, and demands considerable skill if it is to be used without splitting or gouging the workpiece. The surgeon's scalpel is manufactured for the precise purpose of making controlled incisions, is easily portable, and requires years of training to wield with competence. The mason's trowel is manufactured for spreading mortar, fits comfortably in one hand, and requires practiced skill to apply mortar evenly. From such examples one might reasonably infer that a tool is an object manufactured for a specific practical purpose, portable enough for a single person to carry, and requiring skill for effective use.

Yet we should hesitate to accept this induction as capturing the full extent of the concept. Suppose that a person walking along a beach picks up a flat stone from the shore and uses it to crack open a shellfish for consumption. The stone was not manufactured; it is a natural object shaped by geological processes without human intervention. Its portability is a brute physical fact, not a design feature. And cracking a shellfish against a rock requires no particular skill or training; it is an act of simple opportunism. None of the three conditions derived from our background cases is satisfied. Yet the person is using the stone as a tool, and we would describe it in precisely those terms without the slightest hesitation. This case reveals that our generalisation from manufactured implements has been too restrictive: the concept of a tool is grounded in use and functional context rather than in the properties of manufacture, designed portability, and skill-dependence that happen to characterise the paradigm cases from which we reasoned.

definition: An object is a work of art if and only if it is a unique creation produced by an individual artist, it displays technical mastery of a medium, and it is intended to express an emotional or intellectual content.
conditions: The object is a unique creation produced by an individual artist
It displays technical mastery of a medium
It is intended to express an emotional or intellectual content
background_cases: A Rembrandt self-portrait is a unique creation by an individual artist, displays mastery of oil painting, and expresses profound emotional content about age and mortality.
A Rodin sculpture is a unique creation by an individual artist, displays mastery of bronze casting and modelling, and expresses powerful emotional content about the human condition.
A Chopin nocturne is a unique creation by an individual composer, displays mastery of the piano medium, and is intended to express a particular mood or emotional atmosphere.
counterexample: Consider a case in which an industrial designer creates a mass-produced ceramic vase, manufactured in thousands of identical copies by machine. No individual copy is unique; each is a replica of the same template. The designer may possess technical competence, but the mass-production process, being mechanical, does not display the kind of personal technical mastery one associates with artistic creation. Yet the vase was designed with the intention of expressing a particular aesthetic sensibility — a balance of form, colour, and proportion intended to convey serenity. Many such mass-produced objects are collected, exhibited in design museums, and discussed as works of art by critics and curators.
abductive_insight: The background cases, all drawn from the fine arts tradition of unique masterworks by named individuals, tempt one into identifying art with uniqueness, individual mastery, and expressive intention. But the case of the mass-produced design object shows that this induction reflects the biases of a particular artistic tradition rather than the boundaries of art itself. Objects that are neither unique nor the product of individual manual mastery may be recognised as art when they embody aesthetic intention and cultural significance.
passage:
The most celebrated examples of art in the Western tradition tend to share a cluster of salient features. A Rembrandt self-portrait is the unique creation of an individual master, displaying extraordinary command of the oil medium and expressing with penetrating force the emotional realities of ageing and self-scrutiny. A Rodin sculpture is similarly unique, exhibiting mastery of bronze and clay and conveying powerful content about the human condition. A Chopin nocturne is the singular work of a named composer, demonstrating consummate mastery of the piano and intended to evoke a distinctive emotional atmosphere. From such paradigm cases one might infer that a work of art is a unique creation by an individual artist, displaying technical mastery of a medium and intended to express emotional or intellectual content.

Yet we should hesitate to generalise so freely. Consider an industrial designer who creates the template for a ceramic vase subsequently manufactured in thousands of identical copies by machine. No single copy is unique; each is a mechanical replica indistinguishable from the others. The manufacturing process does not display the personal technical mastery of hand and eye that one associates with artistic creation; it is the product of calibrated machinery. Yet the designer intended the vase to express a particular aesthetic sensibility — a harmony of form, colour, and proportion meant to convey a quality of serenity and balance. These vases are collected by discerning buyers, exhibited in design museums, and discussed as works of art by critics and curators. The conditions of uniqueness and individual mastery are both unsatisfied, yet the objects are recognised as art. This reveals the limitations of our induction: the background cases, drawn from a particular tradition of fine art, have encouraged us to mistake features characteristic of that tradition for features necessary to art as such.

definition: A statement is a lie if and only if it is a verbal assertion, the speaker knows its content to be false, and the speaker expects the hearer to believe it.
conditions: The statement is a verbal assertion
The speaker knows its content to be false
The speaker expects the hearer to believe it
background_cases: A merchant tells a customer that a garment is made of pure silk, knowing it is synthetic, and expects the customer to believe him.
A child tells a parent that she did not eat the biscuits, knowing she did, and expects the parent to believe the denial.
A witness tells a court that he was at home on the evening in question, knowing he was elsewhere, and expects the court to accept his testimony.
counterexample: Suppose that a person writes a letter to a friend, stating that she has been promoted at work, knowing this to be false, and expecting the friend to believe it. The letter is a written assertion, not a verbal one. The first condition is unsatisfied. Yet we would not hesitate to call this a lie; the medium of communication — written rather than spoken — does not alter the fundamental character of the deception. A false assertion knowingly made in writing with the expectation of being believed is as much a lie as one delivered in speech.
abductive_insight: The background cases, all involving spoken assertions, encourage the generalisation that lies must be verbal. But the written-lie case reveals this to be an artefact of the sample: lying is a function of assertoric content, knowledge of falsity, and the expectation of belief, not of the particular medium through which the assertion is conveyed. Induction from spoken examples alone yields an unduly narrow conception that excludes written, gestural, and other non-verbal forms of deceptive assertion.
passage:
The cases that first come to mind when one considers lying tend to involve spoken language. A merchant tells his customer that a garment is pure silk, knowing it to be synthetic, and expects the customer to believe this verbal assertion. A child tells her parent that she did not eat the biscuits, knowing full well that she did, and expects the parent to accept the spoken denial. A witness tells a court that he was at home on the relevant evening, knowing himself to have been elsewhere, and expects the tribunal to credit his spoken testimony. From such examples one might plausibly conclude that a lie is a verbal assertion whose content the speaker knows to be false and which the speaker expects the hearer to believe.

Yet we should hesitate to treat the verbal character of these assertions as a necessary feature of lying. Suppose that a person composes a letter to a friend, writing that she has received a promotion at work, knowing this to be false, and fully expecting the friend to believe the written claim. The assertion is conveyed in writing rather than in speech; the first condition of our inductively derived definition is therefore unsatisfied. Yet it seems plain that the letter contains a lie. The shift from spoken to written medium does not alter the essential character of the act: a false assertion has been knowingly made with the expectation that it will be believed, and this is what constitutes the lie. Our induction from cases involving spoken language has led us to include the verbal medium as a necessary condition, when in truth it is merely an incidental feature of the examples from which we reasoned. The concept of lying attaches to assertoric content and intent, not to the particular channel of communication through which the assertion travels.
